title: Comment from Ingram, Constance
comment:
I strongly urge the CDC to take decisive action in recommending meningitis vaccinations as a crucial public health measure. Meningitis is a devastating disease that can escalate quickly, often presenting like the flu and leaving little time for intervention. The staggering facts surrounding this disease, including the potential for brain damage, limb loss, and even death, cannot be overstated.

I have firsthand knowledge of the importance of meningitis awareness and vaccination as we nearly lost our son to meningococcal meningitis B as an otherwise healthy college athlete at the top of his class. I cannot begin to describe the terrifying and rapid onset of this horrific disease, leading to severe septic shock, with the infection disease doctor telling us, “We’re just trying to keep him alive right now.”

With a noted decline in vaccination rates and a concerning increase in meningococcal disease cases, it is imperative that the CDC prioritizes meningitis vaccination, particularly for those most at risk—teens and young adults aged 16-21. The data highlights that MenB accounts for more than half of meningococcal cases in those aged 17 to 23, and yet many remain unvaccinated due to misinformation and reduced perceptions of vaccine importance.

Additionally, recent health advisories regarding antibiotic resistance further emphasize the need for preventive measures. As treatment options dwindle, ensuring equitable access to all available meningococcal vaccines is crucial to protecting our communities.

Please prioritize this issue and provide clear, actionable recommendations for meningitis vaccination. Together, we can work towards preventing this serious and often overlooked disease.

Thank you for your attention to this critical matter.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Please vote yes to allow additional Covid-19 vaccine 'boosters'. (Use of additional doses of COVID-19 vaccine in immunocompromised individuals and older adults following an initial dose of 2024–2025 vaccine.)

It was horrible this past August and September to wait for the current vaccines to come out as a family member had been very recently diagnosed with Stage 4 cancer. Suddenly, our family had a very vulnerable person in it and we couldn't protect them from Covid as it surged through its late summer wave. It was highly concerning to know that if they caught Covid, then there would be weeks of waiting to get them back on track for cancer treatment. Timing really is important -- the weeks to month(s) to get over a Covid infection could be (still could for us) the difference between having a surgery to remove the mass or having enough time pass that the cancer got into other places and surgery was no longer an option. That is absolutely the difference between survival and possibly death. Stop treating these Covid vaccines as if they aren't incredibly important. At the hospital, where almost no one is masking, including the health care professionals, my family member is constantly at risk of picking up an airborne infection like Covid. Especially Covid because of how infectious it is and because of how prevalent it is.

I would also urge the committee to allow for the family members of vulnerable persons to also get another dose of the vaccine. As one of the family members responsible for caring for our person with cancer, if I catch Covid, then I certainly will not be able to care for our loved family member. In addition to being contagious and not wanting to be around my vulnerable family member--anywhere from 5 to 17 days on average--to protect them, I'm not going to feel well, and so can't perform all the duties that I'd need to perform. It's important to protect the people who interact with vulnerable people so that we can additionally protect them.

Covid is a vascular disease. The acute infection can cause health issues and delay care for cancer, but Covid is also working behind the scenes to create other damage to the body. We need to take this disease more seriously.

Now, we wait to see if our family member catches Covid in the hospital during their stay there for surgery and recovery. And hope there is recovery if they are so unlucky to catch Covid from an unmasked health care provider. Please make the vaccines more available so that more people can be protected.

title: Comment from Vaughan, Patrick
comment:
Hi, my name is Patrick Vaughan, I am an engineer and scientist, and I possess no conflicts of interest.
What we need to keep:
1.Everyone needs to be able to access these vaccines, they need to be universally available for 6 months and up.
2.Immunocompromised individuals need to continue to be allowed a covid vaccine every 2 months.
3.We need to keep the 65+ additional dose vaccine recommendations.

I have 6 points I feel we need to add in addition to these requirements.
What we need to add to this:
1.We need to update the covid vaccine frequency to every 3-4 months.

On average the active immunity nearly completely wanes within 3-4 months after receiving it, leaving you basically unprotected after the time that the active immunity has worn off. You’re just left with memory immunity at that point. The natural solution to this is to get a covid vaccine every 3-4 months. We know there is no harm in doing this due to the German man that was vaccinated 217 times.

2.We need to update the covid vaccine formula every 6 months as opposed to once every year.
Due to the unchecked spread of covid, we are allowing it to mutate and evolve exponentially faster, meaning that prior immunity is waning at ever faster rates as the virus moves sufficiently far away to reinfect individuals. As covid is not seasonal, having a summer and winter wave at minimum, we need to utilize the speed of mRNA technology to update the vaccines at a faster rate to keep covid in check. This may even be feasible with Novavax protein-based technology, while it will definitely be more difficult for them, it is possible within a 6-month timeframe.
3.On a every 6-month updated vaccine schedule, the covid vaccine frequency should be set to every 3 months.

This will allow individuals the ability to receive an updated vaccine and a booster for each vaccine update at the 6-month intervals.

4.Allow all immunocompromised individuals to receive multiple vaccine boosters a year, as opposed to limiting it to moderately or severely immunocompromised individuals.
Any immune compromising condition should be allowed to get a vaccine every 2 months with doctor approval, as even if you’re “mildly” immunocompromised, you’re still immunocompromised.
5.Individuals with low BMI (underweight, under 18.5) and High BMI (overweight and obese, greater than or equal to 30), even if immune competent, should be allowed multiple vaccine doses a year.
We know that if you have low or high BMI, that your vaccine effectiveness is going to wane much faster than if you have normal BMI. These are known people that have shortened vaccine efficacy windows, meaning they have a shortened duration of protection.

6.Additional booster doses for pregnant individuals within the third trimester of pregnancy.

As newly born infants can’t get vaccinated until 6 months, maternal covid vaccination is their only protection against severe disease through the passage of covid vaccine antibodies in utero.

title: Comment from Buher, Lori
comment:
October 10, 2024

Dear Members of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP): 

My son, Carl Buher, is a survivor of meningococcal meningitis group B. He contracted the disease at age 14 but had he been recommended to receive a meningococcal vaccination against all strains (MENACWY and B) at the 11-12 year old school health requirement visit, this may not have been the case.  

As a mother and a former Secretary of the National Meningitis Association (NMA), I am submitting this public comment to express my dismay and alarm over the committee’s deliberations regarding the potential elimination of the recommendation for administration of MenACWY vaccine at 11-12 years old, when in fact I would urge that it be strengthened to include vaccination against the B strain as well. 

Meningococcal meningitis - regardless of the strain – is a terrible disease that most healthcare providers have had little experience with thanks to the success of the 11–12 year old recommendation and the availability of life-saving vaccines.  

My son, Carl, was a freshman in high school when he came home from football practice complaining that he didn’t feel well. The next day, he developed flu-like symptoms, including sleepiness, disorientation and nausea. By afternoon, his sister noticed purple splotches all over his face and arms. He was rushed to the doctor, who by chance had recently attended a medical conference and learned about meningococcal meningitis. As a result, the doctor was able to rapidly diagnose Carl with meningcoccemia and he was rushed to the local hospital.

From there, he was immediately airlifted to Seattle Children’s Hospital. His heart stopped twice on the way and a third time at the hospital. He was put into a drug-induced coma that lasted for three weeks. As a result of the infection, he had to undergo amputation of both of his legs below the knees and three of his fingers. The skin on Carl’s legs and arms also became gangrenous requiring nearly a dozen painful skin grafts with skin harvested multiple times from his torso. It was four years before he was healed enough to walk with prosthetics. .

Just weeks ago, on April 20th, 2024, Carl received a kidney from a college friend, after several years of medications and nearly a year of nightly peritoneal dialysis. How can I impress upon professionals who have never witnessed the ravages of this disease, just how significantly a victim’s life is impacted? Carl is living what many would describe as an enviable life. A stimulating career, a supportive partner in his wife of 12 years, Anna, and two perfect young children. Yet, daily he faces challenges that most of us could not overcome. Carl’s long-term struggles are not unique among those impacted by the disease. Through our work with NMA, we met many other survivors of meningococcal meningitis who also live with serious disabilities and health issues. Sadly, we also met many parents whose families were forever changed when they lost their children to this disease.

I implore this committee to consider the importance of the 11–12 year old visit as a key opportunity to ensure continued coverage against all strains of this disease. No parent should have to lose a child and no adolescent should have to face life-long disabilities or succumb to meningococcal meningitis.  

In 2022, NMA ceased active operations after 20 years in large part to the current success of the meningococcal vaccination schedule. Meningococcal meningitis is rare. Let’s keep it that way and protect our young people. It is my sincere hope that I will be the last generation of moms to have to deal with this.  
 
Sincerely, 
Lori Buher  
Former Secretary, National Meningitis Association (inactive)

title: Comment from T, Lex
comment:
Hi, I'm an immunocompromised member of the general public who has dealt with long-COVID and continues to deal with issues it has left behind.

I'm commenting on the COVID-19 vaccine requirements for this meeting, specifically asking that these provisions be kept:

1) The additional dose vaccine recommendation for those over 65.

title: Comment from Harju, Alli
comment:
Hello, my name is Alli, I am a Doctor of Veterinary Medicine, and I possess no conflicts of interest.



I want us to ensure that covid vaccines are equitable and universally accessible to everyone, ages 6 months and older. I believe immunocompromised individuals should continue to be allowed to receive covid vaccines every 2 months, and I want those 65 year of age and older to continue to be allowed additional doses of the covid vaccine every year.



I also believe that immune competent “healthy” people should be allowed to get a covid vaccine every 3 to 4 months, and I believe we need to expedite the update covid vaccine formulas from once every year, to twice every year. As it stands, we are not vaccinating enough of the population to the point where we have strong immunity against covid, and the vaccines are being developed too slowly to the point where they are currently being outpaced by newly emerging covid variants.



As a mildly immunocompromised individual, I believe I should be allowed to receive more than one covid vaccine a year. Arbitrarily splitting immunocompromising conditions into severe/moderate/mild categories makes no sense. An immunocompromising condition is an immunocompromising condition, and I believe I deserve more than 1 covid vaccine a year.



I also want additional covid vaccines for higher risk groups that are not currently recognized or are not specifically called out by this group as requiring more than 1 vaccine a year. These groups include pregnant people, underweight people, and overweight/obese people. First, we need to allow mothers to be vaccinated multiple times a year, particularly if they are in their third trimester, as no covid vaccine is available for those under 6 months of age, and that’s the only way to protect this vulnerable group – by vaccinating the mother close to birth. Young infants suffer major complications and death under 6 months of age from a covid infection, and that is simply unacceptable. We also know that high and low BMI people have accelerated waning immunities following vaccination. These groups certainly need more frequent covid vaccinations, as does everyone.



Do better. Adopt all of these as updated guidance.

comment: I am writing to speak to the COVID vaccine recommendations. We continue to have wave after wave of COVID and the subsequent disabilities it can cause. The antibodies from the vaccine that can prevent infection wane after only 3 months! While there remains T-cell immunity, this does not prevent infection as well as antibodies do, so for 9 months out of the year members of our extended family have much more risk killing my parents and disabling my son with a COVID infection should anyone catch it. Families like ours feel abandoned by the misguided guidance of the CDC on COVID vaccines and unchecked COVID spread in the US. We often wonder if you are trying to kill us even though this is probably not the case since as professionals, one would hope you still have empathy for your fellow human beings. title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
I am an anonymous immunocompromised citizen in Illinois, I write to you concerned about a number of issues related to covid.

I appreciate and want to encourage the continuation of universal vaccine availability for everyone 6 months and older, immunocompromised folks like myself being allowed a covid vaccine every 2 months, and continuing the covid recommendations for elders (65+). Let's keep what's working working.

However, I am especially concerned about vaccine availability being universal, and connected to a person's actual health needs. Vaccine efficacy studies show that most people need availability of a vaccine booster every 3-4 months, and for immunocompromised folks like myself, every 2 months, in accordance with the waning protections after each dose. Memory immunity is not enough, and we've seen repeat vaccinations are not harmful.

Also, because of the rapid mutation of covid, a yearly vaccine update is not keeping up with the strains most prevalent when vaccines are administered. We need new vaccines to be formulated every 6 months. This would give most people 4 doses per year or 2 different formulations, a much better community protection.

I'm also concerned about "mildly immunocompromised" people in my community not being able to receive a booster every 2 months, any preexisting health vulnerability (including being on the high or low end of weight calculators) is a vulnerability of developing long covid.

I also suggest pregnant people be given a vaccine during their third trimester of pregnancy, as vaccines cannot be given until 6 months old, making in-utero vaccine support is the best option for our youngest community members.

title: Comment from zervos, marcus
comment:
Detroit and nearby cities like Flint are the face of many health disparities and social inequities in this country. As a physician serving this area, I am acutely aware of how federal policies translate to individual mortality and morbidity and how they can make existing disparities better or worse.


In October, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) plans to discuss whether to lower the broad age-based recommendation for pneumococcal vaccines to age 50. CDC shared data in June that suggest lowering the age to 50 can lead to significant reduction in disparities in pneumococcal disease risk for Black Americans.


This is a critical opportunity to change the impact of pneumococcal disease, especially for Black Americans. Lowering the age-based pneumococcal vaccine recommendation for adults to age 50 is a meaningful step to reduce health disparities, address the life expectancy gap between Black individuals and their white peers, and leverage what we learned during the Covid pandemic – that is, vaccination coverage gaps can be closed when the public health community addresses the needs of Black Americans.

Marcus Zervos, MD, MACP, FIDSA
Division Head, Infectious Diseases, Henry Ford Health
Director, Center for Emerging Infectious Diseases, Henry Ford Health
Professor of Medicine, Michigan State University College of Human Medicine
Professor of Medicine, and Co-Director, Center for Emerging and Infectious Diseases,
Wayne State University School of Medicine, Detroit, MI

title: Comment from Koslap-Petraco, Mary
comment:
I am very distressed to hear that ACIP is considering changing the Meningitis schedule. We need to enable more adolescents and young adults to be protected and any updates are feasible to implement in clinical practice. There has been a troubling increase in meningitis cases since 2023 with 422 cases. The reason we do not see meningitis from the 4 serogroups in MCV4 is because most schools require this vaccine for all 11-12 year olds and then a second shot at age 16 years. Meningococcal meningitis can strike without warning and be deadly in as little as 24 hours––even with prompt medical care. We need to protect all children because each child is precious even if this disease affect a few. The current shared clinical decision-making (SCDM) recommendation for MenB vaccination leaves gaps in protection. Nearly 7 out of 10 17-year-olds have not received at least one dose of the MenB vaccine. 75% of healthcare providers surveyed believe that the removal of the recommendation to vaccinate 11-12-year-olds would have at least some negative impacts on protection against meningococcal disease for younger adolescents and I am one of those providers. 45% of healthcare providers surveyed believe there is too much
complexity surrounding meningococcal vaccination compared to other vaccine-preventable childhood diseases. I teach about vaccines to both nursing students and providers and the current recommendations are so confusing too many providers do not even discuss meningitis b vaccine. Please ensure that all children are protected against all 5 serotypes with a simple easy to follow schedule.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
I will tell you a secret...well, a not-so-secret secret. People aren't well.

I went to the grocery store this past week and several people were coughing. One gentleman I saw quite a few times as we traveled the aisles, and every few feet, he coughed and coughed again. Another woman was trying to wear a surgical mask, but she kept pulling it down because she was so breathless, and she shook and shook an inhaler, using it to try to help, but it never looked like it did help. Another couple with a baby, standing in line to pay, and the baby had a wet, snotty cough that sounded truly terrible and unrelenting.

My co-workers aren't well. They return from vacation for a day or two, then stay home sick for a week with Covid. When they come back, their little ills compound. A cut that won't heal. A weird growth. Trouble sleeping. Trouble breathing. Periods of being too warm and too cold in a person that never had that issue before. One woman with bad circulation to the point where her skin is starting to take on a blue tint. Newly celiac disease. A broken ankle that healed slowly. Tinnitus. So much tinnitus.

At a small birthday party with family, the teenager whose birthday it was had a wet, lingering cough. He'd had a dry reoccurring cough all last year, but a new infection with 'something' had turned it into something worse. The mom had had a bout of pneumonia, twice, that winter. It had taken a couple months before she could take care of household chores again.

If we're going to have a vaccine only strategy to protect us from Sars-Cov-2, which we know causes terrible systemic damage like cardiovascular issues including heart attacks and strokes and a rise in the risk for diabetes, then people need access to the Covid vaccines at least twice a year.

So, please, yes, approve Covid vaccines for at least the immunocompromised for every 6 months. But maybe also think about everyone else. Because while I can't speak to the strangers in the grocery store, the other people I wrote about didn't have such a panoply of ailments until they started collecting Covid infections.

title: Comment from Buchanon, Mishal
comment:
Howdy do,I am a student of environmental science, and I possess no conflicts of interest.

Regulations we need to keep:
1. Everyone needs to be able to access these vaccines for free; they need to be universally available and recommended for anyone 6 months and older.
2. Immunocompromised people need to continue to be eligible and allowed a Covid vaccine/booster every 2 or fewer months.
3. The 65+ additional dose/booster vaccine recommendations need to remain.

I have many points to add to these requirements, enumerated thusly:
1. We need to update the Covid vaccine frequency to every 3-4 months, or every season, due to the average active immunity almost completely waning within 3-4 months after receiving a vaccine, leaving people effectively unprotected after that amount of time, especially due to the frequency of mutation of the Covid virus. The common-sense solution to this is to either get a Covid vaccine every 3-4 months, and/or develop and implement a pan-coronavirus vaccine that protects people from getting infected by any type of Covid, including other coronaviruses.

2. We need to update the Covid vaccine formula every 3-4 months as opposed to once per year; due to the unmitigated spread of Covid, including conditional availability and ineffectual promotion of vaccines and other known mitigation strategies like masking and air filtration, Covid is mutating and evolving exponentially faster, meaning that prior immunity is waning at ever-faster rates as the virus moves away from what vaccines have prepared immune systems to confront. As covid is year-round, affecting people during every season, we need to utilize all available vaccine technologies to develop seasonal vaccines, and put more funding and research into a pan-coronavirus vaccine to avoid the fatigue of having to get updated vaccinations more frequently than any other virus in history.

3. On an "updated every 6-months" vaccine schedule, the Covid vaccine frequency should be set to every 3 months, but a pan-coronavirus vaccine that entirely prevents infection would be even better. Setting vaccine frequency to every 3-4 months will allow people the ability to receive an updated vaccine and a booster for each vaccine update until a fully preventative pan-coronavirus vaccine can be formulated and implemented.

4. Allow all immunocompromised individuals to receive multiple vaccine boosters per year, as opposed to limiting boosters to moderately or severely immunocompromised people. Any immune-compromising condition should allow someone to get a vaccine or booster every 2 months, as even if someone is “mildly” immunocompromised, any condition that puts someone at a higher risk of a moderate or severe Covid infection is effectively immunocompromised.

5. People with low BMI (underweight, under 18.5) and high BMI (overweight and obese, greater than or equal to 30), even if they are immune-competent, should be allowed multiple vaccine doses/boosters per year. We know that low or high BMI impedes vaccine efficacy, causing immunity to wane much faster than those with a normal BMI.

6. Additional booster doses for pregnant persons within the third trimester of pregnancy. Newborn infants can’t get vaccinated until they are 6 months old, so Covid vaccination through passage of antibodies in utero is their only protection against severe disease until they are eligible for their own vaccination.

title: Comment from Christy, Lauren
comment: Please keep Covid vaccines and boosters available to all. We should have updated vaccines to keep up with evolution of the virus, and we should make boosters available to people who are immune-compromised, older than 65, or have other conditions (low/high BMI, autoimmune diseases, etc) that could mean a shorter duration in vaccine protection.

One time it was because the office wanted the space to put up a seasonal decoration (a Christmas Tree), and then after Christmas was over, the HEPA filter didn't return. A cute little table with tea and coffee things appeared in its place. So, the overall swing was away from cleaner air and toward encouraging patients to enjoy a beverage (take off your mask, drink something).

So it becomes increasingly important that for the people who are aware of how Sars-CoV-2 is more dangerous than a common cold and more dangerous than influenza that vaccines are available at least twice a year.

Because at the dentist, where I have to unmask to get the regular dental care I need to make sure my teeth and gums remain healthy (and my heart, since dental care is linked to better heart health), I need to make sure that my immune system has had a recent boost against Covid so that I can be more protected against infection, transmission, and deleterious effects from both an acute infection and chronic after-effects of possible infection.

We know that the vaccine protection wanes. Every ACIP meeting for years now has had that data presented.

So it is important to make sure that immunocompromised people have access to the vaccine every 6 months, but also that immunocompetent people also have that protection available to them.

title: Comment from Park, Daniel
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

References:
1. Link-Gelles R. Effectiveness of COVID-19 (2023-2024 Formula) vaccines. Presented at: FDA VRBPAC Meeting; June 5, 2024. Accessed June 12, 2024. https://www.fda.gov/media/179140/download

2. Wu N, Joyal-Desmarais K, Vieira AM, et al. COVID-19 boosters versus primary series: update to a living review. The Lancet Respiratory Medicine. 2023;11(10):e87-e88. doi:10.1016/S2213-2600(23)00265-5

3. Menegale F, Manica M, Zardini A, et al. Evaluation of Waning of SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine–Induced Immunity: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. JAMA Netw Open. 2023;6(5):e2310650. doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2023.10650

comment: It's incredibly important for more updated and regular access to vaccines. There's so many in my community who have been unable to be in public because the lack of access.  By having more regular updated vaccines, that would be necessary to contribute towards ensuring that people who have been abandoned by the lack of support during this ongoing pandemic can remain safe.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
In regard to Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001:

I'm writing to urge decision makers to recommend that COVID vaccination happens at least twice a year (at least every six months) for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. comment: As a physician, with waning immunity and new variants regularly, it seems clear that we need universal access to vaccines every 6 months. Each time we get COVID there is a risk of long COVID, in addition to other health issues, attenuated but not entirely prevented by vaccination. We also need increased access to antivirals and longer courses than currently available, including for those under age 12 who may still develop long term problems after COVID, such as fatigue or autoimmune issues. Until there are improvements in either vaccines or treatments, more frequent vaccinations sh5be available to everyone.

title: Comment from Lorentz, Carmen
comment:
I hope the Committee will consider allowing twice annual COVID vaccination for all age groups. The protection from vaccination wanes over a few months, yet we experience three or four waves of COVID infection every year. Allowing twice annual vaccination for all age groups would enable those of us who want to be better protected to do so. Given the relatively low rate of vaccination in the general population, there does not appear to be risk of vaccine shortages.

I also hope the Committee will push vaccines to be updated more than just once per year in order to adjust for new COVID variants.

Thank you.

title: Comment from Mulvaney, Melissa M
comment: I’m a person with moderate asthma and SAHM of a teenager. Having access to vaccines for the general public year round is critical not only to being able to ensure that my spouse and child who are not high risk can be vaccinated and build herd immunity to better protect me. Our schedules do not always allow for us to get them their shots in the fall, and we know anyway that COVID has a pattern it showing up in the summer as well as the winter. Please, let’s continue to offer the maximum amount of vaccines to the maximum of people who want it.

title: Comment from Solazzo, Christine
comment: I am writing to speak to the COVID vaccine recommendations. We continue to have wave after wave of COVID and the subsequent disabilities it can cause. I have a husband who already has Multiple Sclerosis and who also has markers in his blood tests that he may be prone to develop an autoimmune disease. I also have a aunt  who is battling two other kinds of cancer, as well as a father with a heart condition. As such, our extended family goes above and beyond what most people do to protect ourselves from COVID infections since passing it on to my parents could result in their deaths, and passing it on to my hunger could also cause his death. Since there is not adequate mitigation of this airborne disease in most public spaces, my kid’s schools, or even in healthcare facilities, those like our family, who need to protect our vulnerable members would benefit greatly from being able to get vaccinated for COVID more than once a year like you permit those over 65 and immunocompromised individuals. The antibodies from the vaccine that can prevent infection wane after only 3 months! While there remains T-cell immunity, this does not prevent infection as well as antibodies do, so for 9 months out of the year members of our extended family have much more risk killing my parents and disabling my son with a COVID infection should anyone catch it. Families like ours feel abandoned by the misguided guidance of the CDC on COVID vaccines and unchecked COVID spread in the US. We often wonder if you are trying to kill us even though this is probably not the case since as professionals, one would hope you still have empathy for your fellow human beings. Every new case of long COVID is a policy decision at this point, as is every hospital acquired case of COVID, and even some of the deaths from COVID. Please start adjusting your policies on the timing of COVID vaccines to protect everyone better as public health should. Thank you for your time.

title: Comment from Boehle-Silva, Kali
comment: I strongly encourage the CDC to make vaccines available 2x a year to all age groups -- as someone who is still experiencing the effects of COVID 2 years later vaccines are a really important way I can still access public spaces safely.

title: Comment from Lunde, Janet
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

I'm seeing Long COVID strike my family and friends. This is a public health crisis, and the suffering and costs are enormous and building. We can fight this with more frequent, updated, vaccination.

COVID vaccination at least every 6 months must be recommended for people of all ages. Limiting eligibility creates unnecessary barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as the emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least twice a year. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] shows that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Harrop, Lucia
comment:
Dear CDC Leadership,

I am writing to express my concerns about the current COVID-19 vaccination schedule and to urge the CDC to consider allowing two COVID-19 booster shots per year to help curb the virus’s spread and better protect the most vulnerable populations. In the face of ongoing variants, it is clear that more frequent boosters are necessary to provide stronger and longer-lasting immunity.

COVID-19 continues to pose a serious threat, not just to those considered immunocompromised, but to the population as a whole. Emerging evidence suggests that even healthy individuals can suffer long-term immune system damage after infection, a condition often referred to as being "immunocompromised" by COVID-19 itself. This risk underlines the need for stronger preventive measures.

Multiple studies have demonstrated that frequent boosters can provide enhanced protection against severe illness, hospitalization, and death. The immunity from each booster tends to wane over several months, leaving even vaccinated individuals vulnerable to breakthrough infections. A more frequent vaccination schedule would bolster immunity throughout the year, especially as new variants emerge that evade prior immunity. The success of the flu vaccine model, which can be given seasonally or more frequently in certain populations, supports the case for boosting COVID-19 vaccination efforts.

The current once-a-year booster strategy is insufficient in the fight against COVID-19 until better sterilizing vaccines, which can more effectively block transmission, become widely available. By allowing two boosters annually, the CDC can help slow the virus's spread and better protect both vulnerable groups and the broader population.

In light of the ongoing global pandemic and the unpredictable nature of COVID-19, I believe that enhancing our vaccine regimen is a crucial step toward long-term public health and safety. I urge the CDC to consider this approach as part of its commitment to safeguarding the health of all Americans.

Thank you for your time and attention to this matter.

title: Comment from Steele , Katie
comment: Please follow the science and recommend covid boosters twice a year for ALL ages and health statuses. We know that immunity decreases in much less than a year, and anyone can develop severe long covid symptoms from a single infection. We all need proper support from up to date immunizations. Please also mandate that vaccines be updated to match current variants more frequently. Covid mutates quickly, and we should keep up.

title: Comment from Boyd, Danielle
comment: Please allow vaccines twice a year for all age groups and with all medical conditions. I avoided Covid for four years until this August and was sick for ten days. My parents were the only people on our family vacation that didn’t get sick. They had been able to get the spring vaccine because they are over 65. More of us could have avoided getting sick and rushing long covid if we had access to the spring vaccine. Please allow us all to get updated vaccines twice a year!

comment: I cannot imagine a good argument for not continuing at least fall covid vaccinations for everyone. Reducing the availability of those vaccines is just asking for another pandemic breakout. title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Vaccines help all people and access is a public health issue that concerns all members of our communities. Please continue to make Covid-19 vaccines available to all ages and people of all statuses.

comment: As a grandmother of an immune compromised child, I respectfully ask that the government make Covid vaccines available a minimum of twice a year. title: Comment from Musche, Susan
comment:
I am asking that vaccines be available to all people at no cost. This is a simple and low-cost method to reduce future health issues.

Susan

title: Comment from McManus, Victoria
comment: Vaccines need to be updated and available twice a year, and they need to be covered for ALL PEOPLE even if they don't have insurance. Otherwise, it's spitting into the wind. Recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will let people of all ages know about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness. This is especially important given that vaccination is the only amelioration our government seems interested in emphasizing - ventilation and masks have fallen by the wayside in most places despite many more of us being at risk from previous infections and the resultant immune system and organ damage. COVID is relentless and we are never going to outpace variants, but boosting twice a year could make a good dent and give us more time to hopefully finalize some vaccines that cover more ground and help prevent transmission. It would also be great to have more news coverage and advertising about the dangers of COVID and its sequelae. The CDC needs to step up and fight disease.

comment: I am in favor of additional covid boosters (i.e. Use of additional doses of COVID-19 vaccine in immunocompromised individuals and older adults following an initial dose of 2024–2025 vaccine). Especially something that could be administered in the spring ahead of potential summer waves. We've been seeing pretty regularly that covid cases surge in both the late fall/early winter and the late spring/early summer. Given this pattern and how quickly immunity granted by shots wears off, it makes a lot of sense to let people get boosters twice a year.

title: Comment from Kirby, Susannah
comment: Covid vaccine immunity wanes after 4 months, and variants are changing more quickly than we can keep up. An annual vaccine is NOT ENOUGH! Everyone (all ages, all health statuses) should have access to covid vaccines AT LEAST twice a year. We also need vaccines that are targeted to the current circulating variants, not the ones that were around a year (or more) ago.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: See attached file(s)

title: Comment from Steiner, Donna
comment: Please make Covid vaccines/boosters available to anyone who needs them twice a year. Despite the expense, updated vaccines are needed for those with compromised immune systems, those were over 65, and other individuals. The government is not doing all it can to ensure the health of all citizens. We should not have to beg to protect our health. Thank you.

title: Comment from Solovyev , Anna
comment:
COVID vaccines loose effectiveness after 6 months. This means that people should have access to vaccines twice a year rather than the current once a year.

My family has to decide what part of the year is the most important time for effective protection and get the vaccine then because we do not have the option to get a booster once immunity fades. This has meant that last year we chose to get boosters in the fall to prioritize winter holidays and everyone ended up with a new round of Covid in May once immunity waned. Thankfully, my family foes not have immunocompromised people so we hopt our "second booster" by getting sick. This should not be the case as each bout of Covid increases the risk of complications. It also lead us to run out of paid sick leave and use unpaid days and this is best case scenario. If our family had immunocompromised individuals or someone with with other risk factors for getting very ill with Covid what should we have done?

Some people will not want to do 2 boosters a year. That should be their choice. I want to have a choice to get vaccinated for the time period that the vaccine is protective and not pretend that Covid vaccinations work like flu vaccines just because this is convenient. It is not convenient for me and my family to get Covid once year because the current vaccination schedule only protects us half of the 12 month period.

title: Comment from McMahon, Jean
comment:
Dear Members of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP):

I am deeply concerned by the continued spread of COVID-19 and the long-term harm each recurring infection can cause (based on now hundreds of studies). Many Americans are suffering from Long Covid or newly acquired chronic disabilities such as POTS, and many more will become disabled unless further mitigations are taken. Until such time as we have FDA-approved COVID-19 vaccines that actually prevent transmission and can keep up with new variants, I implore you to:

1) Recommend COVID vaccines for all ages and health statuses at least twice a year (spring vaccine access for all), AND

2) Support more frequent updates to the vaccines, adjusted for the latest variants.

Thank you,
J. McMahon
Public Health Analyst

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: We have come to know that COVID season is not a once-a-year phenomenon like the flu. In fact, COVID peaks multiple times throughout the year. As such, I ask you on behalf of myself, my family, and my community, to make sure updated COVID vaccines are available multiple times a year for all demographics. The harmful effects of COVID are felt by people of all ages, including children, teenagers, and young adults. Even so-called "healthy" individuals can suffer from long-term effects known as Long COVID. In fact, even as a young, active woman, I am considered at risk for Long COVID due solely to my gender. Therefore, all of us in the US - all ages, all health levels, all races and ethnicities and genders - need access to multiple COVID vaccines each year to keep us safe from the harms of this virus. We are still in a pandemic - we need these protections.

title: Comment from McCarty, Amy
comment:
I plan travel, visits to ill family members, and my attendance of large gathering events based on current COVID levels and my level of immunity based on my last COVID vaccination. Vaccine induced immunity wanes after a few months; thus, once a year vaccination restricts my ability to fully live my life. I do not travel, attend the theatre, or visit my in-laws during certain times of the year. One way to help alleviate this would be to allow COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) for people of all ages, regardless of health status. People of all ages and health statuses should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025. Vaccines are important for "healthy", young people as this contributes to herd immunity which in turn helps the elderly and immunocompromised population. Additionally, long COVID can impact everyone so there is continued reason to avoid getting COVID and thus prioritize immunity which helps decrease the level of circulating virus.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Denk, Tom
comment: My wife and I have never missed a COVID vaccination (and have also never had COVID), we're big believers in the benefits and would love to have access to update our vaccine twice per year.

title: Comment from C, Nora
comment:
COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The COVID vaccines wane with time and the evolution of variants and once per year is not enough to keep pace. I have been able to get vaccinated with every new formulation of the Pfizer COVID vaccine, but it has not prevented COVID infection. Every time I've been infected, it's been at least 6 months after my last vaccine. It loses efficacy with time, but a 2x/year vaccine would help mitigate these infections. We won't know the full toll that COVID has taken on the world for many more years, but given what we do know, it is scientifically sound to recommend vaccines twice per year, especially to the most vulnerable populations. The cost of caring for infections and their effect is much greater than the cost of developing and offering vaccines twice a year. This is a common sense solution to a serious public health concern.

Thank you for your time,
Nora

title: Comment from Skinner, Shannon
comment: In late spring of 2024, I sought a COVID vaccine booster in preparation for summer travel through multiple airports. I was turned away by three pharmacies in my area because I did not meet the age requirement. I was 58 at the time. I had last received a COVID vaccine in the fall of 2023 and was told I must wait a year to receive my next booster. Frustrated, I proceeded with my travel across the country to attend a memorial service for my mother. Incidentally, both my mother and father died of COVID complications, and my sister-in-law had to leave her job as a teacher due to Long COVID. My trip involved visiting four airports both going and returning (Bellingham, Seattle, Austin, Sarasota). As my bad luck would have it, summer 2024 turned out to break records for US air travel. I believe it, as each airport seemed to be operating well past its legal fire department occupancy designations - dangerous beyond the risk of COVID. Virtually no one besides me and my husband were wearing masks. We managed to avoid getting COVID against our odds. My entire family traveled from various points across the country. On their return, my brother-in-law caught COVID. My younger sister later contracted it from friends as she picked them up from the airport. I got the latest COVID booster on September 5, 2024, the day after it became available at my neighborhood pharmacy. To date, only 6.1% of Washingtonians have received the 2024-2025 booster. Stats for my state show low booster uptake and consistently low demand outside of the winter season (see uploaded chart). I would bet big money that a significant amount of 2023-2024 boosters were thrown away. My husband and I would have felt better protected had we been given access last spring to a booster prior to our high-risk travel. I am a voracious reader of news, especially news about healthcare in general and COVID in particular. I have not read one recent article about vaccine shortages or excess demand. Why not let those of us under 65 who want to be vigilant against this deadly and disabling virus receive booster vaccines more than once per year? They should be free to all who want them. I would have paid for one last spring but was denied at any price due to age. Please provide access to COVID boosters at least twice per year for all who are serious about protecting themselves from a deadly and disabling virus.

title: Comment from Sajedi, Cameron
comment: The largest economic shock to my and other small businesses is the unreliablity of staff due to covid infections and the health reverberations that are ongoing after an infection. Vaccines need to be updated to the latest circulating variant. We land people on the Moon but the best we can do is 6 months old variants to sortof help with the fall? We need more often updated, more reliable vaccines and a drive to more access for the under insured and the children who dont always get approval. We need these things for very grounded reasons.

title: Comment from Durmaz, Evelyn
comment:
Re: “Use of additional doses of COVID-19 vaccine in immunocompromised individuals and older adults following an initial dose of 2024–2025 vaccine”

I support updating Covid 19 vaccines frequently and making them available free of charge twice a year for everyone.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
I am immunocompromised due to two medications to treat Ankylosing Spondylitus. I am able to obtain multiple COVID immunizations a year to keep my immunity up, but my spouse, who we rely on financially, cannot despite knowing that the protection wanes.
We need access to additional COVID immunizations for him to keep our family healthy, financially viable, and safe.

title: Comment from Krahn, Laurel
comment:
Hi,

My husband and I, like the majority of residents of the United States, are at high risk of bad outcomes should we get SARS-CoV-2. (We're each vulnerable for multiple reasons but none of them put us in the severely immunocompromised category.)

The current vaccines for SARS-CoV-2 are great, but could be better. Given how fast immunity wanes, we feel most people should be getting updated SARS-CoV-2 vaccines at least twice a year. In the fall and the spring, perhaps. If more people did this, maybe we'd see fewer waves of infection or at least the peaks wouldn't be as high.

Ideally everyone would be encouraged to get vaccinated against SARS-CoV-2 twice a year and these vaccines would be updated for latest variants. Uptake on vaccines even once a year has not been what it could be, but I know many people who are always eager to get them who would happily get them twice (or three times) a year if it was allowed and made easy to do (and affordable). We just got the updated vaccine (along with flu shots) this October; we were fortunate enough to get doses back in April but not all of our friends who wanted to managed it.

Why not let anyone who wants to get the vaccine twice a year? There haven't been shortages of the vaccine and I know many people who are desperate to get them 2-3 times a year if only it was allowed and paid for.

It has felt like the U.S. went with a vaccine focused approach to protecting people from SARS-CoV-2 in recent years; I feel more needs to be done, but if vaccines are to be the focus, there should be more of a push to get people to get them. And to have the best vaccines we can available to all, with timing that best works to protect people.

Thank you for your time.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Please make COVID vaccines, including Novavax, available to all ages 2x/yr to help mitigate the problem of waning immunity - we had sewer readings over 300 (spiking into four digits) over the entire summer in Northern California, which is much more rampant than ever, and study after study shows that this is a collective-damage, permanent-damage causing virus. Economically, and as a society, it makes no sense to rack up sick days, more disabled people, and more colelctive brain damage. Personally, my 53yo husband/father of our four at-home kids, almost died from a pulmonary embolism in June with no history of anything but one COVID infection in October 23 - his doctors at UC Davis all concur and have written in his chart that this was caused by that infection. It is NOT a "mild" disease outside of comparision to the initial, refrigerated-corpses stage of it. There must be better protection, and until then, more access to the existing layers of mitigation, including more frequent vaccines. Thank you for your time and consideration.

Help us secure free, frequent, and updated vaccines for all.

comment: I have two older family members who died with covid infections in the last few months. We need to be able to receive covid vaccines at least twice a year.  All ages should be able to protect their health and the health of their loved ones via vaccine twice a year.

title: Comment from Feldman, Paul

comment:

title: Comment from De Armond-Reid, Erin
comment:
I urge the committee to adopt a twice annual Covid vaccine schedule. We have consistently seen waves of outbreaks across the country in most seasons. Evolving to a twice annual schedule aligns with the realities of the climate crisis that we face, and will hopefully help us mitigate the impacts of long Covid. As a public health professional, and, healthcare worker, increasing access to vaccines to mitigate these will help reduce strain on a system that is already close to collapse. In addition, we need federal resources to subsidize these vaccines to that they’re freely available to all, as well as culturally and linguistically appropriate public health promotion campaigns that continue to raise awareness on the benefits of vaccination.

Thank you.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Chan, Aaron
comment:
COVID vaccations twice a year must be recommended for people of all ages. They are a net-benefit for everyone because they reduce sick time and are better for people in the long term.
As you're well aware, vaccines have a waning efficacy in the months after the vaccine, and we've all seen the summer spikes in COVID rates.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Thank you for the opportunity to comment on this topic.  As an immunocompromised person and a retired federal attorney/judge with a scientific research background I feel uniquely qualified to provide insight on your consideration.  I lost my mother as a result of this virus so forgive me for the emotional nature of this response.  The ability of immunocompromised individuals to get close family members vaccinated more than once a year is profound. The threat posed by unvaccinated family members being infected and transmitting the virus is significantly increased by their lack of access to the vaccine.  The impact and knowledge that if only they had been able to get protected by another vaccine shot they could have avoided exposure to your family members will never be forgotten.  Additionally, there are many examples of issues effecting everyday individuals today that have been created by the limitations placed on the vaccine.  Take for instance school age children who are potentially exposed on a daily basis in schools that lack adequate ventilation and can only be vaccinated for an effective period of less than half the school year.  Parents face the problem of either homeschooling their children or risking the life of a immunocompromised father or mother.  It would be incredibly simple to just allow anyone who resides with an immunocompromised individual to receive a vaccine three times a year.  The vaccine is not in short supply, the side effects are minimal and I have seen no research that indicates any long term effects from repeated inoculation.  Please, please make this change to your guidelines, it would make an enormous difference in the quality of life for the immunocompromised community!  Thank you.

title: Comment from Woodward, Lia
comment: It has been enough years now that we know one vaccine a year is not enough, and that it is always trailing behind the variant circulating once it is available, just like our current vaccine released this fall is already behind what will circulate this winter. We know it is a fact that immunity wanes around four months, to the extent that major retailers remind us of this fact when they advertise the vaccines encouraging us to get the one shot available to the general public each year. We also know there is a major summer wave of COVID every single year. We have to start “living with the virus” and adapt to these facts with an updated vaccine for each wave each year, minimum. Stop pretending this is the flu- experts agree it is not the flu and we are all sicker because of this falsehood. Too many of my “healthy” friends have had worse health outcomes because they only got one shot a year and were infected in the summer wave. Immunocompromised people are even more vulnerable, but all health statuses need as much protection from infection as possible— long COVID affects people of all health statuses and it is ruining lives. The amount of gatekeeping at the pharmacies from region to region is unconscionable especially in light of how low uptake is. My 77 year old aunt is severely immune compromised and the pharmacist tried to talk her out of the spring vaccine she qualified for and desperately needed. These vaccines need to be accessible for the immune compromised/over 65 and for everyone because vaccine protection wanes quickly and variants far outpace the annual vaccine. Since this administration is insisting on a vaccine only approach, vaccines that are tailored to the current variants are what we need, twice a year minimum for the increasingly predictable massive waves that happen twice a year. Recommending COVID vaccines for all ages and health statuses at least twice a year (spring vaccine access for all) AND more frequent updates to the vaccines, adjusted for the latest variants is the absolute minimum needed to protect the public from this disabling virus.

title: Comment from Webster, Jennifer
comment: Given the nature of COVID-19 surges happening at least twice a year & the fact that mRNA vaccines lose effectiveness over time, it is utterly indefensible to only make the vaccine available once per year. People of all ages should have access to updated vaccines/boosters at LEAST every 6 months.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Covid vaccines must be made available to all regardless of age or health status throughout the year. I am immunocompromised but I don't live in isolation. Regardless of my own vaccines, constant masking, isolation, my friends and family continue to live their lives. Every time a loved one contracts Covid (and most of my closest relatives have all had it by now some two or three times), no matter how it seems to not impact them in the moment, it elevates my own risk for long covid complications and potential death.

Restrictions on spring eligibility to the vaccines could literally kill me. Not because I wouldn't have access, but because the person coughing on the subway didn't have access, the bus driver didn't have access, the person standing in line in front of me at the checkout, the postman, my neighbors' kids... Everyone needs access. We're all interconnected. It boggles the mind how anyone can imagine themselves isolated or separate from another living being in this day and age. Vaccines for everyone. period.

title: Comment from Warren, Shirley
comment: I am incredulous that people are no longer taking COVID seriously. I have friends and relatives who have never recovered from COVID, after months or years. It is important that you recommend COVID vaccines be offered to people of all ages and health conditions, at least twice a year.  In addition, it is important that new COVID vaccines keep up with the current variants. The spectre of Long COVID looms over us all. We don't know how to cure that yet. Thank you for your time and consideration.

title: Comment from Fredrics, Howard
comment:
As the CDC considers recommendations for frequency of COVID vaccinations and for a schedule of updates to current vaccines, I am writing as a member of the public to offer my input.

Having produced public access TV PSAs with well-known health officials in support of COVID vaccination as part of my role as Chairman of a municipal public access station, I have become familiar with many of the issues surrounding the trajectory of COVID variants and efficacy of vaccines. My readings of peer reviewed literature about the rate of mutation of vaccines, durability of vaccines and diverse needs of our population have strongly pointed to the need for COVID vaccines to be made available to all at six month intervals. Immune protection has been shown to wane significantly after 4-6 months, rendering patients vulnerable to severe disease, hospitalization and death. Moreover, Long COVID remains a risk, particularly for those who contract severe disease. Trends have also shown that the virus is mutating at a faster than annual rate to a degree that renders current vaccines much less effective at preventing severe outcomes than they are within the first 2-6 months of administration. These mutations are significant and different enough to warrant faster development of new vaccines to more closely match currently circulating and predominant strains. Moreover, vulnerable populations, including older Americans, people with chronic lung conditions, diabetes, and other similar conditions are particularly vulnerable to the impacts of diminished efficacy of vaccines.

Accordingly, I call upon the CDC to issue new guidelines that more accurately reflect the rate of mutation of COVID-19 viruses, and which also reflect the known waning of immune response within the six month time frame. This means that the CDC should issue guidelines that enable all Americans to receive vaccines every six months, and that manufacturers are encouraged and incentivized to develop new targeted vaccines on a six-month schedule, rather than on an annual schedule. Where appropriate and possible, US government research centers should be encouraged and resourced to produce their own targeted vaccines, so that we are not entirely dependent on profit motives of private companies.

title: Comment from Menchini, Peter
comment:
COVID vaccines for all ages and health statuses at least twice a year (spring vaccine access for all) AND

More frequent updates to the vaccines, adjusted for the latest variants.

title: Comment from Nyapathy, Nikhila
comment:
Hello,

As we know, COVID-19 has continued to silently but surely wreck peoples' lives in the world. Not only immunocompromised folks, but even people with no pre existing conditions. Vaccines have been a large part in mitigating the spread. While they haven't prevented transmission (robust masking and air quality measures would go a long way to help there) it is an important tool in protecting us against a virus, that has continued to mutate into a huge number of variants, making it hard to effectively protect oneself.

The science shows that vaccine protection wanes after 3-4 months. Having a booster twice a year would go a long way in reinforcing that protection.
If the access is not extended to the general public, it becomes inaccessible for one to protect themselves with a spring booster. There are significant out of pocket costs for people today which could be mitigated by extending the access.

In addition, iterating upon the vaccine more frequently is the need of the hour. We are no longer in a phase of the pandemic where there is only one variant. With the rate at which the virus is evolving, evolving the vaccine more frequently, with research and funding, is crucial.

At the end of the day, none of us want long COVID. The only defense we have today is to reduce the number of infections we get. These are important measures in working towards that. Thank you for taking the time to read this comment.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Hello,

I am an everyday citizen who lives with a disabling chronic health condition. My life since Covid has grown much smaller because my condition (which is already similar to long covid) has been shown to get much worse after covid. To help keep myself safe, I stay up to date on the wastewater data, follow blogs and email newsletters from public health researchers, and more so that I can discern when there is a spike in cases in my community (no thanks to the CDC making this data harder and harder to access, and painting the map in soothing blue colors to minimize its significance to the general public).

Even as a layperson, it is abundantly clear to me that Covid does not have the same ebb and flow as the seasonal flu. Instead, we have repeatedly seen two surges each year: one in the summer, typically after the 4th of July through September, and another beginning as winter holidays like Thanksgiving and Christmas are celebrated. Despite this, we are offered a vaccine timed not for Covid, but for the flu-- a totally different virus. A vaccine in October makes little sense when there has been a significant surge for months prior. The general public should be able to access a vaccine twice a year -- in preparation for the summer surge AND the winter surge. We know these vaccines have an incredibly short duration-- offering significant protection for only a handful of months. Yet we repeatedly allow the situation to go on unchecked, while more and more people become disabled through long covid. We have plenty of data that shows the timing of Covid surges, but the CDC and other outlets simply choose to ignore it.

Please do better.

title: Comment from Kendall, Augustin
comment: It is well established that vaccine efficacy wanes significantly in 4-6 months. So in order to be well-protected, the general public needs access to vaccines twice a year; not just vulnerable individuals, but everyone. If you are truly committed to public health you will make this possible.

title: Comment from Lemieux, Brenna
comment:
Hello, CDC leaders. I'm writing to urge you to make COVID vaccines available to everyone every six months (rather than yearly).

The science is clear that the efficacy of the currently available COVID vaccines wanes after four to six months, and we know that COVID surges happen at all times of the year – not seasonally, like the flu. Making semi-annual COVID vaccines (updated for the current strains) available to all of us would make all of us safer.

I can attest firsthand to the high cost of outdated vaccines. I caught COVID for the first time in August 2023, despite having gotten all available vaccines to date and continuing to wear a high-quality mask while indoors outside my home. At the time, I was nearly a year out from my latest vaccine dose – again, the most recent one available to me. That infection hit me hard; in fact, I am still recovering more than a year later. While I'm lucky to be able to work (very lucky, in fact; I am my family's primary breadwinner), I'm aware than many others in my situation are not. And I'm unable to do many of the activities (including running) that brought joy to me prior to my infection.

Making vaccines available to all Americans twice a year will help prevent more people like me becoming disabled by this virus. It will keep more children in school and able to learn. It will protect the most vulnerable among us, as vaccines (like most mitigations) work best when adopted universally.

Thank you for taking time to consider this comment.

Sincerely,
Brenna Lemieux

title: Comment from Underwood, Bridget
comment:
Since we are experiencing multiple COVID waves every year, with each wave representing a new variant, I'm writing to ask that the CDC recommend COVID vaccinations for people of all ages and health statuses twice a year, and that those vaccines be regularly updated to address the most current variants. It's really concerning seeing how many people are getting reinfected over and over again, and we have abundant scientific evidence that reinfection increases your chances of Long Covid and other serious long-term effects.

Having vaccines recommended and available twice a year for all people would make an enormous difference in public health!

comment: For a virus that continues to mutate rapidly, and whose long-term debilitating health effects are still being discovered, the public needs twice-yearly updated vaccines available to all. She needs to have as much current vaccination protection as possible for her age, and I need as much current vaccination protection as possible, so that I can continue to care for her.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: In order to keep people safe and preserve the health we each have left on a public and personal scale, we need access to Covid vaccines twice a year!

comment:
Please make updated Covid 19 vaccines available for all ages at least twice per year, and preferably 3 times per year!

title: Comment from hembree, mariah
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Piepzna-Samarasinha, Leah Lakshmi
comment: I'm writing to urge you to recommend COVID vaccines for all ages and health statuses at least twice a year (aka, spring vaccine access for all) and to support more frequent updates to the vaccines, adjusted for the latest variants. I'm a writer and disability justice organizer and someone who lives with disabilities and is high risk for further disablement and/or death if I get COVID again. In doing this, we work towards not exiling disabled and high risk folks from public human society, something that is endeminc when we feel we cannot safely go into an unmasked, unvaccinated public. Please stand on the right side of history.

title: Comment from Balke, Debra
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

As a pediatric neurologist and parent of a child with lupus it is critical that we bring down the volume of COVID19 cases in the general population, especially for those at highest risk of severe complications. I feel it is critical we recommend twice a year COVID19 vaccination to everyone, not just the high risk population, as there are many caregivers, family members, health care workers that if vaccinated are better able to prevent spread to the vulnerable populations. The general population who feels the need to vaccinate twice a year for the safety of their at risk relatives must essentially lie to the pharmacies to get the twice a year vaccination. This is a very uncomfortable situation to put the public in, just to improve the lives of the highest risk patients. I urge you to stop this nonsense and allow twice a year boosters for all who want them and to at least attempt to improve immunity levels in the population. I realize herd immunity concept is long gone but twice a year boosters may significantly bring down our COVID numbers and also make it possible to update the variants in each booster in a more timely fashion.

Board Certified Pediatric Neurologist
San Luis Obispo, California
