Cheryl Owsen

title: Comment from Lee, Brian
comment: Please recommend two vaccines a year for everyone.  We have two waves a year and current vaccines wain in effectiveness after about four months, leaving everyone, but especially high risk folks like myself, vulnerable.  Now that it's up to insurance providers to decide how and when to provide a vaccine I had trouble getting even my one shot this year and had to pay out of pocket.  Please allow us to better protect ourselves with two shots a year, and with more frequently updated targets for the shots.  Thanks.

title: Comment from Schanbaum, Patsy
comment:
On November 13th 2008, my daughter Jamie Schanbaum was admitted to the hospital severely ill with what was later diagnosed as Meningococcal Meningitis. Jamie survived, though sadly she lost both legs below the knees and all her fingers. They had to be amputated as a direct result of the infection. Jamie’s plight inspired her to educate the public about Meningitis. In the summer of 2009, we passed Senate Bill 819, a.k.a. The Jamie Schanbaum Act, requiring meningitis vaccinations of college students living in dorms/greek life and because of all the hard work of ACIP we were able to amend the Bill in 2011 based on ACIP recommendations.

The existing schedule has proven to be effective in preventing meningococcal disease, safeguarding vulnerable populations, and ensuring consistent immunization coverage. Disrupting this schedule could potentially lead to gaps in protection, creating unnecessary risk for young people and other high-risk groups. Moreover, changes in timing or recommendations might introduce confusion for healthcare providers and the public, potentially diminishing vaccine uptake.

The continuity of the current schedule reflects best practices based on research and years of public health experience. I kindly urge the committee to consider the potential risks of modifying it and to prioritize stability in our immunization strategy.

Thank you for your thoughtful consideration and the work you do to protect public health.
Patsy Schanbaum

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Vaccines twice a year will keep people healthy. Let's make our health a priority!

title: Comment from Marso, Andy
comment:
Dear Members of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP):
My name is Andy Marso. For the last 20 years, I have been a survivor of meningococcal disease. For the last 20 years I have been a quadruple amputee. For the last 20 years I have endured the stares of people who are disturbed by what’s left of my mangled hands and the skin grafts that cover my arms. For the last 20 years nearly every task has been harder for me — a thousand tasks a day including dressing myself and, more recently, dressing my son. For the last 20 years I have started every morning by strapping what’s left of my feet into prosthetic/orthotic leg braces, because I cannot stand or walk without them. For the last 20 years I have endured surgery after surgery — 18 in all — trying to ameliorate the damage meningococcal disease caused. For the last 20 years I have maxed out my out-of-pocket medical costs nearly every year, to the tune of tens of thousands of dollars (in addition to the approximately $2 million in care covered by insurance).
For the last 20 years I have tried to warn people about meningococcal disease, speaking at schools, infectious disease conferences, state legislatures, and yes, even ACIP. I spent years writing a book about my experience to try to reach even more people.
I have now been a meningitis survivor and anti-meningitis advocate almost as many years as not. Frankly, I am exhausted.
I am tired of thinking about this disease. I am tired of talking about this disease. I am tired of trying to convince people to get vaccinated against this disease. I am tired of having my integrity impugned by anti-vaccine activists who insist I must be doing this for the money, even though I do not take any money from pharmaceutical companies. I am tired of being told that I’m against “freedom” when what I’m really against is people being tethered to a ventilator in an ICU when a shot could have prevented it.
A big part of me feels that I have done my job as a vaccination advocate, and it’s time to move on with my life. Yet I will continue to get out there and keep doing it, if only to be a voice for the people who can no longer speak because meningococcal disease killed them. I owe that to them. I am one of the lucky ones.
But I could really use your help. You all understand how horrific this disease can be, at least in a clinical sense if not in a visceral sense like me. So, I am hoping you will have the courage to make a vaccine recommendation that is not based solely on cost or convenience, but also on the unfathomable toll this disease can take on a human life and the lives of the people around them.
To me, that means something clear and easy to implement (e.g., start your ACWY vaccine at age 11, then get your ACWY booster and a dose of MenB at age 16, then finish your MenB series). This maintains ACWY protection for young adolescents and makes it easier for physicians (and advocates like me) to explain to parents how to protect their kids from this horrible disease. The current lukewarm recommendation for the MenB vaccine is not sufficient. I know. MenB put me in the hospital for 140 days and cost me parts of all four limbs. We can do better.

Sincerely,
Andy Marso

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
As a parent of three young kids keeping my family safe from COVID is a major concern. After reviewing the available evidence that current vaccines lose most of their effectiveness in under six months I'm deeply concerned that the current once-a-year policy provides much less protection than twice. As we navigate the ongoing transmission of COVID in our classrooms, I would like to see the CDC taking further steps to protect our kids and the health of our other family members.

As part of this I urge you to prioritize updated and more frequent COVID-19 vaccine availability for my family and all Americans. A multilayered approach, including frequent updates (at least every six months) to match current variants, will go a long way to safeguard our citizens and especially children from the ongoing threat of COVID-19. I also ask that you also advocate for free vaccine access for all, including those who are uninsured and underinsured, both to protect those who are less well off as well as to protect the larger community by reducing prevalence.

Thank you.

title: Comment from Fritz, Inessa
comment: We want vaccines adjusted for current variants for both winter and summer . Covid virus does not behave as a flu therefore it’s vaccine  schedule should not be emulated based on a flu schedule

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Please make COVID vaccines available to everyone regardless of age, health status, and insurance status. Our current system of once-a-year vaccination is not able to keep up with the large COVID waves we have been seeing, with this summer’s wave reaching its peak long before the fall vaccines were available, and vaccines falling behind currently circulating variants. The waning efficacy of vaccines and frequent new COVID variants means people are not getting the protection they need from once-a-year vaccinations.

Uninsured people who cannot afford the vaccine are now being left behind, unable to protect themselves and therefore anyone else around them, who will be exposed to the virus as well if they are infected. When it comes to a widespread, highly transmissible virus, protecting at-risk individuals requires protecting everyone.

Recent vaccination also lowers risk of developing Long COVID. With COVID surging every year and multiple infections increasing a person’s risk of developing Long COVID, protecting people from developing Long COVID must be a priority.

My brother and I both have had Long COVID since 2022. Unable to get an updated vaccine this past spring, we both caught COVID again over the summer, worsening both of our symptoms. My brother has not been able to leave the house since the summer due to severe fatigue and I have since developed pre-diabetes despite being on a strict low-carb diet. We are both young and would have been considered “healthy” were it not for COVID.

comment: My husband and I travel by air in spring and fall to visit an elderly (89 years old) health-vulnerable parent. Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C).

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024, leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Hello all,

I am deeply concerned about this issue. I write in strong support of COVID vaccination at least twice a year (every six months or less) for all people of all ages, regardless of health status. As a high-risk person living in a multi-generational family with several high-risk (but not immunocompromised) people, none of us are currently eligible for extra COVID vaccinations even though several of us are at high risk of complications from COVID. We are eligible for paxlovid, but we are not currently eligible for COVID vaccinations more than once a year. It doesn't make sense! Everyone in my household would be overjoyed to be eligible for COVID vaccines twice a year (or more).

comment: COVID causes lifelong damage to the immune system, much like HIV. Vaccines should be available twice a year to support the immune system given that common sense practices such as barrier protections are not commonly observed.

title: Comment from Cole-Vadjic, Kelli
comment: I am writing in support of making Covid vaccines available to everyone twice yearly and updating the vaccine formulas more regularly for maximum efficacy.  Prior to developing long-Covid with life changing cardiac and neurocognitive symptoms, I worked as a physician assistant in a community oncology practice.  I took care of immunocompromised patients, and I believe that our vulnerable friends and family deserve better protection through a better protected community.  I also need to avoid getting Covid again myself if possible.  I take many precautions including avoiding public crowds and masking, and I depend on vaccination as another layer of protection.  Because of rapidly waning protection following Covid vaccination, I am not protected much of the year, and I would like to have access to another vaccine in the spring for additional protection.  I also worry about my daughter and all of the children who will likely face a lifetime of dealing with Covid and its sequelae.  I believe it is important to minimize the number of cumulative Covid infections for everyone, and improved access to vaccination will help our children and others hopefully avoid frequent and unnecessary infections and the associated risks. Please also consider development and approval of a non-mRNA vaccine (like Novavax) for children so families have vaccine options which could potentially help with vaccine utilization.  Thank you for considering the health of my patients, my family, and me in your decision to improve access to Covid vaccines.

title: Comment from Nespor, Jan
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

To Whom It May Concern,
I am writing as a teacher to urge you to recommend COVID vaccinations at least twice a year, regardless of age or risk categories. There hasn’t been a week this fall when I haven’t had at least 10% of my class absent because of COVID – and a few students have taken more than a week to recover. Things have gotten worse as students mistakenly assume that COVID is “over” and the slow approval of vaccines (which usually happens well after the school year begins). If past experience holds, winter and spring will bring even larger numbers of absentees and sickness, to say nothing of the potentially long-term cognitive deficits associated with severe COVID infection. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

Here I quote from the “Peoples CDC”: “The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C).”

These are critical educational issues as well as health issues. I urge you to make Spring 2025 vaccines available to all and to recommend twice a year vaccinations.

Respectfully,
Jan Nespor, PhD

title: Comment from Cappiello, Mary Ann
comment: My otherwise incredibly healthy and fit 19-year-old daughter has been living with long COVID since spring 2020. She was exposed to COVID before lockdown, had an asymptomatic infection, and the symptoms began in April 2020 during lockdown. By early July we had a COVID test that showed antibodies. She's been working with Boston Children's Hospital since spring of 2021, and undergoing the Mt. Sinai Method of PT for long COVID here in New Hampshire January-August of 2023 changed her life. But she is now a sophomore in college and STILL struggling with maintaining a normal schedule. She STILL has to think about her energy envelope every hour of every day, every day out of every week, every week out of every month in order to not miss out on the things most important to her. Her ballet dancing - which she has done since she was 2, and was quite good at it - has fallen by the wayside as she has to prioritize her studies. We hope for a day when there is a mucosal vaccine that prevents people from catching and spreading the COVID virus. Until that day, please consider offering twice-yearly shots to everyone, as we know summer surges happen as regularly as winter surges on a six-month schedule. So too should the vaccines updated for variants. Maybe in the future there will be a cure for what my daughter has had to deal with and from which she continues to suffer. Expanded and more regular vaccines would allow anyone who wants to protect themselves from this ever-changing virus to do so, and will allow our most vulnerable citizens to go out knowing that others around them are protecting themselves, too, not just those most vulnerable.

title: Comment from Matthews, Shana
comment:
Please recommend COVID vaccination twice a year (at least every six months) for everyone, regardless of health status.

I am young, and do not meet the requirements for being immunocompromised, but I have a serious health condition that is exacerbated by Covid. In addition, my mother is immunocompromised.

Data clearly shows that Covid vaccines wane in efficacy after just a few months, regardless of age or immunocompromised status [1-4].

I deserve the right to protect myself and my family members by getting vaccinated on a schedule that follows the science of vaccine efficacy.

The CDC’s recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
It truly feels dystopian to have to fight for the right and recommendation to access bi-annual vaccination for COVID19. Scientists have again and again re-iterated the 4 month durability of these vaccines. This virus is constantly evolving and is data in regard to long lasting health ramifications. We need at the bare minimum bi-annual access to vaccines against this deadly virus. In the past nine weeks, during a lull of spread over 10K American lives have been lost to this incidious virus. Yet, inadequate and slow response is to blame. What is public health? Is it allowing preventable deaths to continue relentlessly? Where are our science lead decisions? Where is the newest innovations in treatment and vaccines? The public has been painfully left behind. I know, I have lost three family members this year alone. In the spring, when updated, or even recommended access to the vaccination I believe would have saved their lives.!
This slow action and ridiculous protocol in comparison with the flu is frankly inadequate. This virus is absolutely not the flu. It is much more deadly and further, it is a year round virus. Wave after wave of infection and re-infection abound in America. Why is it that you sit idly by? There is more to be done! You must approve accessibility to those who actually care about survival in the onslaught of infection that still plagues us.
Updated vaccines and accessibility. That is the bare minimum. Please give us, that care that opportunity.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Please allow individuals to get an additional vaccine every 6 months for Sars-CoV-2.

Also, a few (or not so few) words should be added to address the poorly used and wrong-headed concept of "hybrid immunity".

What is being referred to here are people who have had infections and have an immune response that continues to provide some protection against infection and people who have been vaccinated and have an immune response that continues to provide some protection against infection.

The useless and recently made-up ("hybrid immunity") term only applies (poorly) to population level protections, and it certainly doesn't apply to the Covid situation. Anyone using it hasn't thought through the ramifications of a population continually being infected repeatedly by Covid, nor applied Covid-specific understanding and are relying on knowledge application from other pathogens that does not apply here.

If we were a small village that was reasonably isolated and some pathogen came along, like chicken pox or small pox, where you reasonably can understand that a person would get one infection and if they survived, would not get it again and have protection going forward, then you might be able to have some semblance of herd protection. Of course, the system is always changing because people are born or die of something else, or people move into the village, or away, so there will be outbreaks at some point in time.

But with Covid we are not a small isolated village. So that doesn't happen. There is no approaching a near steady state. Also, our immune systems ramp up to provide temporary protection and then wane again. Either the same strain of Covid can infect a person again or else they can be infected by another variant of Covid, which is what we commonly see because Covid is mutating rapidly, so we're getting two to three big waves a year of infections.

Another problem is that individuals can forward transmit Covid even if they have had Covid umpteen times before or been previously vaccinated. Again making "hybrid immunity" a nonsensical term to use.

Also, it is highly preferable for individuals to be protected through vaccination rather than infection because each and every infection causes damage to the individual, either a lot of a little, but always damage. Some people will die from the acute infection. Some people will die up to three years later from cardiac issues or stroke.

Having infection-based protection then becomes highly unethical as a way to think about allowing a population to try to contain the pathogen. First, it is a falsehood to think that the population protection will remain. There is no steady state and protection wanes. Second, damage is done to the individuals because of the infections, including death of individuals. Populations are made up of individuals. To protect the population, we must protect the individuals.

No lasting herd immunity has been accomplished without the use of vaccines. While we will not vaccinate our way out of the continuing Covid pandemic (cleaning the air, and other non-pharmaceutical actions will be required on a grand scale to approach that), vaccines are an important part of continuing to protect individuals from accruing as much damage with each additional infection.

The part of "hybrid" that encompasses the people being infected by Sars-CoV-2 is the part where Public health is failing. Failing because those individuals accrue damage from that infection, failing because an infected individual isn't protected from future infections, and failing because that infected individual can forward transmit during that infection and future infections to other people who will also accrue damage and/or die from that infection.

title: Comment from Miller, Emily
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Farinella, Gail
comment: Since the Covid pandemic continues to rage unabated, and public health advice is nonexistent. Please allow everyone to try and protect themselves from this potentially disabling virus. Your failures have been many. Please right this ship.

title: Comment from D'Agostino, Nickolas
comment:
I see numerous studies regarding the long-term impacts of Covid, yet I neither feel nor see the sense of urgency in our news or in policy. I am writing to support the option of twice yearly vaccination for all age groups, given that all evidence points to quickly waning efficacy. I'm willing to pay out of pocket for a 2nd dose, yet I don't even have that option due to policy.

I support a moonshot-type effort, similar to the original pandemic response, toward nasal and pan-coronavirus vaccines, and methods to strengthen the existing vaccines (e.g. adjuvants). I do realize that research is ongoing, but the effort needs to increase 10-fold.

I am a layperson, and I feel left behind and out in the cold. Policy no longer supports masking, even in healthcare settings, and talk of indoor air quality is still at a pitifully low level. I want to do my part for myself, for my family, and for my society, but I no longer feel supported by government.

title: Comment from Trumpener, Katie
comment:
I'm an immune-compromised professor, who just returned to live (masked) teaching after 4 years--and promptly got COVID. The continuing health of the immune-compromised community depends vitally not only on OUR frequent access to vaccines, but to the broader availability of COVID vaccines for all. PLEASE make it possible for the immune-compromised to continue to get frequent doses (ie every 3 months) and PLEASE make it possible for everyone to get a COVID vaccine at least twice a year. Otherwise the immune-compromised will have to remain devastatingly self-isolated, a condition that is TERRIBLE for our mental and physical health. We need to be able to become fully functioning members of society again. In one of the world's richest countries, better epidemic protection for all SHOULD well be possible. THANK YOU FOR LISTENING. I should add that at my university, I am also the faculty advisor to the disabled students' affinity and lobbying group. So I see close up how concrete government policies (or their lack) and government rhetoric has a trickledown effect on how institutions of higher education handle the ongoing crisis of COVID--and whether students can study in some degree of physical safety. Whether the government and the universities wish to acknowledge it or not, we are STILL in a pandemic, and our social and medical policies should reflect that fact.
Thank you for listening.

title: Comment from Wilkinson, Ryli
comment:
Updated vaccines must be available for all people twice a year. As a small business owner who relies heavily on in-person events to pay my bills, the effects of having a spike in positive cases every summer is simply devastating. I have to be at my events in person to sell my wares, and I need them to be busy and crowded to sell enough. If I get sick from someone in the crowd and have to cancel future events, it's not just missed income I have to think about; most events include non-refundable vendor fees, and it impacts my reputation and ability to find new customers for the future.

As an LGBT owned business, June is a staple month for me. It's also when cases start spiking every year without a spring vaccine update. Please make vaccines available for those of us who want to stay safe. Thank you.

title: Comment from Scroggins, Leon
comment:
I'm writing to ask you to please recommend COVID vaccines for everyone at least twice a year. We know now that immunity wanes quickly, and there are multiple surges per year. In addition, everyone is at risk of getting long COVID even if they seem healthy otherwise. The more people who have gotten a vaccine recently, the safer we all are.

Also, please support more frequent updates to the vaccines. We know that the virus mutates quickly, making our older vaccines less effective.

Thanks!

title: Comment from Cournoyer, Shielding
comment: We need twice-yearly covid vaccines authorized for all ages. The rate of mutation is simply too swift for annual vaccines to make a dent in infection and hospitalization rates. Covid has created a bi-annual illness peak, doubling the fallout of a typical flu season. None of our institutions are equipped to absorb so much illness, as demonstrated by last winter's unprecedented capacity crises at hospitals like Massachusetts General Hospital, and schools around the country had to shut down early due to illness levels too great to remain operational. Studies by several HR firms show a sharp increase in absence rates since 2020, with one company (Dayforce) noting an increase in sick calls of 50%! Labor shortages plague every industry, and at the same time school absence rates since 2020 have "exploded almost everywhere," in the words of the New York Times. Caring for sick children additionally pulls many women, in particular, out of the labor force, endangering viable careers. All of this is just the impact of acute covid, without even trying to calculate the economic and personal losses of people disabled by long covid or the grief of the loved ones of those who do not survive infection. Please authorize and encourage twice-annual vaccines, and please be inclusive of vaccine brands like Novavax which may be safer for immunocompromised people. Please keep an eye out as well for the next-gen mucosal vaccines, now in testing stages, which intend to curb transmission as well as hospitalization rates. We'll never truly get back to normal without such tools.

title: Comment from Brutcher, Austin
comment:
Important material modifications that I would like to see are, vaccine access twice a year for all ages and immune conditions, acknowledgment that the virus is airborne, messaging advising masking is an important and effective method to prevent infection and spread of the virus, advising of the importance of ventilation and air filtration in keeping a healthy populace, much the same way filtered water is.

Thank you

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

title: Comment from Black , Jay
comment: It remains unhealthy to allow Americans to fall victim to the 1000 deaths per week we see from COVID, in part, due to the lack of protections like these. In the absence of the needed focus on developing quality nasal vaccines which can stop the spread of coronavirus, our nation knows that biannual vaccination is an important stop gap measure. It is not the only protective measure that needs to be taken. We are seeing how a failure to maintain a focus on mask wearing, and social distancing has just left vulnerable people to repeatedly get the virus thus risking long-term disability and death. Our current president's inability to continue his run for a second term was even linked to a novel infection -- yet school age children are being forced to get educated in unsafe environments where they are likely to get this damaging disease?! Allowing the general populace to repeatedly get infected is anti business, as workers needed to keep the economy flowing are ultimately impaired by repeated infections, which limits their ability to work. They also expose coworkers to infection. Businesses have been fortunate, in some respect, that COVID has not been more deadly overall. title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Related to Covid vaccines, I strongly encourage the committee to recommend the release of up-to-date Covid booster vaccines to everyone twice per year. I know that me and my family would have taken advantage of this were it available, and it likely would have prevented the one case of Covid that I have gotten and from which I continue to suffer long-term symptoms. title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
It is so important for communal public health for Covid vaccitions to be accessible without many barriers attached. Getting sick with Covid has impacted my overall lifelong health as well as done irreversible damage to my family members as well. It is so important!!

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
I’m writing today not only as a member of the public, but as someone who deeply cares about the health and safety of our loved ones, our neighbors, and the most vulnerable in our communities. We’ve all seen the devastating toll that COVID-19 has taken on families and friends, and even now, as the virus continues to unfortunately evolve, the need for protection has never been greater.

The decision to get vaccinated is a choice that protects more than just ourselves — it safeguards everyone around us. This is why I urge the CDC to take a stronger stance: COVID-19 vaccinations must be recommended at bare minimum twice a year, for EVERYONE, regardless of age or health status. Too many people are still being left behind, confused by restrictive guidelines or limited access. We cannot afford to put up barriers that prevent those most at risk from getting the protection they so desperately need.

For families with elderly parents, for those caring for loved ones with compromised immune systems, for the child who could still develop serious complications like MIS-C — these are the people we must keep in our hearts as we make these decisions. Every family should have the peace of mind that comes with knowing their loved ones can receive a COVID-19 vaccine whenever they need it. Every 6 months must be the standard, because we know that vaccine efficacy wanes after just a few months [1-4], and we can’t afford to wait for another surge to act.

But this isn’t just about protection from death. It’s about ensuring that the small moments — family gatherings, birthday parties, even a simple hug — can happen with less fear of acquiring severe illness and long-term disability. Recent research has shown that staying up to date on COVID-19 vaccinations also reduces the risk of Long COVID [5], a condition that has already capsized the lives of millions of people, leaving some unable to work or care for their families.

For many, access to these vaccines remains a challenge. When the CDC’s Bridge Access Program ended in August 2024 [7], it left uninsured and underinsured adults without a clear path to receive these critical vaccines. Imagine the parents, the caregivers, the individuals who want to protect themselves and their loved ones, but can’t because they don’t have access to affordable vaccines. These are the people we need to think about when we make decisions that impact public health. No one should have to choose between protecting their health and paying their bills.

The CDC’s voice carries immense weight. Your clear recommendation for biannual COVID-19 vaccinations would not only guide healthcare providers but also ensure that health insurance covers these critical boosters, making it possible for more people to get vaccinated. We must also advocate for a return of programs like Bridge, so that cost never becomes a barrier to someone getting the protection they deserve.

Please, as you consider the future of COVID-19 vaccination guidance, think about the lives that could be saved, the families that could be spared from grief, and the children who could be shielded from suffering. We are all in this together, and protecting our communities means protecting each other.

Thank you for your dedication and for considering this urgent call to action. Let’s ensure that we are ready to meet the challenges ahead with compassion, equity, and a commitment to keeping all of us safe.

title: Comment from Citizen, A concerned
comment:
You need workers. You can’t have workers if they’re all taken down by uncontrolled Covid.

Pls do the barest minimum for public health. Half the country has no savings and terrible insurance. Please. Sort of care.

title: Comment from Reuter, Katheryn
comment: Please Please Please allow for Covid Vaccine twice a year for everyone.  This virus isn't going away - if we don't manage it better, we'll be suffering the consequences!!!

title: Comment from Martin, Sally
comment: Twice yearly Covid vaccines should be available at no cost to ALL citizens.  To target certain age groups or situations ignores the fact that “Family” should be respected and even honored.  If I (age 73) can get the vaccine, but my 6 grandchildren cannot, then I must isolate from those children because the vaccine is not 100% effective.  At this time in the US, the support of extended family is more important than ever before.  Also, the vaccine should be available and encouraged to ALL individuals, (citizens or not) in order to protect more of us, and slow the spread.  It’s called acting for the Greater Good.

title: Comment from Lundgren, MD, Alston
comment:
I am a retired MD and life long non-smoker who developed lung cancer at age 78 in 2022. Surgery to remove the cancer was successful, but I had a strong autoimmune response to osimertinib, the strongly recommended adjuvant treatment for my type tumor. Thus I effectively have only one lung and am at high risk should I catch Covid.

For my personal protection and others who are immunocompromised or otherwise at high risk, individual vaccination is important. So also would be herd immunity through vaccination of the general public.

Because Covid mutates rapidly, it is essential that new vaccines be developed on an ongoing basis. Because vaccine efficacy falls rapidly, immunization should be encouraged and made available often - as updated vaccines become available but at least quarterly.

title: Comment from Smith, Raven
comment: As an immunocompromised person that provides at-home care for an immunocompromised senior, having up-to-date vaccines are crucial to helping our quality of life. With how quickly the virus has mutated and the frequency of surges throughout the year, having to wait until the fall for a new vaccine is simply egregious. We're essentially told to play Russian Roulette with our health for 3/4s of the year, and HOPE that by the time Fall vaccines are available, we haven't suffered an infection. Biannual vaccines are the bare minimum that we need in order to help protect us from illness, and they should be available to everyone, regardless of age or insurance status. Since the abrupt end of the PHE, navigating the world has become even more difficult as immunocompromised people, because it feels as though society en masse has dubbed us as "acceptable losses". My life, the life of my parent, and the lives of other disabled people are NOT worthless or acceptable losses. We are people that deserve to have a place in society, and the only way that can happen is if we have more tools at our disposal to keep ourselves and others healthy. Please make twice-yearly vaccines available to everyone!

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: I support the availability of free, updated Covid vaccines for all Americans, two times per year. Additionally, Paxlovid should be available to all citizens for free or least at a reasonable cost. Lastly, there should also be more government research dollars allocated to the development of pan-coronavirus vaccines and for nasal vaccines. The science clearly supports this. And it is the morally proper thing to do. It will also ultimately be cost effective for the country as a matter of public health policy because it will enhance the overall health of citizens and reduce more expensive hospitalizations and related services. Thank you for considering this comment.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6]. This massive summer surge that we all experienced recently was very telling of waning vaccine efficacy with only annual vaccines.

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

comment: It would be beneficial for everyone regardless of age and health conditions to have access to an additional dose of the COVID-19 vaccine in the spring. Two shots provide better protection instead of one.

title: Comment from Willer, Janet
comment: As a person who has multiple medical problems, I strongly recommend that the CDC allow everyone to be vaccinated against Covid-19 at least twice per year. title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Please make updated COVID vaccinations available to everyone who wants/needs them every 6 months. Please also make them free and affordable to all.  Please also continue to put pressure on Hospitals and clinics for providers to wear masks when providing patient care. Thank you very much!

title: Comment from Vernier, Sarah
comment:
Vaccination & Protection for All!

Everyone deserves access to protection from disease and severe health effects, so I support any effort to make vaccines available.
I and my partner both benefited from expanded access to the HPV vaccine (a cancer-preventing vaccine!) and no out-of-pocket costs due to the recommendations by regulatory bodies.
I urge this regulatory body to consider how their recommendations influence insurance coverage and general acceptability and uptake of vaccines, especially in a society where vaccines are increasingly considered untrustworthy when they are unparalleled scientific achievements.

I would like to be able to get a COVID-19 booster more often than in just the fall season, as I am concerned about the multiple non-seasonal waves as well as the rapidity of variant change. I have been a two-time recipient of Novavax and would like to see this particular vaccine updated more frequently and available to ALL in the general population. Research demonstrates that SARS-COVID-19 is an immune-depleting virus and it is critical that everyone who is not YET immunocompromised be able to protect themselves from BECOMING immunocompromised. I am lucky that my insurance covers my COVID-19 shots, but there are also many people who are under- or uninsured that also want to be able to protect themselves with basic, easy measures that are proven to reduce severe disease. We should want to increase vaccine uptake and thereby reduce strain on our overburdened healthcare systems and doctors through any means necessary. Expanded vaccination access gives us more opportunities.

title: Comment from Barnett, Therem
comment:
Please make COVID vaccines available for all ages and health statuses at least twice a year (spring vaccine access for all).

Please facilitate more frequent updates to the vaccines, adjusted for the latest variants and the possibility of nasal vaccines that could prevent sickness.

title: Comment from Hertz, Christopher
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be allowed for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Dziedzic, Sarah
comment: Please update your policy to recommend 2 yearly doses of COVID-19 vaccine. It's simply a matter of accommodating for waning immunity, new variants, and multiple waves of infection per year. From a more personal standpoint, as someone who has had Long Covid since 2020, and who takes multiple precautions on a daily basis to avoid Covid exposure and infection (and thus the worsening of my symptoms and quality of life), it would make all the difference for me to have access to a second dose of the vaccine each year. As it currently stands, I diligently monitor wastewater and case counts and can't participate in social events when numbers are high. You have to understand that for so many of us, our social lives have changed dramatically, and not for the better! We're just asking for a chance to engage in the things we enjoy, in our communities, with the people we love––and to be able to do so with the added protection of two doses of the vaccine per year.

title: Comment from Warren, Trina
comment:
For everyone's sake, the CDC needs to recommend COVID vaccines for anyone, whatever their age or health conditions, at least twice a year, so there is spring vaccine access for all. This protects not just the recipient of the vaccine, but everyone they come into contact with, and in turn, everyone who those people come into contact with. AND there needs to be support for more frequent updates to the vaccines, adjusted for the latest variants, as well as faster development of nasal vaccines, and vaccines that prevent infection in the first place.
I have suffered for 17 years with life-diminishing auto-immune disease brought on by a routine infection, one that I'd had numerous times before, until one more occurrence brought on disaster. My grandchildren have chronic health issues. Their other grandmother (a former champion triathlete) is suffering from Long Covid. None of this is unusual anymore. You can do something to help us. You can do something to help everyone. Expand the access to vaccines, so we can better protect ourselves and the people we love, and others in our communities, all of whom deserve better care than is currently being provided.

title: Comment from Moore, Kevin
comment:
Please vote YES on the motion to “Use of additional doses of COVID-19 vaccine in immunocompromised individuals and older adults following an initial dose of 2024–2025 vaccine.”

As an immunocompromised individual of 55 years old with immunocompromised members among my family and friends, I strongly urge the CDC to recommend COVID vaccines for all ages and health statuses at least twice a year, with access for all age groups and health statuses to Spring time vaccines. The CDC must also support more frequent updates to the vaccines, adjusted for the latest variants.

The impact of Covid on my family has been widespread fatigue, compromised immunity, increased severity of postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS), and exacerbation of chronic illness conditions. Consequently, these health impacts have also compromised our engagement with social, educational, and economic activities. The public at large continues to experience surges in infection by Covid variants. Increasing access to vaccines and awareness of their availability and the spread of Covid to our communities is a must for society to function.

Thank you

title: Comment from Lockwood, Madge
comment: I am asking that COVID vaccines for all ages and health statuses be available at least twice a year for everyone regardless of age or health status and that the CDC support more frequent updates to the vaccines, adjusted for the latest variants. The vaccines effectiveness wanes and COVID is a year round virus so more than one dose is needed per year. I work with cancer and immunocompromised people and I want the highest protection for myself to protect them and myself.

title: Comment from Ruday, Clare
comment:
My name is Clare Ruday, and I was a public health nurse through the first year of Covid. I then retired and volunteered with the Medical Reserve Corps giving Covid vaccines for the next two years. The data on the long-term effect of Covid on the body is clear. We are witnessing a mass disabling event, yet we do nothing to improve air quality in our schools, and the CDC guidelines dates to lump it in with other respiratory diseases.

We should be doing everything in our power to protect our children and ourselves, and that includes free vaccines twice yearly. The more often people are infected, the greater the likelihood of long term sequelae, and the United States does not have the social supports in place to support a population with a high percentage of disabled individuals. Maintaining the health of our children should be enough incentive, but, if not, the economy will come grinding to a halt when there aren’t enough individuals healthy enough to work any longer. The government has the duty to protect the citizens, and the bare minimum is providing free vaccines twice yearly and continuing to update those vaccines to best prevent the disease.
Clare Ruday, RN MSN

title: Comment from Baker, Kelsey
comment:
I have been infected with Covid-19 three times, and as a result, I now have small airway disease and asthma. Each times I’ve been infected with Covid, it has been during the summer surge. My pulmonologist recommended that I get vaccinated against Covid in the Spring as well as the Fall now. I wonder, if I had been able to get vaccinated in the Spring the last few years, maybe I would not have been infected with Covid, maybe I wouldn’t have small airway disease and asthma now.

I want others to have access to Covid vaccines to protect themselves and our communities from Covid surges during the Winter and the Summer. There should be no cost barriers to receiving a Covid vaccine. Community health is critically important to our society and economy. What will happen when more and more people become disabled like me from repeated Covid infections? Please make Covid vaccines free and available twice a year.

How you, your family, or your community would be impacted by spring vaccine eligibility being restricted to only high risk groups (such as older age or immunocompromised status)
Barriers to vaccination your have faced, particularly if your eligibility was questioned or misinterpreted by a vaccine provider
How out-of-pocket costs are a barrier to getting the latest vaccines

title: Comment from Snowman, Hallie
comment: I am a teacher concerned about the long term health impacts of repeated reinfection from Covid-19.  Right now, I carefully time my vaccination for ONE of the TWO yearly surges, but since vaccination protection wanes quickly after 3 months, I am left unprotected for the other rise in numbers.  Teachers have some of the highest rates of Long Covid, and despite wearing a mask and adding $1000s of dollars of ventilation to my classroom, I am not protected well enough.  Please encourage and make vaccinations available more than once a year.

title: Comment from Alber, Kate
comment:
Twice-annual vaccine is crucial to slowing the spread and severity of Covid. The government should be breaking down any and all barriers to vaccine access, for everyone, especially the vulnerable and un/under-insured. The burden of this disease on Americans cannot be overstated. It has killed millions, and changed all of our lives. We need our government to be at the forefront of vaccine development and access. The efficacy of these vaccines wanes, and new variants are always popping up. This means a once/year vaccine schedule is not enough, and is actively contributing to the mutations. The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination. Please do the right thing and protect the people of this country. It is your duty.

title: Comment from Lumpkin, Steven
comment:
I’m writing as a citizen who is very concerned about the dangers of covid-19 to ask the ACIP to recommend that the immunization schedule for covid-19 be updated to twice annually for all Americans, regardless of immunocompromised status.

As we have seen, covid-19 remains a very dangerous disease. While our current vaccines and variants have reduced mortality, death from covid remains 4x that of the flu. Aside from mortality, covid produces serious long term consequences- from the most well documented being long covid, which can permanently disable those afflicted, to more recent discoveries that catching covid can increase risk of heart attack and stroke for up to 3 years after even a mild case. This is a disease people need to be protecting themselves from. Additionally, covid, unlike the flu, doesn’t currently have a “season”- while a single fall dose makes sense for something like influenza, which only spreads over the winter, covid is currently surging about 4x annually, and is still spreading when transmission is low. Studies have shown that our current covid vaccines are only protective against covid for 3-6 months, meaning that even conscientious Americans seeking to protect themselves and others as much as possible are effectively unprotected for 1/2 to 3/4 of the year.

For these reasons, until we develop more effective vaccines and treatments (which I deeply hope is receiving serious effort), I’m writing to ask the ACIP to recommend updating the immunization schedule for covid-19 to every 6 months for all Americans, so that those of us like myself who are conscientious about avoiding catching and spreading
this dangerous disease can give ourselves and our peers more effective protection.

Thank you.

title: Comment from Howard, Chae
comment: I am a resident of Tennessee and I am immunocompromised from a medication. Public health is critical to keep people like me safe and healthy. COVID is a devastating disease that can easily kill or permanently disable. Vaccines are a critically important tool in preventing and attenuating cases of COVID-19. COVID-19 is not like influenza. It is not seasonal. There NEEDS to be access to at least two COVID-19 vaccines a year for all ages. COVID doesn't discriminate by age. Socioeconomic status is also a factor. Healthcare should be free for everyone. Not being able to afford a vaccine should not be a factor. Allow access to at least two COVID-19 vaccines to everyone, or people will needlessly die. Their blood will be on your hands.

title: Comment from Black, Matthew
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

This body should be aggressive and proactive in protecting and improving public health. Accessible COVID vaccination is critical to this.

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

comment:
To Whom It May Concern,
I am writing to request that everyone in the US has access to Covid-19 vaccines at least twice a year. In light of numerous scientific studies stating the same findings -this disease causes an exponential risk of cardiovascular events, long Covid, metabolic dysfunction, neurological damage, risks of cancer, etc- we obviously need more protection against a continually evolving virus than just one vaccine a year. Immunocompromised folks may need two so as to continue to be able to participate in society without fear of devastating harm. My family and I are some of these people. Please consider just making the vaccines available at least 2xa year. Thank you

title: Comment from M, Maia
comment: Protection from infection has been shown to wane after several months, meaning I spent half the year if not more with little to no protection from infection via vaccines. I have elderly family and friends with compromised immune systems, who I would like to protect from infection, in addition to caring for my own health. I highly support making Covid vaccinations available to all adults on a twice yearly basis to increase levels of protection from infection. Thank you.

title: Comment from Stark, Lauren
comment: Please make Covid vaccines available for all ages twice per year. I am a parent of two children under six years old, both of whom attend schools. We were in the middle of a Covid surge when they started school this year, and yet no pediatric Covid vaccines were available. This means they had to start the school year surrounded by kids with active Covid cases, without having received any vaccines in the past year. This is unconscionable. We should not have to choose between exposing our kids unprotected to Covid and sending them to school. Long Covid affects both adults and children, and the CDC needs to do much more to fulfill its duty to prevent and control the ongoing Covid pandemic. Making vaccines available twice per year for all ages would be a start.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: I implore you to make Covid vaccination available to all people of all ages at least 2x per year, in order to reduce the impact of Covid infections on both individuals and the community around us. My sister is immune compromised and disabled, and has been living largely in isolation for the past 4.5 years, knowing that getting infected could undermine what little health and energy she has remaining. My family and my brother's family are her main lifelines, taking her to doctor appointments, helping her get and now maintain her disability benefits, and help her with daily tasks such as dishes, cooking food, cleaning, taking out her trash, getting her mail, and much more. Though we adults can minimize our exposure, the children are in elementary and high school, in schools that are a proverbial petri dish of potential disease, since have not taken reasonable steps to improve ventilation or otherwise spread of airborne illnesses that science shows would significantly lower the spread covid and other illnesses. My family is vigilant about ensuring we stay up to date on our vaccines in order to lower the risk to my sister, and our own risks of the long-term, multi-system impacts of Covid, and we continue to mask around her. But with the relatively swift decline in protection from infection, we are risking so much more than we need to. And we know our family is not the only one risking so much by potentially exposing our loved ones to this and other diseases. We need a multi-pronged approach, and while I hope we can better advocate for systemic changes, like improved ventilation standards and funds to update public spaces, UV light sterilization, and others, I and others are currently fending for ourselves. While much of the world around us minimizes or ignores the acute and long-term risks to ourselves and our higher-risk family, friends, and community members, please provide us the tools to better protect ourselves by allowing us to choose an additional dose of the covid vaccine each year, which better matches the multi-wave "seasonality" of Covid.

title: Comment from Wolf, Rachel
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Glover, E
comment:
The success of vaccines has been so great that people today don't remember what it was like before we had them. My mother was old enough to have had childhood classmates killed by polio. I'm of the "get chickenpox as a kid because it's worse as an adult" generation. We declared measles defeated, but now it's reappearing in low-vax-rate communities.

This cannot continue. We can't let the most life-saving technology in human history be compromised and weakened. Anything that encourages vaccination should be supported. Making vaccines easily available for free to everyone (whether through insurance or uninsured) will help that.

Some may complain about the cost. What of the cost to our society with increased rates of death or disability? Businesses complain that they can't hire people, or there are supply shortages—a lot of that has been caused by COVID-19. We've collectively tried pretending it doesn't exist anymore, but clearly that isn't working. Covid has no season, and the efficacy of the vax is only 3-6 months. We should be encouraging twice-yearly vaccination, and updates to the vax as new variants arise.

Children have to go to school, clustered together in rooms that have no regulations for virus-related air cleanliness. Whether COVID or measles or pertussis or polio, children are particularly vulnerable to life-altering (or ending) disease. We need to make it easy for their parents to get them vaxed.

(I personally favor mandatory vaxing, but I don't think that's what's under discussion in this document?)

Thank you for having this important discussion. I want today's children to grow up healthy, and I want to grow old without being extra vulnerable.

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Please make covid vaccines accessible to all twice a year, as they wane too quickly to be fully effective only once a year. Six month intervals should still limit imprinting concerns, and the extra money the vaccines cost will be more than saved in protection reducing future medical costs and strain on the hospital system. I was pregnant last year and had insisted on a spring covid vaccine to protect the baby. As we'd had fall vaccines, my husband was unable to also get one to protect us. We wore KN95/N95 masks everywhere, but he still caught covid at my obstetrics office during a new strain surge 3 weeks before my due date. I was thoroughly exposed for two days, and then he was very sick for two weeks while I isolated from him and was totally alone and 9 months pregnant. Due to my spring vaccine however, I did not catch it! I did not have covid during labor, my baby (having received vaccine benefits from the timing of my shot) did not get covid from us or at the hospital, and my husband recovered only days before needing to go to the hospital. I wish he'd been able to get a spring vaccine to avoid this. It was so stressful, being alone and that pregnant and not knowing if he'd recover soon enough to be able to be with me during delivery. I was so grateful that I'd fought for that vaccine but angry I had to fight plus he was arbitrarily unable to get one. It was also ridiculous that if you didn't get pregnant at the right "time" ie your third trimester lining up with early fall timing, then you weren't technically authorized to get a vaccine that could protect your baby despite having both doctor's notes and pharmacists saying it would be safer to immunize. Please just fix this by offering access to shots for all who want one twice a year. This request is not unique to me or my situation during pregnancy, and there are many, many other people and issues that two covid vaccines a year could help, including providing additional protection from long covid which can impact anyone at any age.

title: Comment from Anderson, Susan
comment: I am writing to strongly advocate for COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) as a general recommendation for people of all ages, regardless of health status. We are only a few years out from COVID’s start. Although there has been significant work done to understand the disease and its impact, we still are years from being able to fully comprehend the long-term effects of COVID and how multiple exposures impact the body over time. I am deeply committed to protecting my family––and particularly my two young children––from getting COVID. I still mask in many or most social occasions. However, I also recognize that masking is not desirable under all circumstances. Regular, biyearly vaccinations offer me and my family an additional layer of protection. They should be freely and widely available.

title: Comment from Lane, Erica
comment: I am commenting today to urge full access to covid vaccines twice a year for all ages and health statues. I have asthma and other health conditions which make twice yearly vaccination a personal priority but everyone would be better served and we could prevent many more infections if recommendations and access includes everyone in the spring vaccines. Vaccines also need to be updated and released quickly as SARS-CoV-2 continues to evolve rapidly to evade our old vaccines. Thank you.

title: Comment from Craig, Ray
comment: I am writing to urge that COVID vaccines be recommended for all ages and health statuses at least twice a year (spring vaccine access for all) and to ask for financial and research support for more frequent updates to COVID vaccines, especially to be adjusted for the latest variants. These vaccines need to be freely available to the public and without barriers like costs and requiring insurance. If they are not easily accessible, people will not be able to get them and will not be able to take advantage of the added protection vaccines can offer. As a young person in a transitional phase of life (getting my first full time employment and moving out of my parents' home), my income and insurance access are not always guaranteed. But if I am not able to get vaccinated and get very sick from COVID or Long COVID, I will be even less able to live independently and maintain my livelihood. Everyone should have easy access to updated vaccines that help keep us safe!

title: Comment from Jarman, Angela
comment:
Please allow all Americans to receive covid shots every 6 months so we can all have better immunity. We know immunity wanes quickly and by 6 months there's not much protection from infection, and every infection brings the risk of long covid with it.
6 month boosters should be free, if they were there'd be less acute illness, less long covid, and better worker productivity, and that's largely what the CDC and government care about now, right? Eventually widespread repeated illnesses DO harm the economy, so let's please be smart about this and make it easier, not harder, to protect ourselves from covid.

title: Comment from Fowler, Kristen
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. Restrictions and physician/pharmacist doubt over a person's eligibility, or even the legitimacy of an illness, is pervasive throughout healthcare and many individuals already jump over hurdles to get the treatment they need. I have had multiple doctors doubt my illness and the need for treatment in the past, and I don't want individuals to have to continue to fight against an ablest system. Vaccines twice a year are for the good of all.

People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025. Waning vaccine efficiency (occurring roughly four months after vaccination regardless of brand) most likely is the reason the US experienced such a high wave of COVID in summer 2024, that was preventable and vaccination twice a year could have eased that wave. There would be less strain on healthcare and the economy if fewer people had to take time off work, or worse become disabled with long COVID after an acute infection.

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

Not only should COVID vaccines be allowed twice a year, they should be affordable for all. Bring back the Bridge program and allow those under-insured and uninsured to receive vaccines. Low income groups are at higher risk of contracting acute COVID infections and need to be protected.

2. Support more frequent updates to the vaccines, adjusted for the latest variants.

If the CDC takes these actions, I would certainly jump at the opportunity to stay strongly vaccinated, and I would encourage my entire family to do so with me.

Thank you.

Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

comment:
