title: Comment from Smith, Emma
comment: The committee is anticipated to vote on the following topic on day 1 of the meeting (October 23): “Use of additional doses of COVID-19 vaccine in immunocompromised individuals and older adults following an initial dose of 2024–2025 vaccine." I have Long COVID. I am 26. Please allow everyone, all ages to be able to receive an updated COVID vaccine twice a year, and tailor them towards current variants.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: I have multiple medical conditions and factors that make me more susceptible to COVID. Studies have proved COVID is not seasonal like the flu and peaks multiple times throughout the year. I have already lost members of my family and others to COVID and know of others who have died as recently as one month ago. On behalf of myself, my family, my friends, and my community, I ask you to make sure updated COVID vaccines are available multiple times a year for all demographics. The harmful effects of COVID are felt by people of all ages, including children, teenagers, and young adults. Even so-called "healthy" individuals can suffer from long-term effects known as long COVID. Every single resident in the US - all ages, all health levels, all races, and ethnicities, genders, and sexual identities - needs easy access to multiple updated COVID vaccines each year to keep us safe from the harms of this virus. We are still in a pandemic - we need these protections!

title: Comment from Forge, Leona
comment: Please allow twice a year boosters for all ages. Vaccines are our best defense against endemic COVID. Protection clearly wanes over the course of a year and we can shore up our immunity with twice yearly boosters.

title: Comment from Randall, Alicia
comment:
Hi, I am a high risk individual and have been paying close attention to the science behind preventing and treating COVID, including Long COVID. Here are my thoughts about what we need to keep:
1. Everyone needs to be able to access these vaccines, they need to be universally available for 6 months and up.
2. Immunocompromised individuals need to continue to be allowed a COVID vaccine every 2 months.
3. We need to keep the 65+ additional dose vaccine recommendations.

In addition to these requirements I think we should add:
1. We need to update the COVID vaccine frequency to every 3-4 months to account for waning immunity.
2. We need to update the COVID vaccine formula every 6 months as opposed to once every year.
Due to the unchecked spread of COVID, we are allowing it to mutate and evolve exponentially faster, meaning that prior immunity is waning at ever faster rates as the virus moves sufficiently far away to reinfect individuals. As COVID is not seasonal, having a summer and winter wave at minimum, we need to utilize the speed of mRNA technology to update the vaccines at a faster rate to keep it in check. This may even be feasible with Novavax protein-based technology, while it will definitely be more difficult for them, it is possible within a 6-month timeframe.
3. On an every 6-month updated vaccine schedule, the COVID vaccine frequency should be set to every 3 months.

This will allow individuals the ability to receive an updated vaccine and a booster for each vaccine update at the 6-month intervals
I have personally had COVID twice in 6 weeks and have yet to recover from these infections. If we had access to vaccines more frequently and the vaccines were updated, it could help so many vulnerable people.

Thank you.

title: Comment from Ryland, Alison
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

I am writing in support of making the spring 2025 COVID vaccine recommended for people of all ages and health statuses. Research shows that COVID vaccines lose efficacy over time, so it's imperative that not only yearly, but twice-yearly vaccines be recommended for everyone. It's also important that each vaccine be updated for the latest variants of COVID, which has and will continue to change rapidly.

Thanks to the fall 2024 vaccine being open to everyone, I was able to get a vaccine without needing to prove that I was especially vulnerable. My sister was also able to get the new vaccine, which makes her substantially safer in her customer-facing job. Please continue making access to COVID vaccines recommended for everyone every 6 months, so we can keep protecting ourselves from COVID.

title: Comment from Serrano, Jesus
comment:
To whom it may concern,

My colleagues and I are desperate for a public health mandate that will protect us from any deadly airborne pathogens. Since a significant number of people are still dying from SARS-COVID 2, I would like to suggest an increase in immunity to the general public. Recent studies have shown that our current covid vaccines are not sterilizing vaccines and it may leave our immune system defenseless against severe illness from COVID infections after 3 months of inoculation. We need to exceed in research and manufacturing of these vaccines and we need to protect the public for more than 3 months in a year. Please, enforce a series of boosters to be administered through a trimester series per year. We also need to educated the public in airborne diseases and the benefits of respirators. You have to fund public health programs that encourage mask fitting education and clean air guidelines. Our future well being depends on government support.

Best,

A worried scientist

title: Comment from Letham, Sarah
comment:
Vaccines are widely known to be one of the best returns on investment in all of health spending. Really unparalleled in how little money has to buy vaccines compared to the savings in costs for sickness, lost work, long COVID treatment and care, and a constellation of other expenses. One of the elements of COVID management that the government got right was giving free access to vaccines for all, even those uninsured.

Now the situation is even more important. COVID is endemic, it isn't gone and it's not going away. Everyone, even people who can't afford vaccines, have to go out into the world and work and interact with other people. They deserve protection from the virus. We deserve a society in which even minimum wage service workers aren't getting sick with COVID multiple times per year.

While my partner and I of course mask when in public, unfortunately masking is not enough when we are the ONLY ones doing it in public spaces. We need layers of protection which is what access to these vaccines allows. Please do not restrict vaccination access. Please let those of us who WANT to be vaccinated take the vaccines.

title: Comment from Carney, Abby
comment: Because immunity wanes so quickly with the current COVID vaccines, it is necessary that all adults--regardless of immune status--have the opportunity to receive biannual (twice a year) vaccinations. This will allow a more robust immune response for recipients and aid in the decrease of COVID cases nationwide. Twice-a-year vaccinations would also allow the opportunity updated protections to new variants in a more timely manner. We've seen as the pandemic continues, lows are no longer that low, and people are getting infected repeatedly. With the constant threat of long COVID and other debilitating consequences of an infection, it is of utmost importance to prevent these infections in the first place through any means possible.

title: Comment from Stone, Elizabeth
comment: Please keep vaccines accessible biannually for all ages! The Covid pandemic was unprecedented for 3 generations of the world after the Influenze pandemic of 1918. But with 100 years of staggering medical, technological, and public health advancements, our ability to quickly create vaccines that can reduce the risk of death for millions was an amazing accomplishment. Unfortunately, I think too many people believe we are “one and done” with needing regular boosters. Popular opinions have started to veer away from the idea that vaccine induced herd immunity is the best way to protect our societies. Yet for the majority of people who do still want to keep everyone safe - pregnant, elderly, disabled, and immunocompromised - these people need to be given both the correct facts about the benefits of and also ready access to receive biannual boosters. This is the best way to actually utilize our amazing production of the vaccine and keep more individual people alive in the long term as our population adjusts to Covid becoming more like the flu over another generation or so. Even if the government no longer wants to push for mask usage or vaccine requirements, at the very least we should keep vaccines available to all who want them (and continue campaigns about the biannual benefits)! The United States should  be a beacon of proof to show how humanity has advanced since the 1918 pandemic. We should be working to regain the trust of the American people in vaccines, in science, and in our public health leaders. At the very least, that means continuing to provide vaccines so we can demonstrate their individual efficacy and keep people healthy so we can have a healthier, more stable population, economy, and democracy.

title: Comment from Walker, Karen
comment: As a longtime registered nurse and former infection control nurse it is critical that Covid vaccinations be offered twice per year in order to maintain at least adequate protection from severe illness and hospitalization if COVID-19 is contracted. It is imperative that our government and public health agencies try to safeguard and protect the health and functionality of the citizens of the United States so they are not debilitated by Covid in the form of long Covid or experience side effects from COVID-19  that lead to their demise.

title: Comment from Dixon, A.
comment:
I am immunocompromised after chemotherapy, and the COVID vaccine gives me good protection - until its efficacy falls off after several months. I need the vaccine twice per year so long as the vaccines are not highly protective for 12 months. Additionally, the COVID waves are not only happening in the winter, so without the spring vaccine, I would be at higher risk for the late summer/early fall widespread infections, such as we saw in 2024.

My family also needs to get the COVID vaccine twice per year so that they don't bring COVID home to me. Please recommend twice per year vaccines for anyone who needs one.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Allowing for easily accessible and free multiple COVID-19 vaccines allows for people with undiagnosed disabilities or people who are unable to receive treatment for their disabilities due to lack of access and a shortage of medical professionals to receive protection from a virus that more and more evidence shows may have lasting effects. This will also lessen the strain on already stressed and underpaid medical professionals (particularly nurses, techs, assistants, and paramedics), many of whom are still feeling the strain from the thousands of medical professionals who died during 2020 because of COVID-19 and the inability to refill those staff positions (mostly as a result from the greedy corporatization of healthcare). Easily accessible and free vaccines is a net benefit for society, public health, and the economy.

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

comment:

To whom it may concern:

I am writing as a loved one of several family members who are immunocompromised or struggling with Long COVID symptoms. I am also someone who cares deeply for my own health and the health of my community members, particularly those who are vulnerable and/or more significantly impacted by COVID-19.

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status in order to address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination as well as the emergence of new strains. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

Such a recommendation would likely also improve public awareness about the importance of recent vaccination to protect oneself (along with other interventions, like masking and ventilation) from COVID-19 and the possibility of Long COVID.

Additionally, the CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024, leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. I ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Riccio, Alexander
comment:
It is extremely important that the CDC, along with all the groups and committees that serve under the CDC umbrella, approve and endorse the maximum possible vaccination and boosting frequency for COVID vaccines.

There are many millions of Americans like me, who did not agree to live in a society where no less than one percent of Americans are actively infectious with COVID year round, reaching as high as ten percent during surges. Dr. Michael Hoerger computes those figures from wastewater data, though the CDC should have internal data to match.

We live every day with the reminders of a society that chose to rely *only* on vaccination to control a virus that mutates rapidly and for which we have no sterilizing vaccines. It is a society that places all the onus on us and our PPE to avoid exposure… and without much more frequent boosting and updating, denies us the backstop of adequate immunity.

With an accumulating mountain of data in the literature, I think it's non-controversial for me to remind them committee that all COVID infections, not just lethal, hospitalizing, or otherwise “severe” infections, cause an unacceptable proportion of long term negative health consequences. By CDC’s own press releases, the prevalence of all negative outcomes in the broader meaning is as high as 25% on the first infection. The Chief Science Officer of Canada has found that in Canada, after the majority of Canadians have had multiple infections, the total rate of Canadians having ever reported persisting health issues has risen to *37.9%*! (“Experiences of Canadians with long-term symptoms following COVID-19”).

These are extraordinary numbers. Thankfully, a smaller proportion of those reporting longer term negative outcomes are reporting truly disabling outcomes, but the number appears to be a few percent… still an unacceptable number. It's a number that's larger than the catastrophe of paralysis of polio infections.

The data does clearly show that many specific populations are at elevated risk for COVID and long COVID, yes! And yet, the general population is infected often enough to make them the largest group of long COVID sufferers. We lack the ability to predict which people develop negative outcomes, only guess. Guesswork makes for terrible policy.

The data that shows all infections, even mild and asymptomatic infections, pose a risk of disability and severe disruption to life, should be plenty reason on its own to err on the side of boosting and updating as frequently as possible to reduce the number of exposures that turn into infections.

That's the big picture.

My personal experience corroborates the importance of frequent boosting and updating.

My brush with COVID was way worse than I expected. I'm not in the official high risk group. I'm a 29 year old adult in acceptable, but not excellent, physical health. When I got infected in september 2023, it had been more than 8 months since my last booster. Which was the last booster that I'd been allowed to get. The implicit and explicit policy of the CDC that COVID should be treated like the flu, a seasonal virus, and boosted once a year, meant that most Americans who'd gone out and gotten a booster the prior fall had waning immunity by then. There was no plan to cover and protect us in the summer with vaccines.

The data showing that COVID cleverly evades cellular immunity in early stages of infection, and that antibody neutralization titers drops rapidly, was clear to me, but I felt the impacts myself. It was the sickest I'd ever been, and it left me unable to work for much of the next month.

Is this an acceptable outcome? Is this even a desirable outcome?

At this point in the pandemic, I have several friends who developed disabling long COVID symptoms despite being vaccinated, and having “mild” cases. I know of at least one person who suffered long COVID after an asymptomatic case. Most concerningly are the long COVID symptoms that only emerged after the second or third infection - which supports the worst case scenario where infection risks *compound*.

Some of these long COVID cases would have been avoided had their last layer of defense - their immune system - been prompted to present the best possible defense against their exposures turning into infections. Is this an outcome we can tolerate? I'd argue that for reasons like this it's a wildly negligent mistake to only focus on lethality, hospitalization, and even symptomaticity - it appears we need to take every step to reduce infection too.

I've heard reasonable concerns about frequent boosting causing some form of immune tolerance instead of propensity to clear. I'm not an immunologist! I wish I could understand all that “class switching” entailed. That said, I've also seen *some* data suggesting novavax causes fewer of these issues. But ACIP mustn't forget the destructive effect that frequent infection can have on our immune systems either!

title: Comment from Helms, Monica
comment:
I'm immunocompromised as well as on immunosuppressants. I have sometimes had a hard time convincing pharmacists that I need to be vaccinated every 6 months. My husband goes through great lengths to protect me, including taking pay cuts so he can have a work from home job to limit our possible exposure, (and we still wear N95's everywhere and the few visitors we have in our home have to wear masks) and he technically doesn't qualify to get vaccinated every 6 months: pharmacists simply question him less when he goes in for them so he has been able to get them so far.

It is vitally important for me to be able to continue living, a life I love very much, for anyone and everyone to be able to be vaccinated. If he were not able to work (have insurance and money, and I did have to self pay once already) or pharmacists start being less agreeable (which does seem to be happening,) then it could coat me my life. It has cost others their lives.

Please make vaccines accessible in every way possible to make sure my disability isn't needlessly deadly. Please help do it to help prevent disability and death in others. Please do it to help create a safer and more equitable world.

title: Comment from Hennessy, Paul
comment:
CDC must allow immunocompromised people to get a 2nd COVID vaccine 2 months after the first, no matter the vaccine. However, this language needs to be much more broad and liberalized. In fact, anyone should be allowed to get a 2nd dose after 2 months. It has been shown that a second dose is needed for the JN1 vaccine to create a more robust protection. While immunocompromised people need that 2nd dose, everyone is at risk for long term effects of COVID. And with so many immune systems damaged from COVID infections, an option for a second dose is necessary. Remove all barriers for access.

Additionally, the COVID vaccines every 6 months instead of yearly. By CDC's own admission, COVID spikes in the winter and summer and is active throughout the yearly. Focusing on only getting shots in the fall is a failed approach to protection. We need protection going into the summer as well. So twice yearly updated vaccine schedule will be more effective.

title: Comment from Smarmy, Joe
comment:
-Allow immunocompromised people to get a 2nd COVID vaccine 2 months after the first
-Broaden language to allow ANYONE to get a 2nd dose after 2 months. Shouldn't just be limited to the immunocompromised.
-Recommend COVID vaccines every 6 months instead of yearly

title: Comment from Henninfliegen, Mindy
comment:
Three things must be accomplished at this upcoming meeting:

1. Allow immunocompromised people to get a 2nd COVID vaccine 2 months after the first. Regardless of vaccine preference, all vaccines should have this option.
2. Broaden language to allow ANYONE to get a 2nd dose after 2 months. Don't just limit it to immunocompromised. Everyone benefits from a 2nd dose.
3. Recommend COVID vaccines every 6 months instead of yearly. Doing it in the fall only is stupid and neglects summer surges.

title: Comment from Mckinley, Anne
comment:
Insisting that Covid is like flu and only needs one shot per year, focusing on the young and the old, is madness. We need to trust the science and offer the vaccine to ALL AGES AT LEAST TWICE PER YEAR. Some people made need more doses, and all should have the opportunity to take a vaccine. Why restrict lifesaving care?

My own example is instructive. I got covid at a well ventilated outdoor event exactly 5 months after receiving a covid booster…just as it was waning. Some studies show a drop off after only 3-4 months. We should do everything in our power to help people, and to make vaccines accessible. This will help not only with saving lives but with vaccine acceptance.

title: Comment from Corvix, Ed
comment: Make 2nd dose COVID vaccine 2 months after the first available to everyone, not just immunocompromised. Additionally, covid vaccines should be every 6 months with updated vaccines instead of once per year in the fall.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: As an older person, I have gotten every single Covid vaccine available at three month intervals. With a number of medical issues, this has kept me safe  from Covid for over five years. Please allow anyone who has medical issues or other issues like age to receive a vaccine every three months.

title: Comment from Hill, Maxx
comment:
Hi, my name is Maxx. I am an educator and graphic designer, and I possess no conflicts of interest.

With regards to vaccines, we need to ensure the following continue:
1) Everyone is able to access all vaccines, especially for Coronavirus, and that they be universally available for no cost to the patient, for ages 6 months and up.
2) Immunocompromised people are able to receive coronavirus vaccines every 2 months
3) Individuals 65 years and up are recommended to also receive additional doses

In addition to these policies already in place, we need to the following additional requirements:

1. The coronavirus vaccine formula needs to be updated every 6 months with widespread information campaigns providing accurate and updated information.
Because of the unmitigated spread, the coronavirus is spreading and mutating at a rapid rate. This leads to a waning efficacy of prior vaccinations to the currently-circulating strains. COVID is no seasonal and never has been. We have the technology with mRNA vaccines, to update the available vaccines with a greater frequency to closer match the viruses currently in the population. This may even be possible for the protein-based novavax vaccine. Additionally - the updates need to continue to be at no cost, and the computer systems for pharmacies providing the vaccines need to be kept up to date so patients are not charged out of pocket for something that should be for free. This has been a serious issue in the latest vaccine roll out.

2) We need to update frequency average people are recommended to receive the vaccine to every 3-4 months.
This frequency, up from every 12 months, is reflective of what science is showing us about the efficacy of the vaccines: the active immunity completely wanes within 3-4 months. Memory immunity is not enough to slow the spread of this disease. People, especially doctors, also need to be informed of the updated recommendations and encouraged to make sure all patients receive their updated vaccines regularly.

3) If we increase the frequency the vaccine formula is updated to every 6 months, the recommendation for receiving the vaccine should be updated to every 3 months.

4) Permit all immunocompromised people, not just those who are moderately or severely immunocompromised, to receive additional coronavirus vaccines to boost their immunity more frequently.
Even those who are mildly immunocompromised are still immunocompromised and still need additional protection.

5) Allow those with low BMI ("underweight", under 18.5) and high BMI ("overweight" and "obese", greater than or equal to 30), even if not otherwise immunocompromised, to receive additional doses of the coronavirus vaccine.
This is based on the data we have showing the vaccine effectiveness in those with low or high BMI wanes faster than those within the "normal" BMI range. The shortened efficacy windows mean these people need to receive the vaccine at a higher frequency to account for the faster wane in effectiveness.

6) Allow and encourage people who are in their third trimester of pregnancy to receive an additional dose of the coronavirus vaccine.
Because newborns until the age of 6 months are not able to vaccinated for coronavirus, the only protection these babies receive is from the vaccine-caused antibodies passing through to them while in utero.

7) More funding, support, and resources need to be provided to those developing nasal-spray vaccines that are showing effectiveness in actually preventing infection. We need these vaccines out, to the people, at no cost, as widely as possible, as soon as possible.

7) An extensive public information campaign needs to be created to combat the serious misinformation that is circulating about coronavirus, immunity, and vaccine efficacy:
- Current coronavirus vaccines do not prevent infection.
- Vaccines can help prevent people from experiencing severe acute illness
- By severe acute illness, it means that less people end up on a ventilator
- Vaccines do not effectively prevent long COVID
- Vaccines do not cause autism
- Autism is not worth risking a child's life to prevent anyway
- Immunity debt does not explain why everyone is getting sicker more often
- Coronavirus damages the immune system, the brain, blood vessels, and dramatically increases the likelihood of severe illness and death in a variety of ways.

comment: Please ensure vaccines are available to everyone twice a year (or more). Thank you.

comment: Please allow anyone to get a second vaccine 2 months after the first dose…  especially when starting a primary series or when there is a new parent family shift in a variant, or when an individual feels they need it (perhaps someone with IgA deficiency or hasn’t had a vax in a while). Novavax recommends a dose 2 month series in some situations. Please make this easier to get by stating it as policy and informing pharmacist. This would be a big help! Thanks a bunch.

title: Comment from Chase, Katherine
comment:
I am writing in regards to the dosing schedule for COVID vaccines. At this point we have ample evidence that antibody titers in immunocompetent people induced by vaccination decline within a period of several months (see Bates JT, Lirette ST. et al. 2024 and Dieckhaus KD, Kim MJ. et al. 2023). We also know that, unlike influenza, SARS-CoV-2 circulates year round and, because of this continued circulation, new variants emerge frequently. In this context, it does not make sense to recommend only annual dosing for COVID vaccines the way we do with flu shots.

mRNA vaccines in particular offer an exciting opportunity because they can be rapidly updated to better match currently circulating variants. However, they are not living up to their full potential when being updated only once a year. SARS-CoV-2 mutates very rapidly and a more closely matched antigen in a vaccine can make a big difference in neutralizing antibody titers.

COVID vaccine uptake is already poor because of widespread distrust sown by misinformation and disinformation propagated mainly through social media. Immunocompetent people like myself who do opt to get the yearly recommended dose of a COVID vaccine tend to be more concerned about COVID than the rest of the general population. Many of us would absolutely get a second (or even third) dose in a year if it were available to us.

Better matched vaccines not only help prevent severe disease, hospitalization, and death, they can decrease the likelihood of symptomatic infection (see the Feb 1st, 2024 MMWR which found a 54% decrease in symptomatic infection among people who received the XBB.1.5 based vaccine). They may not prevent symptomatic infection to the degree that immunizations like the measles vaccine do but, when if we were talking about a flu vaccine that was 54% effective, that would be considered an excellent year by our current standards.

Anything we can do to blunt the transmission and reduce the disease burden of SARS-CoV-2 is important. COVID has killed over 1.2 million people in the US alone and millions of people are estimated to be suffering from the long-term effects of infection. Data from the CDC and NIH has conclusively established a link between COVID and potential sequelae like thromboembolism, diabetes, and cognitive decline. With the massive number of infections that we are seeing on an annual basis, preventing even a relatively small percentage of people from experiencing this outcomes could make a very large difference in our healthcare system and society overall.

In light of all of this, I again ask that you reconsider the decision to recommend only once yearly doses of the COVID vaccine to immunocompetent adults. Thank you for your time.

title: Comment from Scott, Charles
comment: I seriously ask you to make a minimum of two Covid vaccine shots available for people each year. My condition requires three shots every year and I have not been infected by the virus so far.

title: Comment from Davis, Jordan
comment: I strongly urge the CDC to ensure that COVID-19 vaccines be free for all individuals. Vaccines are a critical public health measure that help prevent severe illness, reduce the spread of the virus, and protect vulnerable communities. Removing financial barriers will ensure equitable access, especially for low-income and uninsured populations, and support ongoing efforts to control the pandemic. Maintaining free access to COVID-19 vaccines is essential for public safety and the health of our nation.

title: Comment from Bruce, Brittany
comment:

It’s abysmal health practice to only advertise vaccines as a safety measure for covid, and then deny access to the most vulnerable.

All vaccines, including COVID-19, should be free to access for all at least every 6 months.

title: Comment from Knight, Gabrielle
comment: Please give people the choice to get a COVID vaccine twice a year.  The vaccine's effectiveness at helping to prevent infection wanes after a few months and a year between vaccine boosters leaves people open to system-wide infection and possibly being burdened with long term health issues as a result.

title: Comment from Pujol, Sully
comment: The CDC must support more frequent updates to the vaccines, adjusted for the latest variants and make them available to all ages twice a year!!

title: Comment from Ford, Lee
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

I am writing to you as someone diagnosed with and disabled by Long Covid since June 2021. As an immunocompromised person, I depend on access to vaccines to protect myself, and I want that protection for others to help prevent or reduce their risk of developing Long Covid.
COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

References:
1. Link-Gelles R. Effectiveness of COVID-19 (2023-2024 Formula) vaccines. Presented at: FDA VRBPAC Meeting; June 5, 2024. Accessed June 12, 2024. https://www.fda.gov/media/179140/download

2. Wu N, Joyal-Desmarais K, Vieira AM, et al. COVID-19 boosters versus primary series: update to a living review. The Lancet Respiratory Medicine. 2023;11(10):e87-e88. doi:10.1016/S2213-2600(23)00265-5

3. Menegale F, Manica M, Zardini A, et al. Evaluation of Waning of SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine–Induced Immunity: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. JAMA Netw Open. 2023;6(5):e2310650. doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2023.10650

4. Nguyen DC, Hentenaar IT, Morrison-Porter A, et al. SARS-CoV-2-specific plasma cells are not durably established in the bone marrow long-lived compartment after mRNA vaccination. Nat Med. Published online September 27, 2024:1-10. doi:10.1038/s41591-024-03278-y

5. Fang Z, Ahrnsbrak R, Rekito A. Evidence Mounts That About 7% of US Adults Have Had Long COVID. JAMA. Published online June 7, 2024. doi:10.1001/jama.2024.11370

title: Comment from Davis, Sarah
comment: This constant COVID spread is unbearable. Please take serious action. Vaccine efficacy wanes significantly four to six months following vaccination (according to the FDA) making updated vaccination important for all people, as COVID continues to spread in our communities and across all states. Vaccine approaches that restrict access based on age or risk status put all of us at risk and leave those at high risk of severe consequences of COVID infection confused about whether they qualify to receive additional doses. These high risk patients may also face barriers as vaccine providers misunderstand the guidelines. A more frequent vaccination approach providing vaccination at least every six months as well as frequent updates to match current variants is needed to better protect all of us amid year-round COVID spread. The amount of COVID spreading is insane, and people aren't even aware of it because constant illness has been so normalized, and COVID has been minimized as just a mild respiratory illness, not a vascular disease. COVID needs to be taken as seriously as HIV. A thousand people per week are dying of COVID just in the US.

comment: Major economic benefits to the work-force being less sick for less time and infecting less people through coughing and sneezing. also major benefits to less of the workforce being disabled via long covid, rates of POTS (postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome) have DOUBLED since covid and this severe disability prevents people from working, shopping etc. i personally don't care as much about the economy as the people, but if you need a reason for people with little care for others, then money tends to be a good one. how many of you see your coworkers, classmates, family members getting two-week long 'colds'? have you really convinced yourself that it isn't covid? have they even tested for covid? and even when it isn't covid, how many infections happen because covid is constantly weakening peoples immune systems?  we need vaccines up to twice a year if not more. we need to keep up with these strains. covid is still killing and disabling people. think like a scientist, look at data beyond mortality rates.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. As a newly high-risk person, most likely because of a COVID infection, I have been constantly fearing getting COVID again and worrying about if/when I will have access to another vaccine before the protection wanes. Instating restrictions regarding vulnerability does not maintain access for all high risk people, because not everyone has documentation of their conditions—especially with something like long COVID, which we are still learning about, and which many people may have gotten from infections that they didn’t test for or only tested for on a home test. When I tested positive I requested to get a PCR so that I had concrete proof in case I had long term symptoms, and then I was denied the PCR. I only have an at-home test result, and since I am dealing with fatigue and other illness, am a student and worker, and am occupied with staying afloat and being able to get out of bed everyday, I worry that I will not be able to go through bureaucratic processes in time to access another booster soon enough to prevent me from worsening. I am not a unique case. Not all high-risk people have the necessary proof, and the most marginalized of those high-risk people often don’t. Those of us who cannot withstand all of the barriers that will be instated by making “vulnerability” a requirement will become even more vulnerable, especially as the Bridge Access Program has ended [7], and our blood will be on your hands. Additionally, COVID vaccine efficacy wanes over months [1-3] for all people of all ages and health statuses due to new strains emerging [4]. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6]. If I—completely healthy pre-COVID—had gotten a vaccine in the spring of this year, who knows if I (and many others) would have gotten a summer COVID infection (and consequent Long COVID) that has ruined my life and health. I am 19 years old and I am fatigued every single day. Doctors tell me I have Long COVID with no plan or course of action to treat it. I am in pain as I write this. I have been dealing with non-stop bodily (opportunistic) infections of all kinds since June, and this is what I deal with as someone with great access to health care—which many don’t have. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024, leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
We've been told for years to get a flu shot every year in the fall before flu season ramps up. Protection wanes after six months, which is usually enough to get through flu season.

With COVID being a year-round illness, unlike the flu, it only makes sense to get vaccinated against it more than once a year. Getting a shot in October to last six months of effectiveness followed by another shot in April to provide another six months of effectiveness would provide the best protection for everyone.

And open up the eligibility to everyone, not just those who are older or immunocompromised. Younger people get sick more often and can easily spread it to others.

comment: I'm writing to ask that you provide covid vaccine access to all individuals at least twice a year. With each new study that comes out, we see how dangerous covid is to our bodies and how long it can linger and cause deadly health problems. Everyone (young, old, immunocompromised and not) deserves the highest protections we can offer.

title: Comment from Wright, Richard
comment:
You know the serious dangers of one let alone multiple covid infections.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

References:
1. Link-Gelles R. Effectiveness of COVID-19 (2023-2024 Formula) vaccines. Presented at: FDA VRBPAC Meeting; June 5, 2024. Accessed June 12, 2024. https://www.fda.gov/media/179140/download

2. Wu N, Joyal-Desmarais K, Vieira AM, et al. COVID-19 boosters versus primary series: update to a living review. The Lancet Respiratory Medicine. 2023;11(10):e87-e88. doi:10.1016/S2213-2600(23)00265-5

3. Menegale F, Manica M, Zardini A, et al. Evaluation of Waning of SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine–Induced Immunity: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. JAMA Netw Open. 2023;6(5):e2310650. doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2023.10650

4. Nguyen DC, Hentenaar IT, Morrison-Porter A, et al. SARS-CoV-2-specific plasma cells are not durably established in the bone marrow long-lived compartment after mRNA vaccination. Nat Med. Published online September 27, 2024:1-10. doi:10.1038/s41591-024-03278-y

5. Fang Z, Ahrnsbrak R, Rekito A. Evidence Mounts That About 7% of US Adults Have Had Long COVID. JAMA. Published online June 7, 2024. doi:10.1001/jama.2024.11370

6. Yousaf AR. Notes from the Field: Surveillance for Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in Children — United States, 2023. MMWR Morb Mortal Wkly Rep. 2024;73. doi:10.15585/mmwr.mm7310a2

7. https://www.cdc.gov/vaccines/programs/bridge/index.html

title: Comment from G, Eli
comment: We know that COVID-19 is absolutely not a seasonal disease; spikes in COVID-19 infections regularly happen in the summer and during seasons where people tend to get together. We cannot treat COVID-19 and its vaccinations like other seasonal respiratory diseases and ignore its impact outside of respiratory disease season. Treating it as such only serves to encourage genetic mutations and prolongs the cycles of ravages communities face in the wake of COVID-19 spreader events. COVID-19 vaccinations needs to be available year-round! Vaccines also need to be available at least twice a year - if not more - to accommodate the multiple infection spikes a year.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: I strongly recommend that the CDC allows for at least two COVID vaccine doses each year for all ages and abilities. While disabled, elderly, and immunocompromised individuals may be at higher risk for complications from a COVID infection, we know that everyone is at substantial risk for Long COVID and other complications, regardless of ability status. comment:

People wouldn't be having neurological issues from being exposed to COVID multiple times. People would feel safe visiting their families during holidays or birthdays.

This is coming from someone that is surrounded with friends and family members that are immunocompromised and wants them to feel safe going outside to go shopping for food and necessities.

Disability statistics are skyrocketing and this BSL-3 level pathogen is destroying peoples immune systems. Look at the data! At absolute minimum we should have the same number of opportunities to vaccinate as we have waves - so 2 to 3 times a year. These vaccines need to be free for everyone! The FDA is failing over and over again to protect our citizens. Do your job. And do no harm.

comment: Please approve an updated vaccine recommendation schedule of every 2-4 months for immunocompromised and high risk individuals. For the general population, COVID boosters once a year are not enough. Please change the recommendation to every six months.

comment: We need Covid vaccines twice a year for people of all ages! What’s the plan here otherwise, for everyone to just get Covid over and over again until we’ve all got long covid? Ridiculous.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: It is vital that we allow for all individuals to receive an additional dose of the 2024-2025 formulation SARS-CoV-2 is they want them. Not doing so would discriminate against immunocompromised individuals that need the second dose to reach the protection granted to immune component individuals in a single dose. The vaccine needs be available twice a year anyway as shown by the waning protection in the mRNA vaccines. Please vote yes to allow allow immunocompromised people to get a second SARS-CoV-2 vaccine two months after the first dose. Also, combination flu and SARS-CoV-2 vaccines are not a good idea and we should stick to single type as it allows the product to be more versatile. I am glad to see that CMV vaccines are being discussed at this meeting as CMV is oncogenic and we shouldn't have unmitigated spread of that virus.

title: Comment from Smith, Bec
comment: Having a twice-yearly COVID vaccine available to EVERYONE is essential, regardless of their income level or health insurance status. Making this vaccine free and accessible at pharmacies is a key part of a larger layered precautionary public health plan against COVID reinfections during the winter and summer spikes each year. Research has shown that the effectiveness of a once-yearly COVID vaccine begins to fade after a few months, and markedly so at the 6 month mark. Increase the vaccine to twice yearly and make it accessible to the public, change people's lives for the better.

title: Comment from Decker, Lindsey
comment:
Hello. I am writing as an individual whose career as university faculty was derailed by long COVID to urge the committee to adopt a more frequent vaccination schedule for COVID-19. Given that COVID-19 is not a seasonal illness, proven by the recurrence of summer waves across several years now, it is only reasonable to adopt a twice-yearly vaccination schedule that allows those who choose to protect themselves from severe disease to do so for more than just a few months a year (given that the vaccines are most effective within three months of administration and that they are only thought to protect against severe disease for up to six months). While the incidence of long COVID is not a decided question, and may differ from variant to variant, the implication of ongoing research is that COVID-19 can damage the body with each infection, potentially meaning that one's odds of developing some type of chronic condition that can fall under the long COVID umbrella increases with each infection. There are no real protocols for treating, let alone reversing the damage done by, long COVID beyond pacing and limiting activity to fit one's energy envelope, and until there are effective treatment protocols for the worst and most common symptoms (neurological, pulmonary, autonomic, etc.), more must be done to allow people to protect themselves. The CDC often emphasizes that we have the tools to mitigate COVID-19 — the vaccines are one of those tools, and one that the CDC could begin to make even more effective by recommending a twice-yearly schedule timed to help mitigate both the summer and winter waves.
Thank you for your time,
Lindsey Decker

title: Comment from Schneider, Erik
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

I am 62 years old and already completely disabled from neurological problems that are related to tardive dystonia but are not usually directly associated with it. Since it is unclear to me and to the medical community exactly what is happening in my body and why, I have voluntarily cut down on casual social contact since the beginning of the pandemic in order to keep myself as healthy as I can--a challenging effort given the extreme nonchalance of Federal public health policy regarding the severity of COVID and its possible ongoing effects on multiple body systems. I have no way of knowing whether my disability would be made worse by a COVID infection, and I do not wish to find out by trial and error.

My doctors agree that I should get a six-month booster if it is available to me, but the decision is not theirs: it is yours. In the Spring of 2022 I was denied a six-month vaccine and told that my disability was "not eligible". And so I have been following the guidelines you all issue around vaccines and severely curtailing my indoor social life despite that being not particularly good for my emotional well-being.

Please allow those of us who take this pandemic seriously to protect ourselves as we and our medical teams see fit.

title: Comment from Rose, Vivian
comment: Having a twice-yearly COVID vaccine available to EVERYONE is essential, regardless of their income level or health insurance status. Making this vaccine free and accessible at pharmacies is a key part of a larger layered precautionary public health plan against COVID reinfections during the winter and summer spikes each year. Research has shown that the effectiveness of a once-yearly COVID vaccine begins to fade after a few months, and markedly so at the 6 month mark. Increase the vaccine to twice yearly and make it accessible to the public, change people's lives for the better.

title: Comment from Hopson, Gunes
comment:
COVID vaccination at least twice a year should be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. People of all ages should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

References:

title: Comment from Keim, Stephanie
comment:
I think everyone will agree that to maximize the effectiveness of vaccines, their administration needs to be timed correctly - to when the virus is most prevalent. This is why flu vaccines are timed the way they are, why certain vaccines are only given if you are traveling to certain areas, and why vaccines are given after exposure in some cases. We also know that certain vaccines have waning protection over a period of months, so the timing of administration to peak illness activity is important.

Knowing all that, we need to change our model and optimize the timing of covid vaccines with times that covid peaks. Over the past 4 (almost 5) years, we have seen that covid peaks multiple times throughout the year. It does not mimic influenza which only has a single winter peak. So why are we basing the timing of covid vaccines on the influenza model and only providing a single annual shot to the greater US population? We know that covid vaccine protection wanes over a period of months, so a fall vaccine will not protect people for the spring/summer wave of covid. Furthermore, the fall vaccine is available after schools begin and after we are well into a covid surge. This timing leaves children, who are required to be in school, at a high risk of getting covid and transmitting it to their families and throughout the community. We need to give all ages and all people the opportunity to have vaccine protection twice a year instead of once so that they are better aligned with actual covid peaks.

Vaccines are currently one of the only ways people can attempt to prevent a covid infection, reduce the risk of long covid, and decrease the transmission of covid especially when there are no other community protections in place like masking or improved indoor ventilation and filtration.

The data on vaccine effectiveness, on covid peak activity, and (the lack of) public health mitigation measures necessitate two shots a year for all people of all ages. Please review the timing of covid vaccines so that people are protected before surges and allow people to get two covid vaccines a year.

title: Comment from Petersen, Rachel
comment: Please provide COVID vaccines twice a year to protect against summer and winter waves!

title: Comment from McGovern, Peter
comment:
Hello. I am over 70 years old with left-over cirrhosis from cured-hepatitis C. I am also a liver cancer survivor.
I had hepatitis C for decades and it slowly destroyed my liver leaving me with cirrhosis and liver cancer. Although my illness was cured with drugs, I am still disabled.

The COVID vaccines have been a lifeline for me allowing me to get dental work done and to see medical specialists about all the little things that my condition has gifted me with (metabolic disorders, inflammation, tooth and gum issues, arthritis like symptoms, and my adhd is getting worse.

I have been able to get them about every 5-6 months and need to continue as they wane to insignificance between 3-4 months after receiving them.

The more people vaccinated and the more often, the safer I am.

So I respectfully urge you to allow people over 55 and/or with co-morbid conditions to get Covid vaccines on a four-month basis as needed.

Note: in addition to vaccines, I mask with fit-tested n95 or better masks, use nasal spray and mouthwash, wear a face shield, use a personal laminar flow device, and quarantine away from people whenever I can. I have not interacted normally with any family or friends in about five years.

Vaccines play an important role in this.

I would also urge you to consider a more frequent update to the spike protein in the vaccine to be more in line with currently circulating variants.

Than you for your consideration of this important comment. It’s policies like this that save lives, with very little economic consequences.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: To maintain any kind of optimal public health status we need to have frequent vaccine access for COVID. I've seen what multiple cases of COVID can do to a regular healthy human body and we're fast tracking ourselves to a bleak future. Please give us, the public, one tool to protect ourselves. Make vaccines widely available and free.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:

title: Comment from Bilodeau, Brittany
comment:
It will soon be five years of the COVID pandemic. In the time since, scientific and medical researchers have learned a lot about the impact of COVID on the body with so much yet to uncover with each discovery that is unraveled.

We know COVID is airborne (1). We know it is not a seasonal virus (2). We know, as Anthony Fauci stated in a recent interview, that herd immunity cannot be achieved with a virus like COVID because of rapidly changing variants as well as vaccines that do not ensure lasting protection, and as such, immunity from the virus wanes (3). Based on your meeting in April 2023, the FDA’s data for the mRNA vaccines showed immunity from the vaccine waned to a great degree after 3-4 months (4). Studies have found the vaccines - both mRNA and protein - can reduce the likelihood of a severe case (5). With the same study also finding that Novavax (the protein vaccine) offered the largest reduction in viral load, of all of the vaccines they studied (5).

A recent study found even after having multiple vaccines for COVID, people did not have any cells that produced anti-bodies to protect them from the virus which could be why immunity wanes so quickly (6).

The vaccines we have now do at least help to prevent severe cases of acute COVID which becomes all the more important the more that we learn about the negative long-term impact of contracting it, especially as more and more evidence indicates that is harmful (8) and it's impacts are long reaching as it is a vascular and neurological virus (7).

Knowing how precarious immunity from the vaccines is, it’s imperative that you recommend COVID vaccines for people of all ages regardless of ones health status at least twice a year, with access to all for spring vaccines or other seasonal boosters. Please also recommend the frequent updating of boosters to match the wide array of variants that come about. We are only as healthy as our collective society is. We owe it to each other to keep each other safe.

Thank you for your time in this matter.

At this point there have been surges every winter AND summer!  Please help us prepare for them and avoid severe illness.

title: Comment from Gibson, Rowan
comment:
COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. I personally am lucky to have a job that allows me access to medical care and is pays well enough that I can afford any copays, but this is not true of every person I know, nor every American. Restricting access to COVID vaccines will disproportionately affect vulnerable and marginalized populations whose particular social determinants of health lead to higher percentages of illness and other medical conditions.

With all this in mind, I urge you to please advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Please vote to approve the “Use of additional doses of COVID-19 vaccine in immunocompromised individuals and older adults following an initial dose of 2024–2025 vaccine”.

My family of three was COVID-free until late-September 2024 when my spouse and I were first infected. We are both in higher-risk groups and have been taking precautions beyond vaccinations (e.g., N95 masking in public spaces, additional air filtration in our home, significantly limiting large group interactions, serial testing when exposed), for more than 4 ½ years. It was clear to us that these measures were necessary after the death of my father in June 2020 from COVID-19 complications.

It has taken almost a month for both of us to recover fully despite beginning a 5-day Paxlovid course at the onset of our symptoms. It remains to be seen whether either of us experience long-COVID, an increasingly likely occurrence.

The fact that Coronavirus infections are not seasonal and that mutations are rapid requires more frequent availability of updated vaccines. Likewise, vaccines should not be restricted only to high risk groups and should be encouraged for all age groups regardless of health status. This is a key component of ensuring a healthier overall population. Specifically:

•COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

•The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

•The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

•The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access.

comment:

comment: COVID vaccinations should be available at least twice a year for everyone, regardless of health or age. If the science shows that immunity wanes after 6 months, then we should all be allowed to protect ourselves and each other with twice yearly vaccinations. Anything less would leave my household at risk. A twice yearly vaccine would also allow for more frequent updates based on which variants are circulating. Everyone, regardless of age or health status, deserves to maximize our protection from COVID with an updated vaccine in Spring 2025.

title: Comment from O'Neill, Elyse

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:

It is well known that:
(1) Vaccine immunity wanes considerably after a few months and is close to zero 6 months out (at least for MRNA).
(2) Covid is not seasonal. It has major waves in all four seasons.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Limiting vaccine access would have significant negative consequences myself and my community.

For many, especially those with underlying health conditions that may not be officially classified as “high risk,” access to vaccines is essential for maintaining their health and well-being. Restricting eligibility would reduce their ability to protect themselves and those around them, particularly in communities with lower vaccination rates where the risk of transmission remains higher.

Expanding eligibility would ensure that everyone who needs a vaccine has a straightforward path to access it.

I am concerned that out-of-pocket costs could become a barrier to receiving the latest vaccines. I have close personal friends who are low-income and cannot afford high vaccine costs, which prevents them from receiving necessary protection. For many individuals, including those who are not classified as high-risk but still need protection, the cost could deter them from getting vaccinated. Providing broad eligibility and affordable access ensures that more people can protect themselves and contribute to community health.

Thank you for considering these perspectives as you make decisions regarding vaccine eligibility. I urge the CDC to prioritize accessibility for all individuals who may benefit from vaccination.

title: Comment from P, Em
comment: COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status.The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. title: Comment from Klemow, Corey
comment:
I am writing in support of making COVID vaccine boosters available to all at least twice a year, if not every 3-4 months. There is evidence that the neutralizing antibodies that are stimulated by a booster are key in helping to prevent long COVID in breakthrough infections - and those are the antibodies that wane after somewhere around 3-6 months. The CDC's own data show that long COVID is prevalent and on the rise (6.8% of the US population in March; 7.5% in June); allowing more frequent boosting could cut down these numbers. Long COVID is debilitating for many who have it, and takes them out of both the workforce and their own social lives and we should be doing more to combat it.

It makes no sense to limit most of the population to just one booster a year when the science clearly indicates more than once a year is best for all. At the very least let those of us willing to get multiple boosters a year do so. And changing your guidance will also to some extent impact behavior, leading to better public health outcomes for all.

title: Comment from Appelbaum, Ally
comment: Covid-19 continues to be a life-threatening infection that can permanently disable even young, healthy individuals. We know that vaccines help to reduce the likelihood of contracting covid and reduce the severity of the illness. We need regular and free access to vaccines for public health

title: Comment from Caine, Sarah
comment: I’m writing to let you know offering twice-year updated vaccines is the only way to responsibly approach public health in the face of all the science denial regarding COVID. This allows those who take public health seriously and those at higher risk for complications to pursue protections for themselves and gives everyone a better chance at not getting sick with and illness that has potentially lifelong implications.

title: Comment from Mercurio, Lara
comment:
I just spent the last three months being the only one wearing a mask in most places. I am now trying to guess at the best time to get my COVID booster to minimize my chances of catching COVID yet again this winter; I am worried that my constant state of exhaustion is one of several symptoms of a (mercifully very light--for now!) case of long covid.

The science is clear: the effectiveness of the vaccines wanes well before 12 months. We also have so many people suffering from extreme cases of long-covid, and struggling to find doctors who know enough to provide any sort of meaningful assistance.

It's hard to fathom why anyone would support NOT making updated boosters available at least every 6 months to ALL people. Huge swaths of this country were sitting at the highest levels of COVID in our wastewater for an extended period of time this summer. There were several weeks where the official count listed over 800 people dying of COVID in a single week; I have heard other reputable sources put those numbers at over a thousand. And the estimate for Long COVID cases is still (AFAIK) at 10% for every new COVID infection. There is no need to put people at risk in these ways. Please make updated vaccines available to everyone at least twice a year, to help prevent the widespread infection rates (including death and Long COVID) that we saw this year.

title: Comment from Edbrooke, Chloe
comment: We need access to Covid vaccines twice a year for all ages. The pandemic is not over. Also Novavax access is crucial.

title: Comment from K, K
comment:
Considering the extremely high rates of COVID and the general lack of public health protections, the following are necessary to protect immunocompromised individuals:
We must allow immunocompromised people to get a 2nd COVID vaccine 2 months after the first,
We must broaden language to allow ANYONE to get a 2nd dose after 2 months, especially considering a single COVID can make someone immunocompromised, even if they are not aware of it, and
We MUST recommend COVID vaccines every 6 months instead of yearly.

COVID is not a seasonal virus. There is no data to support it being seasonal or anything less than in a pandemic stage using the epidemiological definition, and the World Health Organization (WHO) agrees. To protect people, these are the things we must do, and anything short of that is wrong.

title: Comment from Anonymous

We can no longer treat this virus as a seasonal respiratory virus. Please mandate a release of updated vaccines every 6 months to be available in April/May and September/October annually. This would allow people to boost before the known highest waves of the year. Thank you.

title: Comment from Anonymous
