title: Comment from Scott, Justin
comment:
I am writing to strongly support a more rapid update and availability schedule for the Covid-19 vaccines. It has long been established that the current vaccines' efficacies wane in 3-6 months, not to mention the fact Covid variants continue to mutate and reappear far faster than the evolution of the vaccines. The major promise/selling point of MRNA was that they could be rapidly adapted. However, the current schedule hasn't followed this promise. We must have faster updates to represent the reality of the current strains (not the late 2023 strain finally being available in late 2024, etc!) and a more frequent schedule to have access. Obviously it is not a silver bullet, and won't end the pandemic at this point, but anything that can be done to help people protect themselves is critical. The oft quoted "We have the tools" has become a sick joke in an era of yearly outdated boosters.

I, and others in my family, are high risk. Many decisions, especially about exposures in medical or dental offices, have to be weighed against current outbreaks, wastewater levels, vaccination timelines, etc. A more rapid update schedule, and availability twice a year, would go a long way to helping make these timing decisions less difficult. It would benefit the most vulnerable in society greatly.

title: Comment from Funk, Sadie
comment:
COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. As someone with an immunocompromised mother who is not immunocompromised themself, it is very important to me to be able to receive the vaccine to be able to see her. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

References:
1. Link-Gelles R. Effectiveness of COVID-19 (2023-2024 Formula) vaccines. Presented at: FDA VRBPAC Meeting; June 5, 2024. Accessed June 12, 2024. https://www.fda.gov/media/179140/download

2. Wu N, Joyal-Desmarais K, Vieira AM, et al. COVID-19 boosters versus primary series: update to a living review. The Lancet Respiratory Medicine. 2023;11(10):e87-e88. doi:10.1016/S2213-2600(23)00265-5

3. Menegale F, Manica M, Zardini A, et al. Evaluation of Waning of SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine–Induced Immunity: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. JAMA Netw Open. 2023;6(5):e2310650. doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2023.10650

4. Nguyen DC, Hentenaar IT, Morrison-Porter A, et al. SARS-CoV-2-specific plasma cells are not durably established in the bone marrow long-lived compartment after mRNA vaccination. Nat Med. Published online September 27, 2024:1-10. doi:10.1038/s41591-024-03278-y

5. Fang Z, Ahrnsbrak R, Rekito A. Evidence Mounts That About 7% of US Adults Have Had Long COVID. JAMA. Published online June 7, 2024. doi:10.1001/jama.2024.11370

6. Yousaf AR. Notes from the Field: Surveillance for Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in Children — United States, 2023. MMWR Morb Mortal Wkly Rep. 2024;73. doi:10.15585/mmwr.mm7310a2

7. https://www.cdc.gov/vaccines/programs/bridge/index.html

title: Comment from Pool, Hannah
comment:
COVID vaccination must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status, at least twice a year (making Spring and Fall vaccinations recommended for everyone). Putting restrictions on eligibility creates barriers and discourages far too many from getting necessary boosters. As someone with multiple chronic illnesses and a weakened immune system, I have personally been prevented from receiving a second booster annually, being told by nurses that I am not “due” for one, despite vaccine efficacy waning in the months following vaccination and my immunocompromised status, which was questioned and my eligibility misinterpreted by the individual providers who denied my vaccination.

Waning vaccine efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Everyone needs access to twice annual COVID boosters due to efficacy waning and the constant and rapid emergence of new strains. Recent vaccination is also associated with lower risk of developing Long COVID after infection. Recommendation of at least twice yearly COVID boosters will result in better health outcomes following infection, alleviate strains on the healthcare system, and a lower number of individuals seeking disability for Long COVID and associated medical problems.

Along with a clear recommendation of COVID boosters at least twice a year for all ages and health statuses, the CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines. With the end of the CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program in August 2024, too many uninsured or underinsured adults are left without access to COVID vaccines due to financial constraints. Please recommend free COVID vaccine access for all to reduce barriers and hesitation for the public.

The CDC’s clear recommendation for COVID vaccination that is free and recommended every 6 months will improve public awareness of the importance of recent vaccination as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness and will also influence recommendation by healthcare providers and insurance (like mine who have blocked me from a second annual booster).

comment: Easy access to vaccination for COVID should be provided by the federal government to all persons within the United States, as COVID is an airborne virus, transmitted between all persons. Currently, vaccination is the only standing pillar of a public health response to this serious threat to the health and economic well being of people and our nation, as billowing numbers of people with Long COVID become severely disabled and will now struggle to lead fulfilling lives and to be able to support themselves. We do not have the healthcare resources in place to take care of this new disease burden, so vaccination remains a critical piece of trying to stymie an increasingly ill population for whom there are no biomedical solutions. Vaccine-driven protection from COVID dwindles rapidly past 3 months so it is absolutely crucial that people can access AT LEAST 2 vaccinations a year. This is especially crucial for the immune compromised, for whom a case of COVID could cause irreparable and unnecessary harm, suffering and death. If we restrict vaccination to once per year, it is a clear message to the chronically ill and immune compromised community, which is a quarter of the voting population, that their lives are disposable, as good as over already. Let's not do that!

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

References:
1. Link-Gelles R. Effectiveness of COVID-19 (2023-2024 Formula) vaccines. Presented at: FDA VRBPAC Meeting; June 5, 2024. Accessed June 12, 2024. https://www.fda.gov/media/179140/download

2. Wu N, Joyal-Desmarais K, Vieira AM, et al. COVID-19 boosters versus primary series: update to a living review. The Lancet Respiratory Medicine. 2023;11(10):e87-e88. doi:10.1016/S2213-2600(23)00265-5

3. Menegale F, Manica M, Zardini A, et al. Evaluation of Waning of SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine–Induced Immunity: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. JAMA Netw Open. 2023;6(5):e2310650. doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2023.10650

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Executive mismanagement of the Covid 19 pandemic exacerbated what would have already have been a grave public health crisis. The coronavirus is here to stay, and if we wish to avoid the worst of its fallout—mortality, overcrowded hospitals, quarantines (whether individual, municipal, county- or state-wide), enduring post-viral complications including cardiovascular and neurological disease, and the associated healthcare costs of those complications—we must do what worked to curb the crisis, and overcome the shortfalls that led to such a protracted battle with the virus.

Universal vaccine access in accordance with evidence-based vaccination schedules (at least every 6 months) MUST be maintained. We must also acknowledge that we do not have universal accessibility without universal affordability—$200 a shot on a biannual schedule simply exceeds the bounds of what is feasible for the average working-class or middle-class person, particularly for those who have families to consider. Vaccines must be regularly updated to immunize recipients against new mutations/strains, and public health agencies such as the CDC must be vocal AND consistent with the evidence and recommendations of researchers about the necessity of current vaccination and regular inoculation to maintain immunity.

title: Comment from Craig, Will
comment: More access is more better!

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Dear Committee,

I am writing as someone with an autoimmune disorder that has been in remission for 21 years. I'm not yet 40, and I have worked since 2020 in clinical research of vaccines, Covid, HIV, and TB. When I have gone to seek covid vaccines more than once a year I have been told by pharmacists that unless I have cancer, or am over 65, I am not eligible. I am aware that covid vaccine efficacy greatly drops off after 2 to 3 months post-vaccination, and that we should be vaccinating 4x a year for a disease that is not seasonal. I also am aware that epidemiologists that work with the government have said that the reason why we don't have vaccinations more than once a year is due to low-uptake, and therefore low incentive for manufacturers to develop current/updated vaccines to the public. I believe public health is a higher priority than profit and politics. I now lead a very active lifestyle and I'm convinced should I contract covid I will no longer be in remission. Before I was able to achieve remission, I would have times where I was immobile and/or hospitalized due to my autoimmune inflammation disorder. I'm currently postponing my latest vaccination as long as I can as I know I will be the best-man in a wedding in February, and should I vaccinate now, I likely won't be protected beyond my mask. The only reason I have to do this type of planning is due to the insufficient public vaccination distribution and schedule. Please vote in support of health and science.

title: Comment from Arizona Public Health Association
comment:
The Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices is considering changes to the meningococcal vaccine schedule that could have serious negative consequences for adolescents. The ACIP is considering three different options to alter the current recommendations for meningococcal disease, one of which is dangerous and administratively damaging.

Option 3 is the worst option ACIP will be considering. It would end the recommendation for adolescents to get their first dose of the meningococcal vaccine (MenACWY) at 11-12 years old and shift that to 16 years old, followed by a second dose at 18.

The 11–12-year age range is the last good opportunity for routine doctor visits for many young people before they enter the more tumultuous teenage years. Many parents stop bringing their kids to the doctor for annual checkups once they hit high school (except for sports physicals that often don’t include vaccine opportunities)
.
Option 3 would also upend school attendance vaccine requirements already in place in most states, including Arizona. These school vaccination requirements are designed to keep our communities safe by ensuring widespread immunization.

If Option 3 is adopted, health insurers would likely stop covering the vaccine at 11-12 years old, making students ineligible for insurance-covered vaccination until they turn 16. This would force states (like Arizona) to choose between either repealing their vaccination requirements for younger adolescents or facing widespread noncompliance due to the lack of insurance coverage at the 11–12-year-old visit.

We can’t afford to take this risk. Vaccine coverage at age 11-12 not only protects against meningococcal disease when kids are at risk but also keeps our schools safe, health systems functioning smoothly. It also gets the vaccine on board before kids are lost to follow up.

comment: Please ensure access to a twice a year updated COVID-19 booster vaccine.

title: Comment from Barghi, Ramin
comment: Please make the COVID vaccine free and accessible multiple times a year

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: I am the spouse of an immunocompromised person. My wife and I are frequently exposed through COVID-19 through our work with the public. Over the past few years, it's become clear that a once-a-year vaccine is not enough. Vaccination with the latest updated vaccines continues to be foundational to a multilayered approach to COVID, providing protection against both acute disease and Long COVID. Far too few Americans have received the latest vaccines. As of October 11, 2024, only 11.2% of all adults and 26.7% of adults aged 65 and older had received an updated 2024-2025 COVID vaccine. Data for children were unavailable at the time of this writing (October 15, 2024). COVID vaccination rates continue to lag behind influenza vaccination rates. As of July 27, 2024, only 9% of adults aged 65 and older received the recommended two doses of last year’s 2023-2024 vaccine. Creating a system where COVID vaccines are offered 1. twice a year at minimum and are also 2. completely free would do a lot to help my wife and I remain healthy throughout all times of the year.

title: Comment from Wylie, Heather
comment:
I am writing to encourage you to recommend COVID vaccines for all ages and health statuses at least twice a year (spring vaccine access for all groups) and to support more frequent updates to the vaccines, adjusted for the latest COVID variants. We have known since 2020 that seasonal protective immunity is short-lasting, making the need for more frequent vaccines (and vaccine uptake) critical. The vaccine schedule should address this waning efficacy. There is no natural immunity. Vaccine efficacy wanes after one to three months following vaccination. A lapse in vaccine protection, as well as low uptake in vaccinations, allows for more COVID strains to develop. Vaccination is critical for controlling this virus and for helping millions of Americans avoid Long COVID, as well as Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome (MIS-C) in children. My family would be made safer by more vaccine access: I have POTS, my husband is our sole income provider, and we have a small son. With growing evidence that POTS is autoimmune, allowing my spouse and my son to have a wider access to vaccines would add another protective layer for our family.

A clear, consistent message from the CDC to participate in a vaccination schedule for COVID at least twice a year would influence the recommendations made by family doctors and healthcare companies. It would also help improve public awareness of the importance of vaccinations - as many people have not kept up with their COVID vaccines. Many families are reluctant to get COVID vaccines because of a lack of consistent messaging (or misinformation campaigns identifying COVID as being the same as a "cold" or the "flu" - they should not be trying to catch the flu virus either) or they find the vaccines cost prohibitive. Making COVID vaccines more accessible throughout the year, and more affordable are critical for public health. Please advocate for free vaccines for uninsured and underinsured Americans. We know multilayered approaches are the best way to prevent illnesses. This is a critical step in a multilayered approach.

title: Comment from P, Rachel
comment:
I and several people in my family are immuno compromised for different reasons. We need access to more frequent vaccination.

I am a city school teacher and my eldest, with an immune deficiency, is a doctor. We both are in small rooms every day with people who are not vaccinated. The lack of public vaccination is a complex issue, but two important factors are (1) public messaging or the lack thereof about the importance of vaccination, which is reinforced by (2) a singular available annual vaccination. The vast majority of people seem to believe that Covid is not a danger to anyone, anymore. One of my high school seniors, who came to school last week with Covid, said, "no one dies of Covid!". My father died of Covid. Messaging about vaccination and access to free, semi-annual vaccination is critical if our lives matter, at all.

title: Comment from Rossi, Alexandria
comment: This falls under the right to life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness. We have the right to live healthy lives and vaccines, especially those against COVID, are necessary for us to protect that right.

title: Comment from Hugh, Brent
comment:
We know that the highest degree of protection from Covid lasts only 4-6 months at best. For that reason, Covid vaccinations should be available and recommended to everyone who needs or wants a higher degree of protection, twice each year.

This will help reduce the number of infections, the overall spread of the disease, pressure on the medical system due to increased disease, hospitalization, and death, and the incidence of Long Covid.

I have been suffering from Long Covid myself for the past 18 months and it has been debilitating personally and devastating financially for our family. The last thing I need is to have another case of Covid infection, with the possibility of worsening symptoms or extending the timeline for complete recovery.

There are millions of other citizens in this same situation. Helping to reduce the number of people with these debilitating systems, and worsening or extension of symptoms in this population, should be among the top priorities in your decision making.

comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

References:
1. Link-Gelles R. Effectiveness of COVID-19 (2023-2024 Formula) vaccines. Presented at: FDA VRBPAC Meeting; June 5, 2024. Accessed June 12, 2024. https://www.fda.gov/media/179140/download

2. Wu N, Joyal-Desmarais K, Vieira AM, et al. COVID-19 boosters versus primary series: update to a living review. The Lancet Respiratory Medicine. 2023;11(10):e87-e88. doi:10.1016/S2213-2600(23)00265-5

3. Menegale F, Manica M, Zardini A, et al. Evaluation of Waning of SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine–Induced Immunity: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. JAMA Netw Open. 2023;6(5):e2310650. doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2023.10650

4. Nguyen DC, Hentenaar IT, Morrison-Porter A, et al. SARS-CoV-2-specific plasma cells are not durably established in the bone marrow long-lived compartment after mRNA vaccination. Nat Med. Published online September 27, 2024:1-10. doi:10.1038/s41591-024-03278-y

5. Fang Z, Ahrnsbrak R, Rekito A. Evidence Mounts That About 7% of US Adults Have Had Long COVID. JAMA. Published online June 7, 2024. doi:10.1001/jama.2024.11370

6. Yousaf AR. Notes from the Field: Surveillance for Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in Children — United States, 2023. MMWR Morb Mortal Wkly Rep. 2024;73. doi:10.15585/mmwr.mm7310a2

7. https://www.cdc.gov/vaccines/programs/bridge/index.html

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Vaccination with the latest updated vaccines continues to be foundational to a multilayered approach to COVID, providing protection against both acute disease and Long COVID. Far too few Americans have received the latest vaccines. As of October 11, 2024, only 11.2% of all adults and 26.7% of adults aged 65 and older had received an updated 2024-2025 COVID vaccine. Data for children were unavailable at the time of this writing (October 15, 2024). COVID vaccination rates continue to lag behind influenza vaccination rates. As of July 27, 2024, only 9% of adults aged 65 and older received the recommended two doses of last year’s 2023-2024 vaccine.

Vaccine efficacy wanes significantly four to six months following vaccination, making updated vaccination important for all people as COVID continues to spread in our communities. Vaccine approaches that restrict access based on age or risk status put all of us at risk and leave those at high risk of severe consequences of COVID infection confused about whether they qualify to receive additional doses. These high risk patients may also face barriers as vaccine providers misunderstand the guidelines. A more frequent vaccination approach providing vaccination at least every six months as well as frequent updates to match current variants is needed to better protect all of us amid year-round COVID spread.

Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C). Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status.

The CDC’s Bridge Access Program, which previously provided COVID vaccines to uninsured and underinsured adults free of charge, ended in August 2024. The end of this program without replacement coverage puts people at risk, and public health officials must advocate for free vaccine access for all of us, including those who are uninsured and underinsured.

comment: Please make covid vaccines available twice a year for people of all ages. Covid isn't going away and will only continue to evolve. We need to be able to protect ourselves, our loved ones, and our communities as best we can. Twice annual vaccines for all will lower covid transmission!

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

Studies show our current COVID vaccines are only effective during the first 3 months after vaccination (https://www.hopkinsmedicine.org/health/conditions-and-diseases/coronavirus/covid-19-vaccine-what-you-need-to-know). A recent study also indicates that our bodies do not make long-lived plasma cells which may be why protection from our current vaccines wane.

It seems clear to me that COVID vaccination and/or boosters are needed every 3 months to address the waning efficacy of vaccines. Ideally, the vaccines would be updated with the most current circulating variant when possible.

I understand this is an unusual request, but COVID is an unusual virus. It is common to have to get injections to maintain your heath. Offering vaccination more often would give science time to develop better, longer-lasting vaccines while keeping the population as safe as possible. My strategy during this entire pandemic has been to do all I can to not catch COVID while we learn more about the disease and how to better prevent and fight it.

The public needs the CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination for all ages. Healthcare providers, dental offices, workplaces, and the general public all cite that they follow CDC recommendations. I’d like to also quickly state that we all need your clear recommendation of using N-95 or better masks as a part of a multilayered approach to preventing COVID.

Finally, your Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 which left many uninsured and under-insured adults without COVID vaccine access. Please reinstate this program or a similar one to ensure access to COVID vaccines for all of us to keep the population safe.

Thank you!

title: Comment from Taka, RJ
comment: Please bring back free vaccines for uninsured or under-insured people. Vaccines must be available and accessible to all. Please updated vaccines at least every 6 months since they wane after a few months and variants emerge frequently all year due to poor public health management. Please allow all immunocompromised people to have access to vaccines more frequently. Thank you.

title: Comment from Kimura, Aiko
comment:
I and 43 million Americans are or have been further or newly disabled by Long Covid. Between .5 and 4 million Americans have lost thier jobs due to Long Covid. I can not withstand a second Covid infection, and that is the case for so many people, especially disabled and immunocompromised people.
It is imperative that the Covid-19 vaccine is available and free. Covid vaccines are proven to lower infection rates and reduce the severity of infection. People deserve access to life saving medicine regardless of insurance status or monetary situation. The ending of the federal Bridge Access Program was an unacceptable act that will have life altering consequences for people who no longer have access to the vaccine as a result.
Covid is a deadly and debilitating infection that is continuously mutating and spreading at horrific rates due to impromper control and prevention on the part of the CDC. This has been heightened by the irresponsible shortening of isolation periods, ending of mask mandates, and the allowance for mask bans to become law in some areas. These is much that needs to be urgently addressed in the prevention of Covid-19 infections, and currently the CDC has the opportunity to take a step in the right direction. Allow Covid-19 vaccine access at least twice a year for all ages, and ensure that it is free.

title: Comment from Ferrell, Carolyn
comment: Since COVID isn’t primarily a seasonal/annual virus like the flu, and since the efficacy of COVID vaccines wanes after several months, and since repeated infections increase the chances of developing Long COVID, it’s logical that updated COVID vaccines should be available at least twice a year. Thank you for your efforts and consideration.

title: Comment from Hanson, Rita
comment: Please recommend COVID-19 vaccines twice a year. The initial studies and series of shots were conducted within 6 month windows, and as the virus continues to mutate and the vaccines continue to offer only meager coverage for 4-6 months, this still leaves all populations open to infection for the rest of the year. We have seen anecdotally, in hospitalizations, and in wastewater data that COVID is circulating year-round with multiple surges throughout the year, particularly in the summer and winter. Treating COVID as if it were a seasonal virus like the flu is ignorant of the data and leaves millions open to infection the other 3 seasons, which puts them at greater risk of Long Covid, compromised immune system, and post-viral sequelae and complications like cardiac issues, fatigue, cognitive decline, and more. Confining COVID-19 vaccinations to an annual shot also means development is lagging behind, both in keeping up with the variants and their mutations in studies and in rolling out relevant vaccines on time. As a result the vaccines are being formulated for variants a generation or two behind, discouraging patients from reuptake which consequently reduces the efficacy of the vaccine due to low turnout. That is, recent data shows that less than a quarter of Americans have gotten the newly formulated vaccines because they are considered irrelevant or ineffective (or for whatever other reason); the efficacy of these vaccines and their schedule of release and distribution must be improved, and subsequently Americans should be encouraged to receive them. However, until such a time that more frequent and effective vaccine updates are available, people of all ages regardless of health status should be able to get the current vaccine twice a year to maximize their coverage, as even a variant behind can still offer some immunity against currently circulating variants (were this not true, there would be no reason to get the vaccine at all, which is unfortunately why many do not receive it). Those of us who want it should be able to get it.

Oct 16, 2024
Make your voice heard and ask the CDC to:

Recommend COVID vaccines for all ages and health statuses at least twice a year (spring vaccine access for all) AND

Support more frequent updates to the vaccines, adjusted for the latest variants.

Submit a public comment using our sample language below.

The committee is anticipated to vote on the following topic on day 1 of the meeting (October 23): “Use of additional doses of COVID-19 vaccine in immunocompromised individuals and older adults following an initial dose of 2024–2025 vaccine”

Your comments make a difference. At this committee’s June 2024 meeting, public comments from our community led to the committee’s decision to make fall COVID vaccines available to people of all ages, rather than limiting eligibility to specific risk groups. Please join us in making your voice heard for spring COVID vaccine access for all, and at least twice a year access going forward.

Submit Written Comment

You can also register to give Oral Public Comment at the upcoming online CDC ACIP Meeting October 23-24 at: https://www2.cdc.gov/vaccines/acip/acip_publiccomment.asp

Submit written comments and/or register to make oral comments at the meeting by Friday October 18 at 11:59pm Eastern Standard Time.

How you, your family, or your community would be impacted by spring vaccine eligibility being restricted to only high risk groups (such as older age or immunocompromised status)

Barriers to vaccination your have faced, particularly if your eligibility was questioned or misinterpreted by a vaccine provider

How out-of-pocket costs are a barrier to getting the latest vaccines

Also feel free to take inspiration from or borrow the language in our sample public comment below.

Step 1. Go to the Regulations.gov to submit your comment.

Step 2. Type in your comment under the field, “Comment.”

Step 3 (optional). Submit a PDF or Word version of your comment under, “Attach Files.”

Step 4. Select either “Individual” or “Anonymous” depending on if you want to share your personal identifiable information that will be publicly available on the Federal Register.

Step 5. If selecting “Individual,” minimally provide your first and last name. If selecting “Anonymous” you can directly submit the comment without sharing your personal identifiable information. Click “Submit Comment.”

title: Comment from Wilson-Ripsom, Nikole
comment:
I urge the committee to recommend that everyone be eligible for a COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) regardless of age or health status. We have seen that in terms of covid and its short and long-term effects, there are very few truly "low-risk" individuals - it's much more of a gamble than other viral illnesses - which is one of the many reasons why people of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The recommended schedule should account for decreasing immunity in the months following vaccination as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Further, we now all know that covid is not seasonal. While there are dips in transmission, there is not a single "covid season", but rather, multiple peaks - the vaccination schedule should reflect that.

The CDC’s strong recommendation of universal COVID vaccination at least twice a year will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

Thank you.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:

Dear CDC,

I write to you as a teacher who is extremely concerned about the lack of opportunity for people of all ages and health statuses to obtain COVID vaccine boosters at least twice per year. Please, please, please make the COVID boosters available at least twice a year to all those who care to take them. Help me believe in public health again. Thank you for your time. Sincerely yours, Lisa M. Dresner

title: Comment from Rosales, Elizabeth
comment:
I am writing to strongly urge you to consider the following recommendations regarding COVID-19 vaccinations:

1. **Allow immunocompromised individuals to receive a second COVID vaccine dose two months after their first dose**: Immunocompromised people face a heightened risk of severe illness and complications from COVID-19. Their immune systems often do not respond as robustly to vaccines, and offering a second dose within a shorter window is critical for maximizing their protection.

2. **Broaden the language to allow *anyone* to receive a second dose after two months**: Flexibility in vaccination schedules is crucial. Different individuals may have varying immune responses to the initial dose based on factors such as age, pre-existing conditions, or lifestyle. Broadening the eligibility for a second dose ensures that those who need additional protection sooner can access it without barriers.

3. **Recommend COVID-19 vaccines every six months instead of yearly**: Given the current evolution of SARS-CoV-2 variants and the decreasing protection from vaccines over time, a six-month vaccination schedule would be far more effective in maintaining immunity at high levels, particularly as new variants continue to emerge. Waiting an entire year between doses leaves many people vulnerable as immunity wanes.

By adopting these recommendations, ACIP would be taking critical steps to protect public health, particularly for those at the highest risk of severe disease. Thank you for considering these urgent changes.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Please choose to make vaccine access available for whoever wants a new booster in the spring. Research has shown that immunity wanes over time (especially after the 6 month mark), so offering a booster in the spring would help to reduce negative disease impacts. Additionally, it would ensure that more doses are used instead of thrown away.

title: Comment from Hillier, Sarah
comment: Research has consistently shown that vaccine efficacy wanes after a few months. And in practice, unlike with the flu, we’re seeing spikes at least twice a year, both winter and summer. Everyone should be eligible to get boosted at least twice per year!

title: Comment from Brown, Megan
comment:
Please do not withhold COVID-19 vaccines from people simply because they are not elderly or immunocompromised.

Everybody benefits from the reduction in Long Covid that these vaccinaton will provide, both the specific individuals vaccinated and the health care and economic systems that support them. These benefits might not be realized for years but they will be substantial. The potential impact of Long Covid on children is especially concerning.

Also, the ability of vaccines to protect against infection wanes in just months. People who want to do all they can to avoid infection should be allowed that opportunity.

title: Comment from Lemer, Alison
comment:
I urge the CDC to please offer access to Covid vaccines, to Americans of all ages, at least twice a year, every year.

There are scores of medical studies that have shown the serious dangers of Covid infections – and especially the risks from multiple infections. We know that Covid damages our immune systems, making everyone who has ever had a part of the "vulnerable" population. We know that vaccines help reduce the chance of serious outcomes, including death and disability.

We also know that vaccine protection peaks after one month, then offers only about 50% protection for the next 4 months, and at that point wanes to the point of offering little to no protection. It is imperative that Americans have access to Covid vaccines multiple times per year to account for this.

I also urge you to require medical facilities across the USA to mask with N95 (or equivalent) respirators at all times, for the safety of both patients AND healthcare workers (who have been retiring from the field in record numbers due to Long Covid). Also, all medical facilities, public buildings, schools, etc should be required to install HEPA (or equivalent) filters in their HVAC systems to filter airborne pathogens out of shared indoor public air.

The CDC is meant to "control" diseases, and Covid is currently running completely unchecked. I implore you to take back control of the fight against Covid.

comment:
PLEASE recommend COVID vaccines for all ages and health statuses at least twice a year AND have more frequent updates to the vaccines, adjusted for the latest variants.

People are not masking and are relying on vaccines, and 1 vaccine with no adjustment for variants leads to even more COVID spread and re-infection. My family is getting COVID 2 times a year at least, and it makes no sense to only have 1 vaccine a year. People of all ages and health statuses should AT THE LEAST be getting 2. People are constantly walking around infected, children at schools are constantly getting re-infected, there is no "seasonal" COVID when COVID levels are high all year round. COVID levels are higher than they were in 2020. Its a disgrace how little public health measures there are, and the very least you can do is recommend COVID vaccines for all ages and health statuses at least twice a year AND have more frequent updates to the vaccines, adjusted for the latest variants. Check out latest COVID levels at https://pmc19.com/data/

title: Comment from S, R
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. title: Comment from Schoonmaker, Alena
comment: We need COVID vaccines for all ages and health statuses at least twice a year (spring vaccine access for all) AND more frequent updates to the vaccines, adjusted for the latest variants. My parents are over 60 and are at higher risk, but my brother is only 31 and he was perfectly healthy in every way until he got long COVID! He may have been better able to fight COVID had he been able to receive more frequent vaccinations, despite being a young and well person at the time.

title: Comment from Turgasen, Ellen
comment: Everyone should be able to get a covid booster twice a year q 6 months and the vaccines should be updated for variants on a regular basis.

title: Comment from Mueller, Ellen
comment:
I’m asking the CDC to:
Recommend COVID vaccines for all ages and health statuses at least twice a year (spring vaccine access for all) AND
Support more frequent updates to the vaccines, adjusted for the latest variants.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
It is an endless source of frustration for our family that we have to attempt to "time the market" when getting vaccinated for Covid-19. Unlike influenza, this virus is not seasonal, and each year we have summer and winter waves that vary in their timing and magnitude. Antibodies that protect against infection wane rapidly after ~3 months. Why are we forcing an influenza vaccine schedule on a virus that does not behave like influenza? At minimum, we should have two Covid-19 vaccination campaigns a year, preceeding the summer and winter waves.

Additionally, infants under 6 months are ineligible for vaccination, but this age group has the highest rate of hospitalization after the very elderly. Pregnant individuals should be encouraged to get an additional Covid-19 vaccine dose in their third trimester in order to confer maternal antibodies on their newborn, similar to the current Tdap recommendations.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
CDC must move to recommend COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.
The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status.

Yearly vaccination is not enough.

title: Comment from Peterson, Elizabeth
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

References:
1. Link-Gelles R. Effectiveness of COVID-19 (2023-2024 Formula) vaccines. Presented at: FDA VRBPAC Meeting; June 5, 2024. Accessed June 12, 2024. https://www.fda.gov/media/179140/download

2. Wu N, Joyal-Desmarais K, Vieira AM, et al. COVID-19 boosters versus primary series: update to a living review. The Lancet Respiratory Medicine. 2023;11(10):e87-e88. doi:10.1016/S2213-2600(23)00265-5

3. Menegale F, Manica M, Zardini A, et al. Evaluation of Waning of SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine–Induced Immunity: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. JAMA Netw Open. 2023;6(5):e2310650. doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2023.10650

4. Nguyen DC, Hentenaar IT, Morrison-Porter A, et al. SARS-CoV-2-specific plasma cells are not durably established in the bone marrow long-lived compartment after mRNA vaccination. Nat Med. Published online September 27, 2024:1-10. doi:10.1038/s41591-024-03278-y

5. Fang Z, Ahrnsbrak R, Rekito A. Evidence Mounts That About 7% of US Adults Have Had Long COVID. JAMA. Published online June 7, 2024. doi:10.1001/jama.2024.11370

6. Yousaf AR. Notes from the Field: Surveillance for Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in Children — United States, 2023. MMWR Morb Mortal Wkly Rep. 2024;73. doi:10.15585/mmwr.mm7310a2

7. https://www.cdc.gov/vaccines/programs/bridge/index.html

comment:
Please do not withhold COVID-19 vaccines from people simply because they are not elderly or immunocompromised.

Everybody benefits from the reduction in Long Covid that these vaccinaton will provide, both the specific individuals vaccinated and the health care and economic systems that support them. These benefits might not be realized for years but they will be substantial. The potential impact of Long Covid on children is especially concerning.

Also, the ability of vaccines to protect against infection wanes in just months. People who want to do all they can to avoid infection should be allowed that opportunity.

comment:
Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C). Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
As someone who is immune compromised and 34 years old, I rely on the people around me (both young and old, healthy and vulnerable) to have access to COVID vaccines twice a year to help protect me from getting sick with COVID a second time. I was already immune compromised but developed long COVID following my first exposure and now deal with even more debilitating exhaustion and sinus tachycardia. My family and friends of all ages and stages of health being able to get vaccinated at least twice a year helps protect me and everyone in my community from being even more severely disabled or dying from this disease. Restricting vaccine access to only vulnerable or older populations does not take into account all the healthy individuals who interact with vulnerable individuals on a daily basis. Having the greatest access to vaccines at least twice a year means more people will get vaccinated and protect each other from this incredibly debilitating disease.

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) is a critical recommendation for people of all ages, regardless of health status. Restricting eligibility creates more barriers for vulnerable people and discourages people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.
The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination and emerging new COVID strains by recommending updated vaccination people of all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen across all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning immunity supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C).
The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.
The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August, leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.
People with disabilities, many of whom have fewer financial resources and are the most vulnerable deserve to have an agency like the CDC advocating for the most vaccine access and protection, since vaccination across the community is critical to limit the spread of this devastating illness. Please don’t let us and our communities down.

The covid virus mutates quickly enough that a twice a year vaccine would be effective as well as helpful for people like me with milder conditions that nevertheless effect our immunity. Because my condition is not on the list that qualifies patients for twice/year vaccines, I can't get one. That seems ridiculous: the vaccines are available and accessible. title: Comment from Patterson, Laura
comment: In order to keep the harmful — and occassionally prolonged —side effects of COVID at bay, we must move towards a better strategy for addressing the virus. Allowing conscientious citizens the opportunity to protect themselves, their loved ones and their communities through twice a year vaccinations helps everyone in our society — including and especially those among us who are immunocompromised. It’s clear that the majority does not take COVID seriously, but there are so many of us who do, and we want to participate in our lives with less fear and take care of ourselves and each other with more freedom.

title: Comment from McCall, Laura
comment:
It is important that the guidelines allow flexibility so that individuals can maximize protective effects of vaccines. For instance, the lengthy period between booster doses means that many should repeat the initial series for maximal effect. This is especially important for people who are immunocompromised, but it would benefit everyone to have the flexibility to receive a second booster.
Further, allowing people to have an additional booster within the current 6-month period would reduce the vulnerability of patients during the summer, when the previous season’s vaccines have expired but before the updated formulas are available.

title: Comment from Smith, John
comment:
I wish to challenge the committee members to reflect upon their own experiences over the past four years. Is there someone you used to see in the hallways at work, or bump into at a local restaurant, but when you actually stop to think about it, you haven't seen them in quite some time? Years ago that could mean they moved or changed jobs or a hundred other innocuous things.

Now, it's Covid.

We don't talk about it, we don't admit it to ourselves, but Covid is the reason those people have disappeared from your world. Maybe they got sick and the crippling fatigue and brain fog prevents them from navigating in-person interactions. Maybe they're just trying to protect themselves and their families from suffering that fate. And maybe they're just dead. But whatever the reason, Covid is what made your life less bright, less dynamic, less human.

Vaccines are our only hope. The only prayer those of us who accept the dangers of Covid have, that maybe some day, some how, the world will be safe enough to rejoin.

You have the power to help bring those missing people back into your world. To stand for truth, and righteousness, and justice for all of us who have been belittled and ridiculed and injured, just because we adhere to medical science.

Give us a chance to be part of your lives again, and for you to be a part of ours. You owe it to yourselves, and to the thousands upon thousands of Americans who rely upon these vaccines for their very lives. Vote for maximum Covid vaccine updates and availability. Vote for truth.

title: Comment from Fitzpatrick, Charlie
comment:
As the pandemic rages on and people are forced into workplaces where there are no masking protections, twice-yearly vaccinations are essentially the only solution people can access that gives any kind of significant protection against covid and long covid and the disabling effects of both. Many people in high-risk jobs — any public-facing job — are under the age of 65. Twenty and 30-year-olds are the main age group employed in these high-traffic, high-risk jobs. For those under 18 are exposed to covid waves in schools and universities where masks are also not mandatory and spread is rampant. The full effects of covid on young people's bodies is still relatively unknown. People in these age groups can and do die from covid, or else can become significantly disabled by long covid, for which there is still no cure or treatment solution.

Additionally, as there is little to no mask protection in health care anymore, lack of vaccine protection forces sick and vulnerable people who have not had access to vaccines in over a year to choose between not receiving treatment at a doctor's office or hospital where staff and patients are not masked, and a medical visit that may make them sicker if they do contract covid. Many vulnerable and disabled people do not qualify for the CDC's limited list of people who are considered to be "at risk." The thousands of weekly covid deaths and millions of people becoming disabled by covid and long covid each year should be testament to revisiting who is truly "at risk" of severe covid and lifelong covid complications.

As we know, vaccines help protect us against severe covid and hospitalization and covid-related death (there are still thousands of covid deaths each week in the US alone), but as with all vaccines, the covid vaccine's protection does not last forever. Once a year is not enough protection if we are using vaccines as the only prevention method for covid. Additionally, vaccines must be free and accessible to everyone. The current cost of vaccines is inhumane.

Long covid cases are lower in those who are up-to-date on vaccinations, which can only be only beneficial, considering there is still no cure or treatment plan for long covid, and the healthcare system is not currently capable of taking on the millions of people still becoming disabled from covid and long covid each year.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
I am writing in regard to the vaccines for Covid that are now available. I am hoping that we can get the best protection we can and would like to see a recommendation of more than one vaccine per year. One vaccine a year is still making everyone susceptible to bad outcomes of Covid. The virus is not seasonal and so has the ability to infect multiple times per year. We need to have coverage for this virus all year round.

Also I would like to see more emphasis put on the nasal vaccines and pushing to get them out sooner than what is being planned for. I just saw an article stating the present expectation is in 2027. I have family who are getting some debilitating illnesses after their Covid infections now. By the time 2027 is here, we will see so many who need medical care for issues caused by Covid that I fear the health of our families and friends will be too far gone. Many will be disabled or will have passed away by the time these vaccines are developed. I am already seeing this in so many of my family members. There is no plan for mitigation either. These vaccines are our best chance to stop or slow the transmission. Please work to make these vaccines available as soon as can be possible. Thank you.

title: Comment from Adkin, Diane
comment:
Please recommend COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages and health statuses. Vaccines should be updated frequently to adjust for the latest varients, so even every 6 months might not be often enough as the virus mutates.
Restricting your recommendations to certain people causes hesitation and worry we don’t meet the guidelines. In fact, universal vaccines as a public health measure actually makes us all safer.
Your guidelines need to take into account that vaccines lose their effect a few months after vaccination. Also, there is less risk of developing Long COVID after a recent vaccination.
CDC must improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of vaccination within the last six months for the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness. The CDC and all public health officials must advocate for free COVID tests and vaccines for all of us, including those who are uninsured and underinsured. Bring back the CDC’s Bridge Access Program!

title: Comment from Anderson, Bev
comment:
This situation is personal for me and for my husband. We’re both in our 70s, and he's immune compromised, which means we are both especially vulnerable to Covid. Covid vaccinations every six months (or sooner) must be recommended for everyone. Restricting eligibility to anyone makes it unduly hard for people who are most vulnerable, and it discourages high risk people from receiving critical boosters.

This vaccine schedule is critical since the vaccine’s effectiveness decreases in the months after receiving a vaccination and, as the virus continues to morph, the vaccine needs to be updated. comment: I would like Covid vaccines, preferably updated, to be available to everyone TWICE a year. If the protection from a vaccine only lasts about 4 months, that’s a lot of time unprotected. title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Please consider making COVID vaccines available in advance of school starting, recommend them every six months for any age group that wants them, and updating them more often (like 2x/year with new variants). I have a medically complex child in my home, so the risk of COVID is very much still a reality for us (not to mention that we know several people with long COVID and would like to avoid that as well). Once we hit 3-4 months out from getting our vaccines we know they’re not working and well anymore, which is stressful. Starting the school year during a big surge, with my kids’ last doses being nearly a year prior, was stressful. Our schools are old with poor ventilation and horrendous vaccination rates. I don’t want to have my kids wear masks forever, but it appears they’ll need to as I’m pretty disappointed in CDC’s guidance for when people have COVID.   Novody in our school distict wears a mask around my high risk child anymore when I’ll because per my school district “CDC says masks are optional and not a core mitigation strategy.”  They also skip the part about staying home until symptoms improve and just focus on fever. (Why isn’t wearing a mask a core mitigation strategy? Isn’t COVID airborne? How does washing hands even help with COVID?) My child is a sitting duck with so-so vaccines that she can only get once a year and no mitigations whatsoever at her school. We need to do better for high risk people and kids.

title: Comment from Romine, Kal
comment:
PLEASE insist upon availability of Covid vaccines every six months!

The science is indisputable that vaccine efficacy wanes within a few months, and the science is also indisputable that twice-a-year vaccination has no serious side effects.

I’m a 45-year-old disabled person with POTS (which I have had for 20 years but is caused/exacerbated by Covid), Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome (which research shows puts me at 30% higher risk for long COVID), fibromyalgia + autism + trans (communities who demonstrably fare worse with Covid infections), living with immunocompromised people.

My HMO refused to give me a six-month booster because I am not 65+ or, technically, “immunocompromised” myself.

Does that sound right to you?

Does that sound like responsible public health?

Do you think I, as a disabled person, can afford $190 out of pocket for this elsewhere?

Do you think I will cost the government MUCH more money than that if I do get sick?

Twice-yearly vaccines are scientifically sound, humane, realistic, and even cost-effective.

I am BEGGING you, as a human being, to help me not become more ill than I already am, and to prevent my death and the death of those I love.

Please.

Otherwise, what is this whole agency for?

title: Comment from Valenzuela, Ananda
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

Please increase the frequency and accessibility of COVID vaccines, to support a healthier society as a whole, and to tend to those most impacted by the pandemic! We had a big summer surge in COVID cases- this could truly make a difference.

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

3. Menegale F, Manica M, Zardini A, et al. Evaluation of Waning of SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine–Induced Immunity: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. JAMA Netw Open. 2023;6(5):e2310650. doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2023.10650

title: Comment from Abbatiello, Angela
comment: Please expand Covid boosters to twice a year! Research shows that they all wane in 4-6 months. And it’s NOT seasonal like flu. So please let immunocompromised and vulnerable patients like myself get the option to be FULLY covered. Even at the current “full booster” schedule, my one Covid case retriggered my preexisting CFS that had gotten under control. I go everywhere masked and I zero faith in your explanation to the American people of the actual risk to their health from repeated Covid exposure. Please do better!!!!

title: Comment from Morinville, Megan
comment:
COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. I work in a school and am around hundreds of students every day. It is completely misguided to only have vaccines available once a year. The science tells us that the vaccine wanes after a few months and we cannot afford to have people be vulnerable. COVID is not the flu and we need to stop treating it as such. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from rudd, helen
comment: 2 vaccines per year are needed!

title: Comment from Godlewski, Nina
comment: Please allow people of all ages to get two Covid vaccines a year! We need whatever we can to protect ourselves and there is no drawback to multiple vaccines. Though there is a major risk and drawback to getting multiple infections. Please change the guidance.

title: Comment from Snyder, Jennifer
comment:
COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

Vaccination with the latest updated vaccines continues to be foundational to a multilayered approach to COVID, providing protection against both acute disease and Long COVID. Far too few Americans have received the latest vaccines. Making updated COVD vaccinations available to everyone more frequently throughout the year would increase awareness to the public that these are a necessary form of a multilayered approach to mitigation and health.

title: Comment from Massa, Megan
comment: I am a high risk individual and I hope that we can get more updated SARS-CoV2 vaccination guidance that follows the science. Data has shown that protections from vaccines wanes in a few months. Therefore, not only should  immunocompromised people be able to get a second SARS-CoV2 booster 2 months after the first, but all people should be able to get multiple booster vaccinations. This, in addition to increasing booster frequency to every 6 months, will do better to protect the populace from COVID surges and thus prevent reduce long-term disability.

title: Comment from Flanagan, Elizabeth
comment: it is critical we allow immunocompromised people to get a 2nd COVID vaccine 2 months after the first. that we broaden language to allow ANYONE to get a 2nd dose after 2 months. And recommend COVID vaccines every 6 months instead of yearly.

title: Comment from M, Ana
comment: Hello, it is very important to both myself and my loved ones that COVID vaccines be recommended every six months for all ages and health statuses. I am not immunocompromised but I take care of my parents who both are so I mask and vaccinate in part because how much more painful and life threatening it would be if I gave them COVID. We have risks that we cannot avoid however, my sister and I travel for work and her daughter is exposed regularly in school so vaccination is an important layer of protection. For all of us, getting COVID again would increase our risk of long covid but recent vaccination reduces that risk.  Clear guidance from the CDC to vaccinate twice a year will help insurance cover it which is a critical part of making it accessible enough for people to actually go and get vaccinated. (The Bridge program should also be brought back so that folks without insurance can also access the vaccine.) The more of us that vaccinate the better for all of us but especially my aging parents who want to stick around longer to see my niece grow up.

title: Comment from Boland, Breanne
comment:
We have years of evidence showing the accumulating and devastating damage of repeated Covid infections. Some people's lives are changed by just one.

We also know that vaccines have played a large part in improved outcomes and reduced hospitalizations. Furthermore, we know that vaccine protection peaks at just a month after vaccination and provides relatively little protection after just four months.

Our current Covid vaccine schedule is based on a flu-like infection schedule, but the two simply aren't comparable. Summer 2024 on its own gives ample reason to offer vaccines twice a year, to keep protection stronger and to keep up with new mutations.

Please, offer Covid vaccine access to all ages at least twice a year. Please also require medical facilities across the USA to mask with respirators at all times. A friend had surgery this summer and had to accept the risk of coming home to recover from a procedure with Covid caught in a hospital. This is controllable and is a ridiculous risk to accept. Please protect your people. Please protect us.

title: Comment from Hunter , Tiffany
comment:
As research shows that COVID immunity from vaccines only lasts for 3 months, much like the flu vaccine, once per year is not enough.

With the flu, the 3 months of protection lines up with the amount of time that the flu is spreading, so by the time your immunity/benefits of the vaccine decline, the flu is no longer a major concern.

However COVID does not follow this same pattern and is prevalent all year long, with surges in the winter and the summer, as seen now over multiple years of data collection. Therefore a once yearly vaccine that only benefits you for 3 months is not enough. Vaccines should be available at least twice yearly to cover major surges.

In addition the CDC should cease to lump COVID in with the flu in it's information campaigns as it causes the general public to not know amd understand the above information.

With COVID mortality rates much higher than the flu, and with much more severe long term consequences to those infected and reinfected, it can only help the public to make the vaccine available to provide the most protection, which is at least twice a year.

Thank you for taking the time to read and consider.

title: Comment from Connelly, M
comment: Given the short term efficacy of current vaccines and the multi-peak trends we have observed in the last several years, with spikes at the end of summer and around the new year, it would make a lot of sense to increase the annual vaccine / booster schedule to twice a year at minimum.  It is already that frequent for older adults, but given what we know the risks of repeated exposure can be, this should be true for everyone.

title: Comment from Degann, Ilana
comment: I have had Long Covid since first getting Covid in August of 2023. After over a year of becoming immunocompromised from this virus, I find that going out, living my life, and meaningfully participating is scarier and more difficult than ever. An additional vaccine every year could help keep myself and my community safer and allow me to continue working, playing, and doing the things I love

title: Comment from Heitz, J
comment: I got covid for the first time this month. I'm so lucky it was very mild, and I fully believe that one of the main reasons is that I've been vaccinated 6 times, with the most recent vaccine less than a month ago. The risk of long term impacts increases with every subsequent infection, but the vaccines help mitigate risk of infection, risk of severe illness, risk of long term side effects. But we know the efficacy decreases after a few months. This is why we need vaccine access for everyone available in the spring and fall.

title: Comment from J., Daisy
comment:
I implore you all to not withhold COVID-19 vaccines from people simply because they are not considered elderly or immunocompromised. The benefits of these vaccines, in particular for the reduction of Long COVID, are innumerable for both the specific individuals vaccinated as well as the health care and economic systems that support them. These benefits may not be measurable right away, but they will be substantial in the long term of managing this ongoing pandemic. The potential impact of Long COVID on children is especially concerning, as we do not yet understand the full scope of the impact that these infections will have on their growing brains and bodies.

Additionally, the ability of the vaccines to protect against infection wanes in just months (if not sooner). Individuals who want to protect themselves against infection should be allowed that opportunity, regardless of demographic or medical history; we all deserve the right to pursue wellness and protect our health by any means possible.

title: Comment from Marvin, Harvey
comment:
-Allow immunocompromised people to get a 2nd COVID vaccine 2 months after the first
-Broaden language to allow ANYONE to get a 2nd dose after 2 months
-Recommend COVID vaccines every 6 months instead of yearly
