title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Please vote to allow extra COVID boosters every few months. As a medically vulnerable person (with vulnerable family members), I would greatly benefit from having multiple COVID vaccine doses each year, rather than suffering waning protection a few months after my yearly shot. Until a vaccine with more durable immunity is developed, getting multiple vaccine doses per year is a good strategy to provide increased protection.

title: Comment from M, Peter
comment: You should allow anyone to get a second vaccine dose and recommend Covid vaccines every six months and require masks in airports

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

Recommending COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) for people of all ages, regardless of health status is necessary to protect the American people. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. It is also discrimination. People of all ages, abilities, and heath statuses should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The current annual vaccine schedule does not go far enough to address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. As you are no doubt already aware, efficacy wanes with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

Giving all Americans greater access to COVID vaccines will help ensure that vulnerable populations are protected. It will also help ensure that COVID outbreaks do not impact businesses and government. I currently work in a large government office where it is impossible to know whether other employees are vaccinated. If I and other concerned employees could get additional vaccines on a schedule that matches waning efficacy, we could do more to protect ourselves and our colleagues. This would mean fewer missed days of works and more safe in-person work.

title: Comment from Richardson, Michal
comment:
Hi, I am high risk for Covid complications and currently have Long Covid. I need people to be more on top of their vaccinations and Covid precautions so that I don't get sicker.

Please authorize more frequent vaccinations.
Please ensure they are free to everyone.
Please guide Americans to take precautions like wearing masks. Especially while contagious. This is basic human decency.
Please require masks in healthcare. People like me are avoiding necessary healthcare because we are in danger of getting Covid at the doctor's office. This is unconscionable.

Thanks. Please do your job and control disease. I would really appreciate it.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Please let us manage COVID! Let us vaccinate 2 months out from our first vaccine and have a booster every six months. Have updated vaccines ready before the start of the school year. We used to use science to help manage and even cure diseases. Let’s do that again!

title: Comment from Rider, Janet
comment: As a person with multiple health issues, I see it as critical the CDC take a rational approach and recommend COVID vaccination at least the very least twice annually. All of our lives matter. Public health practices have lagged behind what is needed and so many individuals have developed long COVID!  We need the CDC to provide leadership and care.

title: Comment from Schweers, Gretchen
comment: Over 17 million Americans are suffering from long Covid and over 1 million Americans have died from the disease. Because life has returned to normal and health measures like masks and social distancing are no longer being practiced, Covid has the ability to spread rampantly through the population and evolve new variants quickly. Since the pandemic started, there has consistently been a “summer wave” that causes a huge spike in Covid cases. Over the summer doctors tested those with respiratory symptoms approximately equally for RSV, Covid, and flu, and 97% of cases ended up being Covid. Americans need the protection of twice annual shots to prevent further debilitating damage by long Covid and to reduce the risk for immune compromised individuals of all ages. Those who have been vaccinated experience long Covid and approximately half the rate of the unvaccinated and vaccines that are up to date with newer variants increase the overall effectiveness at preventing serious health effects. By offering twice annual vaccine protection to the American public, the burden on the health care system will be reduced, with fewer hospitalizations and fewer cases of disabling long Covid. Please maintain the twice a year vaccine schedule to protect public health and our most vulnerable citizens.

title: Comment from Yamrose, Darlene
comment: I would like to recommend covid vaccines for all ages at least twice a year.  The problem would be that without them, people are at more risk for systemic damage, and by letting the disease go unchecked, it puts the most vulnerable at greater risk.  It would be shameful not to offer this.  And as more variants pop up, we don't know if this will become even more dangerous.  We do know it's a vascular disease, which can lead to long-term covid, disabilities, hospitalizations, and at worst, death.  Consequently, we need continued research into this virus and updated vaccines.  Since masking and no other real preventions are being taken to limit this disease, it's the very least we can do.  A sick nation becomes a weak nation.

title: Comment from Daniels, Erin
comment: I’m writing to request that Covid vaccinations be available twice each year for everyone (not just immunocompromised people). I’m a single parent and one of my children is high risk. We get covid shots each Fall, but I would love to be able to better protect my entire family with a shot every 6 months instead of every 12.

title: Comment from Heitz, Theresa
comment: It is so important to make sure people have access to get the vaccine. COVID does not behave like the flu—it is not seasonal and mutates much more quickly. MRNA vaccines offer great protection for a very short period and then wane over about 4 months. That means whole swaths of the population are still vulnerable to the disease for most of the year if we follow the one vaccine annually model. Studies are showing that ALL people are susceptible to long term effects of repeated covid infections and public health has a responsibility to mitigate that harm in every way we can. People who have had covid have elevated risk of heart disease and strokes for three years following an infection. This is a massive amount of widespread harm, particularly given that the virus continues to spread uncontrollably, infecting millions again and again every year. We need to be doing everything we can to protect each other and ourselves long term and that means following the science and maintaining access to tools like vaccines that reduce the scale of this burden.

title: Comment from Wu, Victoria
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

comment:

I read the scientific literature, I recognize that COVID is not benign, and I have adapted to its endemicity by adopting layers of mitigations to prevent infection/minimize our family’s infections over our lifetimes. I believe health professionals and give have a duty of care to help people (themselves included) maintain health by preventing disease and serious illness.

Here, I appeal to your interest in supporting collective health. I ask that you please:
- Allow immunocompromised people to get a 2nd COVID vaccine 2 months after the first
-Broaden language to allow ANYONE to get a 2nd dose after 2 months.
-Recommend COVID vaccines every 6 months instead of yearly.

Evidence is clear: protection via vaccination wanes. Please allow people doing their best to stay healthy to use all of the available tools to best advantage.

I also ask that use of N95 and better respirators be recommended in healthcare settings, which should be places where people find health and healing, not high-risk, illness-promoting environments for vulnerable individuals and populations. COVID is airborne and can affect every organ system, to devastating effect; airborne precautions are appropriate.

title: Comment from Mead, Carrie
comment:
COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Rollier, Jonathan
comment:
Hi, as an asthma patient I am a higher risk individual and have been paying close attention to the science behind preventing and treating COVID, including Long COVID. Here are my thoughts about what we need to keep:
1. Everyone needs to be able to access these vaccines, they need to be universally available for 6 months and up.

3. We need to keep the 65+ additional dose vaccine recommendations.

1. We need to update the COVID vaccine frequency to every 3-4 months to account for waning immunity.
2. We need to update the COVID vaccine formula every 6 months as opposed to once every year.
Due to the unchecked spread of COVID, we are allowing it to mutate and evolve exponentially faster, meaning that prior immunity is waning at ever faster rates as the virus moves sufficiently far away to reinfect individuals. As COVID is not seasonal, having a summer and winter wave at minimum, we need to utilize the speed of mRNA technology to update the vaccines at a faster rate to keep it in check. 3. On an every 6-month updated vaccine schedule, the COVID vaccine frequency should be set to every 3 months.

This will allow individuals the ability to receive an updated vaccine and a booster for each vaccine update at the 6-month intervals.

Thank you.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
I urge you to take action to mandate and program that ensures ongoing COVID vaccine access at least twice a year, for all ages.

Due to a COVID infection in February of 2020 at 19 years of age, I became oxygen dependent for a short period of time, and almost succumbed to sepsis due to severe pneumonia. I’m writing this because I still remember the feeling of knowing I was about to lose my battle, and I don’t wish that on anyone. To this day, I still live with complications of that infection and Long Covid symptoms.

If access to the vaccines are increased to at least twice a year, then countless individuals can mitigate the severity of their infections, before they even contract them.

Please consider increasing access to these life-saving vaccines.

title: Comment from Stone, Judy
comment:
We know that protective immunity from Covid vaccines wanes quickly, e.g.,: one month after people received two doses of either mRNA vaccine (from Moderna or Pfizer-BioNTech), the vaccine from AstraZeneca, or the shot from Sinovac, the vaccine effectiveness was 53% in protecting against symptoms of COVID-19. After six months, the overall effectiveness of the vaccines dropped further to 14%, and to 9% after nine months. This waning was greater during the Omicron wave. (https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2804451).

Because of this and that we have limited effective treatments and almost no public health efforts to prevent Covid through masking or improved ventialtion, our main line of defense is vaccination.

Given quickly waning immunity, we should:
— Allow immunocompromise​d people to get a second Covid vaccine two months after the first
— Broaden language to allow anyone to get a second dose after two months
— recommend Covid vaccines every six months instead of yearly.

I urge you to make these changes.
Judy Stone, MD,
Infectious Disease physician

comment: Given the 6month cycle of covid waves and varient mutations, vaccines should be covered by insurance every 6 months. Please help those who care about their own and their community’s long term health access the vaccines they need. Preventative care is much more cost efficient than taking care of all the effects having Covid multiple times will do to a person.

title: Comment from Roberts, Miriam
comment:
Hi,

Unlike the flu, COVID is a year-round disease, and all the information I've seen says that COVID vaccines provide the best protection for 3-6 months. There doesn't seem to be any shortage of vaccines available in the US, and I don't understand why the vaccines are limited to once per year, rather that allowing people to get them twice a year if that seems better for their personal medical situation. Personally, I'm in the middle of a debate about whether I should boost my immunity now, when it's been a year since my last vaccine, or hold out another month or so to have the best protection as long as possible during the peak of the winter season. It feels like we're artificially limiting one of the best tools we have to protect people's health.

Thank you.

title: Comment from Drutchas, Genevieve
comment: We feel strongly that the govt. should be making two vaccines a year available to all ages of people at no cost. Instead of doing the more practical things that could have been done in the early years of the pandemic, continuing indoor masking in medical and public facilities, focussing on funding the updating of ventilation systems in our shared indoor spaces, hiring more nurses and teachers and increasing their pay, offering paid time off for those that delivered front line services, and many other more practical and fact-based interventions, we're now left in a place of rampant high covid levels and public denial and misinformation. Providing a free vaccine to everyone twice a year is one of the more direct action steps that can be taken to help lower the numbers of people that will continue to get Covid and Long Covid and experience the longer term systemic damage related to having this virus over and over again.

title: Comment from Khan, Ayesha
comment:
— Allow immunocompromised people to get a second Covid vaccine two months after the first
— Broaden language to allow anyone to get a second dose after two months
— recommend Covid vaccines every six months instead of yearly.
.

- immunity wanes pretty rapidly, so 6 months should be the standard, with more frequently if immunocompromised.

title: Comment from Smolen, Kelsey
comment: I am a disabled, housebound, and immunocompromised person married to a middle school teacher. He does everything he can to protect me, including wearing an N95 mask all day everyday, using an air filter in his classroom purchased with our own funds, as well as getting vaccinated. We know that vaccination does not stop transmission, but could reduce viral load and decrease the chance of his infecting me. He is also my only caregiver, as we live far from family and have no friends who can help, so we cannot afford for him to get sick. For all of these reasons, it would be so helpful if he could fortify one of his layers of protection by being able to get vaccinated twice a year. Why let vials sit unused, expiring on pharmacy shelves when people in need would use them? What is the harm in giving us a greater chance when society has turned their backs on us by refusing to do anything to reduce transmission? Thank you for reading my comment.

title: Comment from Gray , A
comment: The COVID pandemic is not over; it still represents a threat to vulnerable populations, and the long term effects of illness are still coming to light, even for otherwise healthy individuals. Free access to twice-yearly COVID vaccines should be available to individuals of all ages and health statuses, as the vaccines represent an essential part of the CDC's responsibility to protect the health of all Americans.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: The COVID vaccine should follow the same regulations as the Influenza vaccines year over year, IF NOT COVID available twice a year or more as needed according to global trends.

title: Comment from McDermott, L.
comment: I am both a social worker who works in federal and state health policy implementation, and a surviving daughter of my parents who have become disabled and died from COVID. Evidence-based research, and practice-based evidence, shows us that long COVID cases are increasingly, particularly as the public health emergency began its unwinding in the spring of 2023. Evidence and data collected from agencies like the CDC and local public health departments has shown us since 2020 that COVID cases tend to peak at MINIMUM, biannually. In a political landscape where the federal government has abandoned collective public health policy and best practices, millions of people in America are left to navigate vaccine access within a country that has a fraught healthcare system, inaccessible vaccine administration, and is slowly implementing bans to public health best practices, such as masking in public. One, annual COVID vaccine is insufficient in today's current public health landscape, and I urge the CDC to push for biannual COVID vaccine access.

comment: COVID 19 vaccine access is critical for ensuring our health! The alternative is larger waves of COVID that result in more deaths and long term disability. COVID vaccines MUST remain accessible and available, especially for our immunocompromised community.

title: Comment from Horne, Adrienne
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001
COVID vaccines should be recommended for people of all ages regardless of health status at least twice a year (every six months) and should have the opportunity to be vaccinated again in spring of 2025. Vaccination not only lowers risks of acute illness, it has also been associated with lower risks of chronic symptoms of Long COVID. All vaccines have shown waning efficacy, while new SARS-CoV-2 strains continue to emerge. My doctor, like many others, has only recommended COVID vaccination sporadically, in contrast to how they regularly recommend the flu vaccine. This is why the CDC needs to clearly recommend COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages to influence healthcare provider recommendations and coverage by health insurance while also increasing public awareness about importance of vaccination every 6 months. As someone who has recently become uninsured, it has been more challenging for me to access the updated vaccine this fall since the CDC's Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024. Please advocate for free vaccine access to make vaccination possible for everyone looking to access this layer of protection for preventing illness.

title: Comment from Lietzau, Amy
comment: Please support two yearly vaccinations for COVID, with each one proceeding the winter/summer peaks. Each year families, especially those with school age children, are left with a hard choice. Get the vaccination before school (to hopefully cover the end of the summer peak) or to wait till later in fall and try and cover the winter peak (to be safe to visit family during the holidays). It's a lose/lose situation because you risk being under protected for both peaks trying to use one yearly vaccination cover everything.

title: Comment from Thurman , Elizabeth
comment:
I am asking that everyone over 6 months be allowed to have TWO Covid vaccines per year, regardless of age or risk category. These requirements cause gatekeeping and confusion about eligibility, in both the public and those who provide vaccinations. I’ve even seen people who were eligible turned away because the pharmacist didn’t understand the guidelines, and people don’t usually go back and try again after ann embarrassing experience like that. Just let people who want them get two vaccines a year. This won’t increase costs that much because few people will actually choose to receive this, but it can still decrease disease burden, symptoms, and suffering amongst those who do.

Stop pandering to the vaccine hesitant by trying to make this Only once a year like the flu.

title: Comment from El-Rady, Ziad
comment: As someone who has had COVID many times. I amo not sure if it's affected my immune system. I'm very grateful to be able to consistently get boosters and for the choices available.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: We keep trying to treat COVID like influenza, assuming that it follows once-a-year infection patterns, and it does not. Since vaccination is pretty much the last tool that we, the public, have in our toolkit and vaccination's effects wane after approx. 4 months, we need to all have access to vaccination every six months, minimum. Universal vaccines/nasal vaccines are a must and we need to be investing more in those options, but with imperfect vaccines, 6 months is key.

title: Comment from Carlson, Patty
comment: Twice a year updated Covid vaccines are necessary for all ages. Very few people masking and most think Covid is now a cold, so it is spreading and mutating freely. People I know are getting it 2-3 times a year. Paxlovid and even Rapid tests are largely unavailable now because people can't afford them. Making vaccines available to all would at least allow those of us still living in reality to have some chance of avoiding hospitalization and death.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Here is one more comment in favor of developing and circulating two SARS-CoV-2 vaccines per year. The fact of waning immunity is well documented [1]. Even if the goal is to prevent hospitalization rather than infection and transmission, the vaccines still grow less effective over time, and the timeline is decidedly less than a year.

Meanwhile there should be more attention spent on researching post-acute sequelae and on developing vaccines that more effectively prevent infection and transmission in the first place. The acute phase of infection is still killing people—per the CDC’s public data about 5,500 people died in the U.S. this August [2]—and the post-acute longterm effects of infection are still dangerous and potentially life-altering or deadly in their own right [3,4] and not reliably prevented by the existing vaccines [5].

title: Comment from Dieperink, Julia
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

As someone with long COVID repercussions, it is extremely important for the health of me and my family for everyone to have access to 2x a year covid vaccines.

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Market Institute
comment:
There are sundry problems with our healthcare system, and, unfortunately, many of our most significant challenges originate with government policies and public health recommendations – well-meaning intentions seldom have the outcome that was designed.

New recommendations to lower the age for routine pneumococcal vaccination from age 65 to 50 is good. Such a policy change will save lives by expanding access to immunizations. Greater access to life-affirming care is a policy move that must be lauded from all quarters of the health care debate.

Greater access, though, is only half of what patients need when it comes to their medicine – greater access must be linked with more choice. Who should decide what medicine is best for a patient? The answer is simple and grounded in good science: patients with their doctors. So, with its decision to back the new recommendation to lower the age for pneumococcal vaccination to 50 and older, ACIP must not pick a ‘winner’ or a ‘loser’ regarding FDA approved vaccines in this health space.

There are two primary FDA-approved vaccines for pneumococcal disease. Health advocates and policy experts are not pushing one over the other, and neither should ACIP. Both vaccines should be included in ACIP’s recommendation to physicians.

It makes no good health-sense for these decisions – the right medicine for the right patient at the right time – to happen anywhere outside of the exam room. ACIP is comprised of the very best minds on vaccine practices. However, gifted physicians and professionals in Atlanta should not be making intimate health decisions for patients in rural North Carolina or the Southside of Chicago. Modern medicine demands greater access (lowering the recommended age) and more choice (recommending both FDA-approved vaccines).

Given the post-COVID distrust many patients hold toward public health experts, ACIP (an agency unaccountable to the public) must not exclude innovations that could save lives. We know vaccines save lives; why limit recommendations on what vaccine a doctor should prescribe? Decisions made at the CDC impact Medicare and private insurance coverage so limiting patients to only one vaccine could impact compliance. ACIP’s recommendation will affect patients – positively or negatively – from coast to coast.

Recommending one vaccine raises several concerns:

-The patient-physician relationship is breached
-Lack of insurance coverage, higher out-of-pocket costs, and non-compliance
-Sufficient supply
-Lack of competition (we know drug costs come down as competition increases)

Greater access and more choice make for good vaccine policy. Patients deserve a healthcare system that encourages competition, allows for innovation, and promotes the doctor-patient relationship. What’s more, ACIP’s decision on pneumococcal vaccinations could help reset the public’s attitude toward the public health community.

Charles Sauer
President
Market Institute

If I had the ability to receive the vaccine multiple times per year, I could have avoided this debilitating illness and my overall quality of life would be improved. I worry that if I get COVID again, for a fifth time, I may never recover.

I am begging the CDC to consider granting access to the COVID vaccine at least twice a year to help high risk people such as myself avoid contracting and spreading COVID.

Thank you for your time.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Please allow anyone to get a Covid booster twice per year, (as opposed to just immunocompromised individuals). There are several reasons this is good policy:
1. We know that Covid boosters wane after several months. Twice per year would provide greater protection.
2. We know there will be multiple Covid waves each year. Allowing twice per year vaccination would allow individuals more flexibility to protect themselves. People can then vaccinate before a wave, or before a life event that requires extra caution (such as caring for a relative with cancer). Rather than once a year vaccination, which cannot be timed to maximize protection.
3. Even people who are not vulnerable often live with or care for vulnerable people. Allowing everyone to vaccinate twice per year provides an extra layer of protection for vulnerable people who interact with non-vulnerable people.
4. The cost-benefit analysis is in favor of allowing greater vaccination. While there are side effects to vaccination, risk is much lower than actually getting Covid.

If you believe that the public as a whole will follow a once per year booster policy more than a twice per year policy, you can continue to recommend boosting once per year for non-vulnerable people. But if you allow people the choice to get a twice per year booster, even if you only recommend once per year, you will still see a benefit among public health conscious individuals, and greater protection for vulnerable people.

Additionally, and especially if you only allow boosters once per year, please time the booster campaign so that people can be boosted before the school year begins. We know that school is a prime vector of transmission, and it is frustrating to have to wait to vaccinate my family and my child until a few weeks or a month after he has already been in school. Timing vaccination to before school would better protect children and their caretakers.

While the acute sickness of Covid has been attenuated due to exposure and partial immunity, there is still a risk of Long Covid and other chronic health issues. We should continue to help people protect themselves, rather than limit their ability to do so.

title: Comment from Imam, Tanya
comment:
COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C).

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024, leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from AH, Sue Ann
comment: I think it would be great for everyone to have access to covid vaccines twice a year and for the vaccines to be updated more frequently!

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: I am writing to ask that ACIP recommend (or at minimum allow), everyone to receive COVID vaccines every 6 months instead of yearly. COVID is not yet following an annual pattern. There are still at least 2 waves a year - one in the summer and one in the winter. We can't force COVID into a box of being like the flu when it isn't the flu. And being immunocompromised isn't the only COVID risk factor. I only have one lung and have asthma, and have received every COVID shot available to me. My mother is receiving chemo for cancer. However, I myself am not considered immunocompromised. And because of that, I have caught COVID twice, both times more than 6 months after my shot. (When I was able to get shots more regularly, I never caught COVID.) My asthma has been worse ever since contracting COVID. Unless you allow anyone who wants it to receive a shot every 6 months, I feel I will be stuck catching COVID once a year, despite doing what you ask and getting vaccinated. And it will still be a long time before we know anyone possible longterm effects of recurring COVID infections.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Please don't discriminate against folks who want to get vaccinated every 4-6 months but don't meet the criteria for "immunocompromised". title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Covid-19 vaccines must be provided for free—accessible to everyone, regardless of insurance status—twice annually. This is what current research has shown to be necessary. This is a major issue of health equity and doing so is the absolute BARE MINIMUM needed to protect our communities. Long-Covid can be severely debilitating and is going to be a major public health crisis for many years to come—if not because you care about human beings and the quality of our lives, please care because the financial burden and healthcare strain it will have to not prevent US citizens from developing Long-Covid.

title: Comment from Courtney, Steph
comment: I strongly advocate for increased access to COVID vaccines for all individuals, especially timely and updated vaccines, multiple times per year if that is what is needed to maintain high levels of immunity and hopefully stop the trend of more and more variants which may have more dangerous impacts or increased transmission rates. As a young (ish) person, I'm trying to plan ahead for decades on this Earth, and every exposure to COVID has the chance of giving me a new disability or health condition that can be dangerous, and likely expensive, in the future. At least as long as we have our unpredictable employment-based healthcare system, it makes a huge difference in my life whether preventative treatments like vaccines are easy to get and widespread. No, I don't have a compromising health condition right now, but I want to keep it that way, and do so while still being able to attend community and cultural events -- effective, accesible vaccines are a major means to do that. I currently have pretty good health insurance, but public access with no out-of-pocket cost keeps me safer! Not to mention, people are more likely to get healthcare (even if they have insurance) if it's simple, public, and free -- again, because of how insurance works, scheduling at the right place and right time to get maximum coverage is hard! Any barriers that reduce public vaccination are a threat to all of us, especially my immunocompromised relatives, who I want to be able to join me in public celebrations and events. Unmitigated COVID, which is what we've seen for the last few years with optional annual vaccines, has created a "variant soup" still getting so many people sick and permanently disabled -- my state has been at "high" or "very high" COVID viral activity for months now. I haven't even referenced all of the relevant studies here, like those that have found similarities between COVID's attack on the immune system and HIV's, because that's y'all's job to review and consider. But as a citizen, I assure you that every piece of evidence about this relatively new, but demonstrably devastating, disease impacts my life and actions, and it is this agency's sole job to protect me from such threats.

title: Comment from Cooke, Isabel
comment: It is well-documented that covid does not follow the typical seasonal pattern of respiratory illnesses like the flu. Although it has respiratory symptoms, it is a vascular disease, and it peaks seasonally in summer as well as winter. Due to the severity of both acute and long-term outcomes and the year-round risk of transmission, it’s critical that updated covid vaccines be provided twice annually.

title: Comment from Miller, Jae
comment: Allow everyone to get a second dose two months after the first as a booster and change the guidelines to a vax every 6 months.

title: Comment from Gatlin, Jennifer
comment: We know that Covid vaccines wane within 6 months, and that Covid is not seasonal but rather a year round threat, therefore it should be allowed to get a Covid vaccine every 6 months rather than yearly.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
COVID vaccine access should be expanded so that immunocompromised individuals can get a second COVID vaccine after two months. The language should also be expanded to allow anyone to get a second COVID vaccine after two months. A COVID vaccine every six months should be the recommendation to limit infection.

As someone who is not currently immunocompromised, but who is in close contact with people who are, I must take steps to avoid getting infected and put them at risk. I should be able to get additional COVID vaccine doses to limit my chances of infection. Limiting this to immunocompromised individuals still leaves them vulnerable to others in their lives. Additionally, infection prevention of the vaccine begins to wane quickly after a few months. Having vaccines recommended twice a year will allow people to have insurance cover a shot every six months, drastically increasing protection from infection year round. Given there is both a large summer and winter wave every year, being able to vaccinate before each makes sense for minimizing spread of the virus and protecting people from potentially life altering Long COVID symptoms.

title: Comment from DANIELS, JOANNA
comment:
I respectfully urge you recommend COVID vaccines for all ages and health statuses at least twice a year (spring vaccine access for all) because with the current vaccines, effectiveness against overall infection falls from 44.4% at one month to 20.7% at six months to 13.4% at nine months. The threat of Long Covid over all ages groups makes effectiveness even more important as “[v]accines partially reduce the risk of Long Covid in adults by 15-70% (~ mean 40%); they partially reduce the risk of Long Covid in kids.”
My interests are not speculative. First, the lack of global access has already denied me timely access although I met the criteria for an additional dose. While the CDC states that self-confirmation is sufficient , the administering pharmacist refused, stating that only chemotherapy and organ transplant patients met those criteria and told me, “You don’t look sick.” I therefore had to do without a boost when the hospitalization of a close relative soon after meant that my daily exposure to viral aerosols increased. In addition, my family includes people with asthma and one or more conditions with increased risks for severe illness from COVID-19. Our situation is common: an estimated 75.4% of U.S. adults have at least one increased-risk condition, 40.3% two or more and 18.5% have three or more.
As non-pharmaceutical interventions have been abandoned or banned, any individual intervention, including vaccination, to reduce the risk of severe Covid-19 and Long Covid is more important than ever. In my home state, Florida, not even physicians or dentists can require masking or testing from even staff under recent legislation and administrative rules, so every office visit or hospitalization brings added risk of healthcare-associated infection (HAI) with SARS-CoV-2. My relatives live in and near areas, North Carolina and Nassau County, New York, now subject to mask bans. These bans, despite some stated exemptions, discourage masking and expose the wearers to substantiated exposure from even a brief unmasking: a mere five (5) seconds can pass an infectious dose and on average, only six hundred (600) inhaled virus particles are necessary to establish an infection.

title: Comment from The People's CDC
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001
From: The People’s CDC, https://peoplescdc.org/
October 17, 2024
COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.
The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].
The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.
The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

comment: Hello! I strongly believe that spring vaccine eligibility should not be restricted to high risk groups and should instead be open to all ages. title: Comment from Guttman, Maxwell
comment:
For years now, there has been consistent evidence that COVID vaccine effectiveness wanes significantly after 4-6 months. There has also been both a summer AND winter surge of COVID cases every year since the pandemic began. One vaccine every 12 months is not effective protection; all of the evidence points to the fact that there needs to be coverage for two vaccines a year, one every six months, in order for people to actually have year-round protection.

Since the end of universal vaccination coverage, it’s become immensely difficult for people who are uninsured to access vaccination. A good friend of mine in Minnesota recently took three buses bus for a nearly 2 hour trip each way to be able to access a free vaccine, because the alternative was paying nearly $200 at any local pharmacy.

The motto for ending COVID mitigations was “we have the tools,“ but the CDC has continued to take away those tools one by one until there are none left available to people who cannot afford to pay for them themselves — and low income people are more likely to be affected by severe and/or long COVID. There are no masks, we get four rapid tests a year if we’re lucky (and those test have a 70% false negative rate), no provided sick leave when infected, an unpaid isolation guidance that has been wildly miscommunicated to employers who think that we get 24 hours and that’s it, and now millions of people are unable to access vaccination. The absolute bare minimum the CDC can do is offer free vaccination for all Americans every 6 months so that everyone can have year round protection.

It would put myself (immunocompromised) and my mother (immunocompromised and over 65+) at even higher risk because while we would be vaccinated if only certain groups were eligible there is a high possibility that two of the other people would bring home covid since they would not be vaccinated.  I want to ensure that my daughter lives a long healthy life and the ability for her to be vaccinated against covid twice a year would help with that.  Please open up 2x yearly vaccines to all!!!!

title: Comment from Johnson, Casey
comment:
As a citizen with vulnerable family members, I believe we need to extend and expand access to COVID vaccines. Every individual vaccinated makes me and the people I love better protected, which means the we need to keep universal vaccine access funded, immune-compromised individuals need to continue to be allowed a COVID vaccine every 2 months, and additional doses need to be available for people over the age of 65.

In addition to maintaining these guidelines, we could improve protection for everyone by implementing more science backed guidelines like increased vaccine frequency as active immunity nearly completely wanes within 3-4 months after receiving a vaccine, more frequent updates to the vaccine as new strains are emerging rapidly, broader definitions of who should follow the vaccine schedule for immune-compromised individuals to include all groups shown to have shortened vaccine efficacy windows, and additional booster doses for pregnant individuals within the third trimester of pregnancy to boost protection for newborns who cannot themselves be vaccinated.

title: Comment from Krausch, Meghan
comment: CDC should approve 2x/yr COVID vaccinations and boosters for people of all ages. We know that COVID is not a seasonal virus, but rather is one that is spread in increased periods of contact and travel. If we didn't know that before for sure, we should certainly be clear after the wave of infections this past summer (including thousands of preventable deaths). It is also clear that the immunity from the vaccines and boosters lasts much less than 12 months. It is time to try to prevent further spread by recommending more regular vaccination; think how many people could have been saved illness, long term disability, and death over summer 2024 by a second spring booster shot. title: Comment from Chan, Vania
comment: While side effects from COVID vaccines are unpleasant (I get temporary heart issues from them), I feel much more protected when I get 2 doses annually. title: Comment from WEHRLE, BECK
comment:
I'm a biology professor and I work with pre-health students and I possess no conflicts of interest.
Everyone needs access to vaccines-- for individual health, for community health, for economic health, for the functioning of our society. Specifically:
1. COVID vaccines need to be universally available for people ages 6 months and up. This is necessary for individual and community protection.
2. Immunocompromised individuals need to continue to be allowed a COVID vaccine every 2 months, and everyone should be encouraged to get vaccinated every 3-4 months. CDC recommendations can be used at point of care to deny vaccination (e.g. I was denied vaccination at 3 months post vaccination because of recommendations that immunocompromised people get vaccinated every 2 months meant I must not be eligible if I didn't look sick.) Any immune compromising condition should be allowed to get a vaccine every 2 months with doctor approval, as even if you’re “mildly” immunocompromised, that can still have catastrophic physiological reprocussions.
The current literature shows that active immunity nearly completely wanes within 3-4 months after receiving a COVID vaccine and there has been no evidence of harm to increasing the option to be vaccinated on a more frequent schedule.
3. We need to keep the 65+ additional dose vaccine recommendations.

Additionally, we need to make additional booster doses available for pregnant individuals within the third trimester of pregnancy. As infants can’t get vaccinated until 6 months, gestational COVID vaccination is their only protection against severe disease through the passage of COVID vaccine antibodies in utero.

Thank you for your time,
Dr. Beck Wehrle

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Vaccination with the latest updated vaccines continues to be foundational to a multilayered approach to COVID, providing protection against both acute disease and Long COVID. Far too few Americans have received the latest vaccines. As of October 11, 2024, only 11.2% of all adults and 26.7% of adults aged 65 and older had received an updated 2024-2025 COVID vaccine. Data for children were unavailable at the time of this writing (October 15, 2024). COVID vaccination rates continue to lag behind influenza vaccination rates. As of July 27, 2024, only 9% of adults aged 65 and older received the recommended two doses of last year’s 2023-2024 vaccine.

Vaccine efficacy wanes significantly four to six months following vaccination, making updated vaccination important for all people as COVID continues to spread in our communities. Vaccine approaches that restrict access based on age or risk status put all of us at risk and leave those at high risk of severe consequences of COVID infection confused about whether they qualify to receive additional doses. These high risk patients may also face barriers as vaccine providers misunderstand the guidelines. A more frequent vaccination approach providing vaccination at least every six months as well as frequent updates to match current variants is needed to better protect all of us amid year-round COVID spread.

Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C). Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status.

The CDC’s Bridge Access Program, which previously provided COVID vaccines to uninsured and underinsured adults free of charge, ended in August 2024. The end of this program without replacement coverage puts people at risk, and public health officials must advocate for free vaccine access for all of us, including those who are uninsured and underinsured.

title: Comment from National Association of Pediatric Nurse Practitioners (NAPNAP)
organization: National Association of Pediatric Nurse Practitioners (NAPNAP)
comment:
The National Association of Pediatric Nurse Practitioners, a professional organization representing 8,000 pediatric-focused nurse practitioners, supports the timely and complete immunization of all eligible infants, children and adults to maximize the health and wellbeing of all people. Although rare, our members have seen first-hand how meningitis can be a potentially serious disease that can quickly cause brain or organ damage, loss of limbs or death.

While there are vaccines for the five strains (A, C, W, Y and B) of meningococcal disease, data as of 2021 suggests that up to 40% of teens are not fully immunized against A, C, W and Y although 2-dose vaccines are recommended at age 11-12 and age 16. Vaccine rates for meningitis B are much lower with only about 3 out of 10 teens being immunized. Meningitis can initially mimic other diseases and viruses such as influenza and COVID delaying the diagnosis and treatment but also the exposure risk to others, so it is imperative to increase meningitis vaccination rates.

NAPNAP appreciates ACIP’s continued review and consideration on how to improve awareness about meningococcal vaccines and increase uptake rates in adolescents and teens. Our experts feel it is critical to simplify and streamline recommendations based on safety and efficacy data.

The various meningococcal vaccines and CDC’s different recommendations, routine vs. shared clinical decision-making, for the vaccines can be confusing to parents and providers. According to a 2019 study published in Vaccine, the majority of surveyed parents were unaware of available vaccines for MenACWY or MenB. Even some clinicians are unsure about ACIP’s recommendations for meningococcal vaccines.

Our members report frustration about the shared decision-making model, saying it is hard to counsel patients to receive a vaccine that is not prioritized at the same level as other child and adolescent vaccines. Having the conversation about one meningitis vaccine, ACWY, being mandatory while another, B, is not negates the seriousness of the more common MenB infections. Based on clinical experience, our members support dosing recommendations that are clear and consistent to encourage uptake while limiting vaccine type and dosage confusion for providers and patient families.

As ACIP considers meningitis recommendations, we encourage the committee to recognize that families with younger adolescents are more likely to adhere to annual well visits and/or seek health care guidance. Therefore, we think it is critical to include meningitis vaccine recommendations for 11-12 years old. We look to the committee to provide strong guidance on how to effectively utilize the available meningitis vaccines (MenACWY, MenB and penta) in a cost-effective manner so that practices can stock and recommend the vaccines for all eligible patients.

Finally, NAPNAP urges the committee to consider how all meningitis vaccines (MenACWY, MenB and penta) can and will be accessible and administered in an equitable way to best protect all adolescents regardless of their families’ ability to pay.

Thank you in advance for showing your commitment to pediatric providers and the patients we serve.

title: Comment from Williams, Connor
comment:
COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Carlton, Lidia
comment:
I'm submitting comment to encourage the ACIP to allow and fully cover two COVID vaccines per year to cover the summer and winter waves. Although I'm writing as an individual, I work in public health and spent over a decade at a state public health department working in infectious disease. At this point in time, the communication around vaccine eligibility and recommendations is very convoluted and the public is confused. By making vaccines available at two distinct points in time, we could enhance public uptake of vaccine. Many people get COVID in the traditional wave periods and the wait for 3 months after infection puts them into a time period where they may forget or find the vaccine unavailable. At minimum, we may be able to increase uptake for those lost, yet motivated people. In addition, those of us who are caretakers for immunocompromised and elder people do not get access to protective vaccines and we could strengthen the safety net around our most at-risk groups.

Without strong evidence that additional vaccines are damaging, withholding effective vaccines from the public is immoral. This is a harm reduction strategy only available to a sub-set of the population and withheld from the rest without clear scientific reasoning. Public health is about groups and considering how to protect the population, but this policy is individualistic and does not make sense since we know vaccines do not fully prevent the acquisition of COVID. We have a moral obligation to prevent the spread of COVID in communities using whatever means possible.

Addendum: since I am currently pregnant, here are just some examples of the studies that are informing my thinking about trying to stay as COVID-safe as humanly possible.

Babies Exposed to COVID in the Womb Are More Likely to Suffer Breathing Problems

Research also suggests that maternal COVID infection may increase the risk that male infants will be diagnosed with a neurodevelopmental disorder such as autism.”

The severity of delays is predominantly mild. 

“fever has been associated in some studies with neural tube defects and other adverse outcomes for a developing baby” - Flu Vaccine Safety and Pregnancy, CDC https://www.cdc.gov/flu/vaccine-safety/vaccine-pregnant.html

COVID-19 diagnosis, vaccination during pregnancy, and adverse pregnancy outcomes of 865,654 women in England and Wales: a population-based cohort study
https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lanepe/article/PIIS2666-7762(24)00204-7/fulltext
Findings
Amongst 865,654 pregnant women, we recorded 60,134 (7%) COVID-19 diagnoses and 182,120 (21%) adverse pregnancy outcomes. COVID-19 diagnosis was associated with
- a higher risk of gestational diabetes (adjusted HR 1.22, 95% CI 1.18–1.26),
- gestational hypertension (1.16, 1.10–1.22),
- pre-eclampsia (1.20, 1.12–1.28),
- preterm birth (1.63, 1.57–1.69, and 1.68, 1.61–1.75 for spontaneous preterm),
- very preterm birth (2.04, 1.86–2.23),
- small for gestational age (1.12, 1.07–1.18),
- thrombotic venous events (1.85, 1.56–2.20),
- stillbirth (only within 14-days since COVID-19 diagnosis, 3.39, 2.23–5.15).
 HRs were more pronounced in the pre-vaccination era, within 14-days since COVID-19 diagnosis, when COVID-19 diagnosis occurred in the 3rd trimester, and in the original variant era. There was no evidence to suggest COVID-19 vaccination during pregnancy was associated with a higher risk of adverse pregnancy outcomes. Instead, dose 1 of COVID-19 vaccine was associated with lower risks of preterm birth, small for gestational age, and stillbirth.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
I’m writing today not only as a member of the public, but as someone who deeply cares about the health and safety of our loved ones, our neighbors, and the most vulnerable in our communities. We’ve all seen the devastating toll that COVID-19 has taken on families and friends, and even now, as the virus continues to unfortunately evolve, the need for protection has never been greater.

The decision to get vaccinated is a choice that protects more than just ourselves — it safeguards everyone around us. This is why I urge the CDC to take a stronger stance: COVID-19 vaccinations must be recommended at bare minimum twice a year, for EVERYONE, regardless of age or health status. Too many people are still being left behind, confused by restrictive guidelines or limited access. We cannot afford to put up barriers that prevent those most at risk from getting the protection they so desperately need.

For families with elderly parents, for those caring for loved ones with compromised immune systems, for the child who could still develop serious complications like MIS-C — these are the people we must keep in our hearts as we make these decisions. Every family should have the peace of mind that comes with knowing their loved ones can receive a COVID-19 vaccine whenever they need it. Every 6 months must be the standard, because we know that vaccine efficacy wanes after just a few months [1-4], and we can’t afford to wait for another surge to act.

But this isn’t just about protection from death. It’s about ensuring that the small moments — family gatherings, birthday parties, even a simple hug — can happen with less fear of acquiring severe illness and long-term disability. Recent research has shown that staying up to date on COVID-19 vaccinations also reduces the risk of Long COVID [5], a condition that has already capsized the lives of millions of people, leaving some unable to work or care for their families.

For many, access to these vaccines remains a challenge. When the CDC’s Bridge Access Program ended in August 2024 [7], it left uninsured and underinsured adults without a clear path to receive these critical vaccines. Imagine the parents, the caregivers, the individuals who want to protect themselves and their loved ones, but can’t because they don’t have access to affordable vaccines. These are the people we need to think about when we make decisions that impact public health. No one should have to choose between protecting their health and paying their bills.

The CDC’s voice carries immense weight. Your clear recommendation for biannual COVID-19 vaccinations would not only guide healthcare providers but also ensure that health insurance covers these critical boosters, making it possible for more people to get vaccinated. We must also advocate for a return of programs like Bridge, so that cost never becomes a barrier to someone getting the protection they deserve.

Please, as you consider the future of COVID-19 vaccination guidance, think about the lives that could be saved, the families that could be spared from grief, and the children who could be shielded from suffering. We are all in this together, and protecting our communities means protecting each other.

Thank you for your dedication and for considering this urgent call to action. Let’s ensure that we are ready to meet the challenges ahead with compassion, equity, and a commitment to keeping all of us safe.

title: Comment from Jones, Emma
comment:
It is imperative that COVID vaccination is available twice a year for people of all ages and health statuses. While it is very important for people aged 65 and over, as well as immunocompromised individuals such as myself, to receive the latest vaccines, it needs to be available to the entire population.

We know that vaccine efficacy and immunity from infection both wane after several months -- about six for vaccines, about three for infection antibodies. Vaccination is associated with a decreased risk for Long COVID after a COVID infection and a decreased risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome. Both of these conditions are life-altering and potentially disabling. COVID vaccines, especially for people under the age of 40, are associated with a lower viral load during infection -- if more people are vaccinated, there will be fewer viral particles spread. There will be fewer disabilities from infection.

As someone who is now disabled with a post-viral autoimmune disorder, after being infected by someone who was not vaccinated, I urge you to make vaccines available to the entire population. It is absurd to think that we can continue ignoring the ongoing pandemic and allow more and more people to accrue post-viral conditions when so much of that is preventable. Part of that is the CDC taking infection seriously, and doing so openly. We must ensure equitable access to vaccines and I ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccines for all. This could deeply reduce barriers of cost and culture hesitancy around vaccines, leading to fewer infections, less severe infections, and fewer cases of Long COVID and other post-viral conditions. We cannot allow our population to continually get reinfected and weakened by this virus.

title: Comment from Reigelman , Jessica
comment:
Scientific evidence indicates updated vaccines should ideally be allowed, available, and fully covered by public funds and/or insurance, for people of all ages at least every six months.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new variants. The CDC’s decision will affect everything about the current and future vaccine approach including what healthcare providers recommend, what health insurance covers, and what the public decides is needed.

Restricting vaccinations to only annual updates misses an opportunity, given that there is the potential to update the vaccines to better match perpetually emerging variants. Updates to all vaccine types are needed, and mRNA vaccines are particularly suited to frequent updates.

The recommendation for only annual vaccination also creates barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics by expanding and indefinitely extending the COVID Vaccine Bridge Access Program [4].

title: Comment from Osman, Marissa
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least every six months must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

Vaccination with the latest updated vaccines continues to be foundational to a multilayered approach to COVID, providing protection against both acute disease and Long COVID. Far too few Americans have received the latest vaccines. As of October 11, 2024, only 11.2% of all adults and 26.7% of adults aged 65 and older had received an updated 2024-2025 COVID vaccine. COVID vaccination rates continue to lag behind influenza vaccination rates. As of July 27, 2024, only 9% of adults aged 65 and older received the recommended two doses of last year’s 2023-2024 vaccine.

Vaccine efficacy wanes significantly four to six months following vaccination, making updated vaccination important for all people as COVID continues to spread in our communities. Vaccine approaches that restrict access based on age or risk status put all of us at risk and leave those at high risk of severe consequences of COVID infection confused about whether they qualify to receive additional doses. These high risk patients may also face barriers as vaccine providers misunderstand the guidelines. A more frequent vaccination approach providing vaccination at least every six months as well as frequent updates to match current variants is needed to better protect all of us amid year-round COVID spread.

Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C). Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status.

The CDC’s Bridge Access Program, which previously provided COVID vaccines to uninsured and underinsured adults free of charge, ended in August 2024. The end of this program without replacement coverage puts people at risk, and public health officials must advocate for free vaccine access for all of us, including those who are uninsured and underinsured.

2. Wu N, Joyal-Desmarais K, Vieira AM, et al. COVID-19 boosters versus primary series: update to a living review. The Lancet Respiratory Medicine. 2023;11(10):e87-e88.

4. Nguyen DC, Hentenaar IT, Morrison-Porter A, et al. SARS-CoV-2-specific plasma cells are not durably established in the bone marrow long-lived compartment after mRNA vaccination. Nat Med. Published online September 27, 2024:1-10.

5. Fang Z, Ahrnsbrak R, Rekito A. Evidence Mounts That About 7% of US Adults Have Had Long COVID. JAMA. Published online June 7, 2024.
