title: Comment from Garvey, Deborah
comment:
Dear ACIP Committee Members,

As a former academic with many friends in the epidemiological community, I ask that you:

— Allow immunocompromised people to get a second COVID vaccine two months after the first vaccine;
— Broaden vaccine advisory language to allow anyone to get a second COVID dose after two months; and
— Recommend all persons get COVID vaccination every six months instead of yearly.

In addition to protecting the most immunocompromised in our communities, COVID vaccine immunity wanes rapidly even in healthy individuals in the face of a rapidly mutating virus. A 6-month vaccine should be the recommended standard.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Please start to right the wrongs that you created by willfully ignoring your responsibility to public health since the vaccines were rolled out in 2021. You need to reverse the damage you caused by letting the “back to normal” fallacy substitute your commitment to disease prevention.

Kids in particular are vulnerable to increasing illness and long covid and shortened lifespans now. Mostly through airborne viral transmission at schools. You have this data, but the CDC persists in sitting back and knowingly allowing kids to have their health sacrificed. Allowing anti scientific attitudes to persist. Attitudes such as, “getting sick with viral pathogens is good for their immune systems.” You are also complicit in the anti vax sentiment because your ill-fated, “vax and relax” campaign helped imply that vaccines stop transmission. Even though that was never a scientific claim, your whole campaign premise and instruction to remove masks after vaccination made people believe the vaccines stopped transmission. So when they saw that vaccines didn’t stop transmission, they just dropped vaccines rather than push health agencies to do better with mitigation efforts.

We all know that covid vaccines help the individual who gets them. They help decrease rates of hospitalization and death. But we also know that once a year is not enough. Covid spikes SEVERAL times a year now. The vaccines wane in efficacy after a few months. That’s simple math to do. Once a year is inadequate. It was a horror show to watch the August/September spike when kids were returning to school and know that nobody (except elderly people), could get a booster.

I remember when CDC director Dr Walensky told the Washington Post that the CDC had a responsibility to the economy. I knew then that you were abdicating your true responsibility, which is to public health. And I have watched that unfold ever since. So many lives have been sacrificed to this false sense of loyalty the CDC has to “the economy”.

At a minimum, you need to officially recommend that everyone get boosted at least twice a year. You should also recommend masks and mandatory quarantine periods for institutions like schools and medical facilities where most of the community outbreaks happen. You should educated the public on what a superspreader event is (concerts, etc), and recommend mitigations. You should recommend air filtration AND monitoring. And I mean a conspicuous recommendation. Not something you quietly bury in the background.

Everything you have been doing since you rolled out your disastrous, “vax and relax” and “covid is over” announcements has been in the service of covering your tracks and not taking accountability for abdicating your duty to public health. Your responsibility isn’t to the business owners and commercial landlords who pressure you. It’s to public health.

We need to be able to get our covid vaccines more often. We should also ALL be given the ability to get RSV vaccines. Not just babies and old people. And you really need to push masking requirements for health facilities and schools. You should also push for robust quarantine periods when someone is sick. School classrooms sound like plague boats now and it’s unacceptable. You also need to come out with a strong debunking of the “immunity debt” myth which has facilitated so much unnecessary illness. We still have hundreds to thousands of Americans dying of covid infections every week. And we have even more Americans than that contracting long term damage from
Covid infections that will lead to disabilities and even premature death down the road after their acute infections have cleared. This number includes children. It is unjust for you to abdicate your responsibility to the public health like this. Even if you helped make masks unpopular and don’t know what to do about that now. You’re not in the business of changing hearts and minds. Just making recommendations in the best interest of public health. So please finally start doing that. We can’t afford for you to continue to fail like this.

title: Comment from Isbell, Donald
comment:
I strongly endorse the concept of 6-month dosing of the Covid 19 vaccines. Not doing so puts our population at risk for more damage than we have already endured. Studies confirm that the vaccines wane after about four months and become much less protective. Public interest in the vaccine has also waned and needs to be countered. It is very concerning that pregnant women are not aware of how important it is that they be vaccinated, as an example of this loss of interest.
Studies show that even mild cases of covid, especially after multiple instances, increases the risk of long covid causing both cognitive and physical damage. Recent studies estimate that one in five children have already been diagnosed with long covid. Covid can affect many organs systems in the body and can linger to cause damage many months after a mild infection.
It is not difficult to see that the intellectual, physical and economic losses from Covid are not behind us and pose a catastrophic danger if not taken seriously and managed studiously. This includes not only vaccination for all according to the CDC age guidelines. We also need clear messaging about how dangerous Covid is, that it is not seasonal or like the flu, encouraging masking when appropriate (health care facilities, confined spaces with more than a few people) and adequate ventilation. There is a great deal of misinformation on social media about vaccines in general and the CDC should find ways to address and counter this.
I appreciate you asking for comments prior to your decision and hope you will take mine to heart.

Also, please allow all people (including immunocompromised) to obtain a Covid vaccine every 6 months.

Covid isn't seasonal. We've been seeing at least two or more waves during a given year. Most recently, August had a wave. August isn't the typical respiratory season. It is not part of winter. I'm harvesting tomatoes in August. That's why we need to be able to get another vaccine six months apart.

Depending on people catching Covid to provide part of the group protection is monstrous because each Covid infection brings with it the danger of that person getting Long Covid and/or having other effects on their heart or brain or other organs. Each infection can cause damage to health. People need to avoid infection, not try to use it as a shield. Catch covid to avoid catching covid is a silly idea. Catching covid causes health issues. So we definitely need the vaccines to be available more than once a year because Covid is not a once a year virus.

And we need it for everyone.

Every time the supposedly immunocompetent people in my office catch Covid, they're out for a week or more (we have adequate sick leave because we're a very specialized bunch). That's a lot of lost work hours that don't ever get recovered. And they usually come back to work and prop themselves up at their desks for a few more days after that.

title: Comment from Bowser, Jennifer
comment: Please provide access to vaccines twice a year for all. The science shows that benefits of vaccination only last for 4-6 months. Please correct the vaccine recommendation to match the current available science.

title: Comment from COPD Foundation
comment: On behalf of the COPD Foundation (COPDF), which represents more than 16 million Americans diagnosed with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and countless more at risk, we again appreciate the opportunity to provide comments to the ACIP. Specifically, we would like to address the recommendations for the Community Acquired Pneumonia (CAP) and the pneumococcal vaccine. The COPDF strongly supports the work of the ACIP and is grateful for the critical efforts required from the group. The COPDF was established to improve the lives of people with COPD, bronchiectasis, and nontuberculous mycobacterial lung disease through initiatives that expand services and speed innovations, which will make treatment more effective and affordable. We do this through scientific research, education, advocacy, and awareness with the goal of disease prevention, slowed progression, and ultimately a cure. We remain concerned that COPD patients, a population of individuals at high risk of CAP, hospitalization for CAP, and adverse outcomes including increased mortality will have less protection from CAP and specifically streptococcal-associated CAP if recommendations for the related vaccines are not simplified and expanded. While well-intentioned, the current CAP recommendations, including shared clinical decision-making, have generated confusion. In addition, under the current recommendations, individuals with chronic underlying conditions including COPD under the age of 65 are not addressed in the recommendations. To address this gap, the COPDF advocates for a simplified age-based recommendation for CAP and pneumococcal vaccination. The age of individuals with a diagnosis of COPD is decreasing and, to address the needs of these individuals, we strongly advocate to also include a risk-based recommendation for individuals with chronic underlying conditions, including COPD, that are under the age-based recommendation. A study from Louisville of adults 40 and older demonstrates an 18 times greater incidence of pneumonia in adults with COPD than in those without COPD. In addition, in a 2020 review article it was determined that, in a long-term study, a diagnosis of COPD is associated with worse outcomes for individuals who also get CAP. Further, individuals with a COPD diagnosis who also have CAP are admitted to the ICU at a rate of 25% compared to 18% for patients without COPD and have exhibited a significantly higher 30- and 90-day mortality rate than individuals without COPD. Overall, individuals with COPD have more CAP, and episodes of CAP are more severe, resulting in more hospitalizations, greater burden, and worse outcomes compared to those without COPD who develop CAP. We understand from the July 2021 ACIP meeting that there has been a demonstrated cost-effectiveness of Prevnar20 without the polysaccharide in both 50+ and 65+ individuals and in Prevnar15 without the polysaccharide in 65+ individuals. Additionally, in the June 2024 ACIP meeting, it was recommended that 21-valent PCV (PCV21) serve as an option for adults aged ≥19 years who are currently recommended to receive PCV15 or PCV20. These actions strongly support the COPDF recommendation that the ACIP implement a simplified age-based CAP vaccine recommendation that also includes a risk-based recommendation for individuals with chronic underlying conditions including COPD who do not meet the age requirement (19-49 or 19-64). Thank you for the opportunity to submit this letter and for your consideration. Now, more than ever, vaccines and protecting lung health are even more essential. We need to ensure that we equip our community with clear CAP vaccine guidelines that incorporate at-risk populations, including those with COPD. (References on attached file).

title: Comment from Gevarter, Laura
comment:
ACIP should:

-Allow immunocompromised people to get a 2nd COVID vaccine 2 months after the first
-Broaden language to allow ANYONE to get a 2nd dose after 2 months
-Recommend COVID vaccines every 6 months instead of yearly

title: Comment from McMasters, Kelly
comment:
It can be easy to forget that it was not that long ago that our entire country shut down due to the destruction and devastation COVID-19 was spreading. At the time, the opportunity to keep our families, friends, coworkers, and neighbors safe was something nearly everyone hoped for. Even though we found differences in how we would achieve that safety, we as a country that tried to work together to protect the most vulnerable.

The support for two COVID-19 boosters a year for all is another effort in that bit of unity. It is not a requirement for anyone, just the opportunity for people to protect themselves and loved ones.

I recently flew home to see my mom who has stage 4 cancer and my nephews who are 1 and 4 yrs old. To prepare for my travels and protect myself and my family, I scheduled a flu shot and COVID booster. I am in between full time employment and while I pay about $160/month for insurance, my healthcare is extremely limited. I was informed a flu shot would cost $37, while a COVID booster would be $200. I had the privilege of having an HSA from my old job that I could use for my flu shot, but the booster was not in my budget. It would be more affordable for me to chance getting COVID and miss a day or two of work than it would be to get the shot. For my family and immunocompromised folks I encountered on my travels, they could be paying a higher price with their health complications.

Free COVID tests are now available through the government, but the preventative care that we have the technology for, comes at an exorbitant cost for an average American. Studies show that reactive care (hospitalizations, vents, etc) are often much more expensive than preventative care (vaccines). If the CDC/government does not feel the moral incentive, I hope the financial savings would be considered.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
I would like to see:

- immunocompromised people allowed to get a 2nd COVID vaccine 2 months after the first
-Broaden language to allow ANYONE to get a 2nd dose after 2 months. With people just starting Novavax, this is really necessary.
-Recommend COVID vaccines every 6 months instead of year. This is not a seasonal virus and so far no available vaccines give us any sort of yearly protection.

title: Comment from Askren, Michael
comment: Epidemiologists and public health officials have been publicly commenting about how poorly the recent spread of H1N1 has been controlled in this country. Namely, they’ve said that the CDC has failed to learn the lessons of the last pandemic. If it isn’t damning enough that every time public comment is available on your meetings, you have citizens and experts telling you how to do your jobs, you now have another potential pandemic on your doorsteps, with none of the willingness or assertiveness to prevent it. I call upon the CDC to facilitate the distribution of vaccinations for California farm workers that are now quickly being infected by this potentially deadly virus.

title: Comment from Radcliffe, Sean
comment:
There was a massive gap between vaccinations available to the general public, such that caregivers and people living with high-risk individuals were only able to get one vaccine from October 2023 to September 2024, a far longer gap than is recommended between doses. The public absolutely does not understand the risks and economic costs of Long COVID, despite the news of its frequency decreasing, and how that decrease is thought to be dependent on continuing population-level participation in receiving vaccinations [8].

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

3. Menegale F, Manica M, Zardini A, et al. Evaluation of Waning of SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine–Induced Immunity: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. JAMA Netw Open. 2023;6(5):e2310650. doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2023.10650

comment:

The benefits from regular, accessible vaccines cannot be understated. Curbing the spread and impact of any disease is beneficial, but especially for one that is showing an alarming uptick in the rates of disability and comorbidities associated with an infection. The American public cannot afford to be disabled at such a widespread rate. We already learned this from such diseases as polio, measles, and AIDs. When vaccines are available for these diseases, they are widely distributed and mandated by public institutions such as schools. When antivirals are available for these diseases, they are subsidized and made widely available to those at the most risk. The same must be done for covid.

Twice a year vaccines, and rapid adjustments/moving targets for the vaccine development must be advocated for and strongly supported by a CDC charged with protecting public health in America. This is your duty to the American public, to do anything less would be to shirk it and betray every single American who place their trust in such a world-renown organization.

title: Comment from C. , N.
comment: I encourage the CDC to make SARSCOV2 vaccinations available at no cost to people of all ages who are in need of them. title: Comment from Wallace, Robin
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

3. Menegale F, Manica M, Zardini A, et al. Evaluation of Waning of SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine–Induced Immunity: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. JAMA Netw Open. 2023;6(5):e2310650. doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2023.10650

4. Nguyen DC, Hentenaar IT, Morrison-Porter A, et al. SARS-CoV-2-specific plasma cells are not durably established in the bone marrow long-lived compartment after mRNA vaccination. Nat Med. Published online September 27, 2024:1-10. doi:10.1038/s41591-024-03278-y

comment:

Additionally, please allocate resources to expedite the study of Covid nasal vaccines so we can get them sooner.

Thank you for reading my comments and thank you for your work.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
I’m commenting about two different items.

The CDC must support affordable and equitable access to the COVID vaccine twice a year. We know immunity from infection is negligible and the vaccine gives us 4-6 months at best. The public deserves access to vaccination regardless of health insurance or health status. As covid evolves, the world must adapt to make sure there is less chance of hospitalization, long term illness, and death.

Additionally, for the H5N1 concerns in the world, with a fatality rate of 50% the virus is a danger to everyone. We must move to vaccinate farm workers and those who are in contact with herds of animals that expose them to illness. H5N1 is becoming a widespread issue, with Japan and the UK escalating warnings of their own danger with this virus. We should not be waiting for action, we should be insisting on precaution and proactive care for the public. We have ruined the covid response and it is a much easier virus to deal with than H5N1. Please stop making the same mistakes. The public deserves protection and access, not handholding and blindfolding. It is an egregious act to sit on your hands and wait for something huge to explode. That will only make things worse in the long run. Please think of our future- of the elderly, the children, the disabled and immunocompromised. It is not okay to wait. We must act now and maintain the safety and health of the world.

title: Comment from Miller, Ashley
comment:
I write as someone who cares deeply about the health and safety of everyone around me – my family, coworkers, friends, and neighbors alike. Though I wish it were not so, SARS-CoV-2 continues to be a deadly and difficult disease, no matter one’s age or health status. Vaccines protect not only ourselves, but those around us, from the worst of this illness. Therefore, ensuring widespread availability of vaccines is critical, as is establishing a vaccine schedule that provides us all the very best protection possible.

We now know that the efficacy of SARS-CoV-2 vaccines wane after 1-3 months regardless of age or health status, and new SARS-CoV-2 strains continue to emerge year-round. Up-to-date vaccines help mitigate the likelihood of people experiencing long COVID, which can be so debilitating and isolating. It is clear now that a twice-annual (every six months) vaccination schedule is smart for everyone – not just those over the age of 65 or who are immunocompromised. People of all ages and health statuses should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will be hugely impactful. It will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, coverage by health insurance, and public awareness about regular and timely vaccination as one part of an effective illness prevention strategy. Please support a twice-annual COVID vaccine schedule and ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines without limited access due to financial constraints or demographics.

title: Comment from Smalley, Ruth
comment: Please support better public health by promoting available Covid 19 vaccines more than once a year. We already have a decreasing rate of uptake on the vaccines because of poor messaging, and while death rates have declined, infection and long Covid rates are still unacceptably high. People who do wish to have greater protection against the virus should be able to have more than one annual vaccine, especially because it is well-known that efficacy wanes long before the passage of a year. Multiple circulating variants make it highly possible for vulnerable people to be exposed repeatedly throughout the year. This is not a seasonal virus, and many immune-compromised people have had their quality of life, their activities, and their mental health affected by the very real risks posed by a serious and still largely unchecked virus. With fewer people testing, almost no one masking, and unrealistic guidance that has shortened isolation periods, the very young and the elderly are continuously exposed to risk. Caregivers and people who must travel for work reasons may have a strong need to receive another vaccination long before a year elapses. This is a very practical consideration; I'm aware of many accounts of people who have been unable to obtain another vaccination on a timely basis. Why withhold this health measure from those who are willing to seek it out? Thank you for this opportunity to offer comments.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Continued access to current and new COVID vaccines is essential for maintaining quality of life for all Americans and protecting against creating a permanent underclass of immunocompromised and high-risk citizens.

My partner was hospitalized after coming down with COVID in 2023 and diagnosed with multiple blood clots in their lungs. They have still not recovered stamina and mobility since that hospitalization 18 months ago. We have to take safeguards to prevent re-infection and one of those safeguards is regular vaccination. Loss of access to those vaccinations means they don't get to socialize, work, or even do something as simple as go to the grocery store without risk of permanent harm or death. If vaccines are unavailable or only available via purchase, their life and the lives of millions of Americans get significantly worse.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
I am writing to demand that H5N1 vaccines be authorized and recommended to farmworkers and more. This must not become another global pandemic. We must do everything we can to stop the spread of disease that disproportionately affects workers.

ACIP should recommend the authorization of the use for H5N1 vaccines for farmworkers and others facing occupational risk of H5N1 exposure and infection. While the CDC is recommending seasonal flu vaccines for farmworkers to reduce the risk of human flu viruses mixing with H5N1, the seasonal vaccine does not provide protection against H5N1. As we head into fall and winter flu season, if H5N1 swaps genes with a human flu virus it raises the risk of a more transmissible and virulent strain that could spread as easily among people as seasonal flu viruses do. The US should be proactively taking action to offer the vaccine for those facing the highest risk, and precedent already exists in the case of Finland, which has already moved to vaccinate farmworkers at risk to prevent the risk of severe illness and death with a virus that has historically had over a 50% fatality rate in humans.
In the past year or two, the virus has quickly adapted to spread among a growing range of mammals and is now widespread in dairy cattle in the United States and most recently has found a foothold in dairy farms in California, the largest dairy producer in the nation. With the growing number of dairy farms with H5N1 cases and little sign of this outbreak being controlled, farmworkers are at increased risk and this opens up the possibility of the likelihood of a strain that is better at infecting and spreading between humans. There has been little testing and a lack of transparency in data and information around H5N1 from both the USDA and CDC and we should not wait to take action until we see even more clusters of infection. Vaccines should be authorized and made available to dairy and poultry farmers, those working in slaughterhouses, and others facing occupational risk of exposure. In the words of Rick Bright, former Deputy Assistant Secretary for Preparedness and Response and the Director of the Biomedical Advanced Research and Development Authority (BARDA),“There’s no such thing as just a little conjunctivitis or just a little respiratory (issue) when you’re dealing with a deadly virus. The virus has already demonstrated the ability to mutate very easily, very quickly, and it can cause severe illness and death. So let’s stop it in its tracks, not wait and see and let it get worse.”

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
The CDC must support equitable and affordable access to at least twice a year COVID vaccination for people of all ages, regardless of health insurance status. The CDC should also authorize the use of vaccines for H5N1 for people exposed to increased risk of H5N1 due to occupational risk to reduce the likelihood of severe disease and death from the virus. For H5N1, these groups would include people working in dairy, slaughterhouses, or poultry farms, local communities affected by H5N1 outbreaks, and others working closely with animals or the virus. The H5N1 vaccine should be made freely available for those at high-risk, especially as we enter the seasonal flu season, increasing the possibility that the H5N1 virus may reassort with a human seasonal flu into a more transmissible and dangerous virus.
Additionally, with the COVID-19 Bridge Program ending earlier this year, those who are uninsured no longer have access to COVID Vaccines at no cost, exacerbating vaccine inequities. As COVID-19 continues to evolve, it is essential that communities have access to the most up-to-date vaccines to prevent the likelihood of hospitalization, severe disease, and death. Vaccines should be affordable and accessible to all and we call on you to take action to ensure that those who are uninsured or underinsured have access to updated COVID Vaccines.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Please vote 'yes' to allow additional vaccines per year for all individuals. While Covid-19 is not as deadly as it was in the acute stages, Long Covid is an emerging crisis that must be addressed by health leadership. As of this writing, Long Covid still has no approved treatments or cures, and the current best method to prevent Long Covid is to not be infected with Covid. Avoiding Covid infection completely is becoming increasingly difficult, and is not a sustainable or achievable goal for most people.

According to leading experts (paper attached), Long Covid may affect as many as 400 million people globally, and cost the economy $1 trillion annually. Long Covid risk also increases with every Covid infection, so the numbers infected and cost to the global economy will continue to grow unless we find a way to substantially reduce transmission or find a treatment or cure.

Until we reach this time, vaccines are a good tool that is currently available to us. Regular vaccinations are safe and seem to reduce the likelihood of Long Covid. The current vaccines offer heightened protection for several months, but currently most people can only access them once a year. Everyone is vulnerable to Long Covid, not just the elderly and immune compromised. It would be in the best interest of public and economic health to allow and encourage additional vaccines as protection wears off. It would also be ideal for the CDC to better advertise and encourage vaccine uptake, as well as educate all Americans about their vulnerability to Long Covid so long as the virus persists.

comment: Please make covid vaccines available to everyone every six months. Vaccine effectiveness wanes quickly, and covid is at an elevated level throughout the year. My sister and I live with and care for our elderly parents who have multiple serious health issues including cancer and diabetes, and my sister and I have our own health issues that make us all more vulnerable to serious outcomes from covid infections. title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Others have said it better than I can, we need more help and help more often in various forms. In this matter, the vaccines must be made available to anyone who is willing to get them.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Hello,

I became chronically ill by covid in 2020. In three years I barely managed to finish the master's degree in social work I had just begun. My second infection in 2022 brought on more long-term issues.

Up-to-date covid vaccinations are an incredibly important tool to mitigating the risk to covid and its spread, thus mitigating long covid issues. My daily life is quite limited by long covid issues, and I limit my socializing not only to manage my energy limitations but to minimize exposure risk to covid. Having twice a year vaccinations targetted at the quickly mutating virus and its many variants could help expand my activities of daily living, possibly even begin working again. There is great need for social workers, yet I am not able to find accommodating work because of my health limitations. I imagine there are many other people like me in a wide range of professions whose participation in the work force is limited due to long covid. This isn't even addressing the extra burden placed on healthcare.

Notice how often I used forms of "limit" in the previous paragraph? Now, covid vaccines are not the silver bullet we used to think it would be, but it is a powerful tool among several we could use to better manage covid infections (universal masking, accessible testing and treatment, supported sick time off, cleaner air, etc). With how quickly the virus mutates, having twice a year updated vaccines can make a huge difference. It is also important to open avenues to making those vaccines accessible: adequate marketing on availability and benefits of doing so, making it affordable or free.

You hold great power. Thank you for your consideration.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
ACIP should recommend the authorization of the use for H5N1 vaccines for farmworkers and others facing occupational risk of H5N1 exposure and infection. While the CDC is recommending seasonal flu vaccines for farmworkers to reduce the risk of human flu viruses mixing with H5N1, the seasonal vaccine does not provide protection against H5N1. As we head into fall and winter flu season, if H5N1 swaps genes with a human flu virus it raises the risk of a more transmissible and virulent strain that could spread as easily among people as seasonal flu viruses do. The US should be proactively taking action to offer the vaccine for those facing the highest risk, and precedent already exists in the case of Finland, which has already moved to vaccinate farmworkers at risk to prevent the risk of severe illness and death with a virus that has historically had over a 50% fatality rate in humans.

In the past year or two, the virus has quickly adapted to spread among a growing range of mammals and is now widespread in dairy cattle in the United States and most recently has found a foothold in dairy farms in California, the largest dairy producer in the nation. With the growing number of dairy farms with H5N1 cases and little sign of this outbreak being controlled, farmworkers are at increased risk and this opens up the possibility of the likelihood of a strain that is better at infecting and spreading between humans. There has been little testing and a lack of transparency in data and information around H5N1 from both the USDA and CDC and we should not wait to take action until we see even more clusters of infection. Vaccines should be authorized and made available to dairy and poultry farmers, those working in slaughterhouses, and others facing occupational risk of exposure. In the words of Rick Bright, former Deputy Assistant Secretary for Preparedness and Response and the Director of the Biomedical Advanced Research and Development Authority (BARDA),“There’s no such thing as just a little conjunctivitis or just a little respiratory (issue) when you’re dealing with a deadly virus. The virus has already demonstrated the ability to mutate very easily, very quickly, and it can cause severe illness and death. So let’s stop it in its tracks, not wait and see and let it get worse.”

title: Comment from Vishnoi , Manal
comment:
As a country, we need to provide equitable healthcare to every community. To this end, the CDC must support equitable and affordable access to at least twice a year COVID vaccination for people of all ages, regardless of health insurance status. The CDC should also authorize the use of vaccines for H5N1 for people exposed to increased risk of H5N1 due to occupational risk to reduce the likelihood of severe disease and death from the virus. For H5N1, these groups would include people working in dairy, slaughterhouses, or poultry farms, local communities affected by H5N1 outbreaks, and others working closely with animals or the virus. The H5N1 vaccine should be made freely available for those at high-risk, especially as we enter the seasonal flu season, increasing the possibility that the H5N1 virus may reassort with a human seasonal flu into a more transmissible and dangerous virus.

Additionally, with the COVID-19 Bridge Program ending earlier this year, those who are uninsured no longer have access to COVID Vaccines at no cost, exacerbating vaccine inequities. As COVID-19 continues to evolve, it is essential that communities have access to the most up-to-date vaccines to prevent the likelihood of hospitalization, severe disease, and death. Vaccines should be affordable and accessible to all and we call on you to take action to ensure that those who are uninsured or underinsured have access to updated COVID Vaccines.

title: Comment from Yandell, Alex
comment:
Covid is still a great public risk that harms people every day. Vaccines should be provided for free as often as they are available. COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6]. The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
ACIP should recommend the authorization of the use for H5N1 vaccines for farmworkers and others facing occupational risk of H5N1 exposure and infection. While the CDC is recommending seasonal flu vaccines for farmworkers to reduce the risk of human flu viruses mixing with H5N1, the seasonal vaccine does not provide protection against H5N1. As we head into fall and winter flu season, if H5N1 swaps genes with a human flu virus it raises the risk of a more transmissible and virulent strain that could spread as easily among people as seasonal flu viruses do. The US should be proactively taking action to offer the vaccine for those facing the highest risk, and precedent already exists in the case of Finland, which has already moved to vaccinate farmworkers at risk to prevent the risk of severe illness and death with a virus that has historically had over a 50% fatality rate in humans. It is unacceptable to put people at risk like this.

In the past year or two, the virus has quickly adapted to spread among a growing range of mammals and is now widespread in dairy cattle in the United States and most recently has found a foothold in dairy farms in California, the largest dairy producer in the nation. With the growing number of dairy farms with H5N1 cases and little sign of this outbreak being controlled, farmworkers are at increased risk and this opens up the possibility of the likelihood of a strain that is better at infecting and spreading between humans. There has been little testing and a lack of transparency in data and information around H5N1 from both the USDA and CDC and we should not wait to take action until we see even more clusters of infection. Vaccines should be authorized and made available to dairy and poultry farmers, those working in slaughterhouses, and others facing occupational risk of exposure. In the words of Rick Bright, former Deputy Assistant Secretary for Preparedness and Response and the Director of the Biomedical Advanced Research and Development Authority (BARDA),“There’s no such thing as just a little conjunctivitis or just a little respiratory (issue) when you’re dealing with a deadly virus. The virus has already demonstrated the ability to mutate very easily, very quickly, and it can cause severe illness and death. So let’s stop it in its tracks, not wait and see and let it get worse.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
The CDC must support equitable and affordable access to at least twice a year COVID vaccination for people of all ages, regardless of health insurance status. The CDC should also authorize the use of vaccines for H5N1 for people exposed to increased risk of H5N1 due to occupational risk to reduce the likelihood of severe disease and death from the virus. For H5N1, these groups would include people working in dairy, slaughterhouses, or poultry farms, local communities affected by H5N1 outbreaks, and others working closely with animals or the virus. The H5N1 vaccine should be made freely available for those at high-risk, especially as we enter the seasonal flu season, increasing the possibility that the H5N1 virus may reassort with a human seasonal flu into a more transmissible and dangerous virus.

Additionally, with the COVID-19 Bridge Program ending earlier this year, those who are uninsured no longer have access to COVID Vaccines at no cost, exacerbating vaccine inequities. As COVID-19 continues to evolve, it is essential that communities have access to the most up-to-date vaccines to prevent the likelihood of hospitalization, severe disease, and death. Vaccines should be affordable and accessible to all and we call on you to take action to ensure that those who are uninsured or underinsured have access to updated COVID Vaccines.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
I'm writing to voice my support of the CDC updating guidelines to include two covid vaccines a year (one fall dose and one spring dose) for all members of the public regardless of age or pre-existing conditions.

The reasoning for this is primarily because Covid is a year long virus - it's not seasonal. Additionally, due to how quickly covid mutates, our once a year model has quickly become outdated and cannot keep up with new variants. By the time the yearly fall vaccine comes out, it's already been eclipsed by a newer variant and loses its potency. Furthermore, considering that our current updated vaccines also only offer peak protection for around 3 months before they begin to wane, the combination of all this leaves the public at large vulnerable for the majority of the year. Not only does this contribute to increased case numbers and hospitalizations, but it also allows more room for the virus to mutate, which begins the cycle all over again.

In order to prevent covid from continuing to be a major threat to public health, we need vaccines to be available at two doses per year with more frequent updates to keep up with mutating variants. This protects not only our health care workers and other frontline and essential workers, but it also protects our most vulnerable populations and everyone in between.

Thank you for your consideration in this matter.

PLEASE keep people like me in mind (and our healthier loved ones who need to keep us safe) by not preventing our access to 2-3 Novavax doses per year. Thank you.

title: Comment from Goldberg, Natalie
comment:
Dear ACIP Members,

I want to express my appreciation for the critical role the ACIP has played in guiding the nation’s vaccination strategy during the COVID-19 pandemic. As we continue to confront emerging variants and waning immunity, I respectfully urge the committee to consider adjustments to the current COVID-19 vaccination guidelines to better protect vulnerable populations and improve flexibility for the broader public.

I ask that the ACIP consider the following recommendations:

1.Allow a second vaccine dose after two months for immunocompromised individuals. Immunocompromised people remain at high risk for severe disease despite vaccination. Providing an option for a second dose two months after the first could offer vital, timely protection, especially during periods of increased transmission or when immunity wanes.
2.Extend the two-month second dose option to all individuals. Many individuals, such as healthcare workers or those with frequent exposure risks, could benefit from the flexibility to receive a second dose after two months if necessary. This would empower individuals to make decisions based on their personal risk and circumstances, enhancing overall public health without mandating additional doses for everyone.
3.Recommend COVID-19 vaccination every six months. Evidence suggests that immunity from COVID-19 vaccines declines more rapidly than initially anticipated, especially with new variants. A six-month booster schedule would more effectively maintain protective immunity and reduce the risk of severe illness, rather than relying on annual vaccinations.

In addition to these immediate adjustments, I would also encourage further efforts in the following areas:

4.Improve accessibility to Novavax. Novavax has proven to be a valuable option, particularly for those who may not tolerate or prefer mRNA vaccines. Ensuring that this vaccine remains widely available will give individuals more options and could help increase vaccination uptake.
5.Accelerate research into nasal vaccines like Castlevax and Novavax. Nasal vaccines, especially non-live attenuated versions, hold promise in providing broader mucosal immunity and potentially reducing transmission. Urgent exploration of these vaccines, in conjunction with intramuscular vaccines, could offer a more comprehensive approach to pandemic control.

I understand the complexity of the decisions you face, and I deeply appreciate your ongoing efforts to adapt our public health strategies to the evolving nature of the virus. Thank you for considering these recommendations, which I believe can enhance protection for all individuals while providing much-needed flexibility in our vaccination strategy.

Sincerely,
Natalie Goldberg

I have to travel to see my family, which includes both older parents with increased vulnerability to severe outcomes from COVID infection, and young children, who may be at increased risk of diabetes due to COVID [4]. It is stressful to manage my emotional ties with my family while trying to ensure that I do not increase their COVID exposure and potential serious negative outcomes. If my whole family, not just my parents, had access to more frequent vaccinations, it would be healthier for all of us.

With COVID demonstrating a pattern of at least two substantial spikes per year nationwide over the past several years and continual mutations of the virus, it is also critical to public health for vaccines to be updated more frequently. The case for increasing updates and expanding access to more frequent vaccination is clearly emerging: waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [5] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [6] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [7].

A clear and unequivocal recommendation from the CDC of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. It will also improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

Finally, it is absolutely crucial to public health that the CDC ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The end of the CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program in August 2024 [8] left many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. Ensuring that as many people as possible have access to vaccination is essential to our socioeconomic well-being.

Please advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers to and hesitation about vaccination, and recommend COVID vaccines for all ages and health statuses at least twice a year, as well as more frequent updates to the vaccines, adjusted for the latest variants.

title: Comment from B, Rhiannon
comment:
ACIP should recommend the authorization of the use for H5N1 vaccines for farmworkers and others facing occupational risk of H5N1 exposure and infection. While the CDC is recommending seasonal flu vaccines for farmworkers to reduce the risk of human flu viruses mixing with H5N1, the seasonal vaccine does not provide protection against H5N1. As we head into fall and winter flu season, if H5N1 swaps genes with a human flu virus it raises the risk of a more transmissible and virulent strain that could spread as easily among people as seasonal flu viruses do. The US should be proactively taking action to offer the vaccine for those facing the highest risk, and precedent already exists in the case of Finland, which has already moved to vaccinate farmworkers at risk to prevent the risk of severe illness and death with a virus that has historically had over a 50% fatality rate in humans.
In the past year or two, the virus has quickly adapted to spread among a growing range of mammals and is now widespread in dairy cattle in the United States and most recently has found a foothold in dairy farms in California, the largest dairy producer in the nation. With the growing number of dairy farms with H5N1 cases and little sign of this outbreak being controlled, farmworkers are at increased risk and this opens up the possibility of the likelihood of a strain that is better at infecting and spreading between humans. There has been little testing and a lack of transparency in data and information around H5N1 from both the USDA and CDC and we should not wait to take action until we see even more clusters of infection. Vaccines should be authorized and made available to dairy and poultry farmers, those working in slaughterhouses, and others facing occupational risk of exposure. In the words of Rick Bright, former Deputy Assistant Secretary for Preparedness and Response and the Director of the Biomedical Advanced Research and Development Authority (BARDA),“There’s no such thing as just a little conjunctivitis or just a little respiratory (issue) when you’re dealing with a deadly virus. The virus has already demonstrated the ability to mutate very easily, very quickly, and it can cause severe illness and death. So let’s stop it in its tracks, not wait and see and let it get worse.

title: Comment from B, Rhiannon
comment:
The CDC must support equitable and affordable access to at least twice a year COVID vaccination for people of all ages, regardless of health insurance status. The CDC should also authorize the use of vaccines for H5N1 for people exposed to increased risk of H5N1 due to occupational risk to reduce the likelihood of severe disease and death from the virus. For H5N1, these groups would include people working in dairy, slaughterhouses, or poultry farms, local communities affected by H5N1 outbreaks, and others working closely with animals or the virus. The H5N1 vaccine should be made freely available for those at high-risk, especially as we enter the seasonal flu season, increasing the possibility that the H5N1 virus may reassort with a human seasonal flu into a more transmissible and dangerous virus.
Additionally, with the COVID-19 Bridge Program ending earlier this year, those who are uninsured no longer have access to COVID Vaccines at no cost, exacerbating vaccine inequities. As COVID-19 continues to evolve, it is essential that communities have access to the most up-to-date vaccines to prevent the likelihood of hospitalization, severe disease, and death. Vaccines should be affordable and accessible to all and we call on you to take action to ensure that those who are uninsured or underinsured have access to updated COVID Vaccines.

title: Comment from Dunkle, Bela
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

References:
1. Link-Gelles R. Effectiveness of COVID-19 (2023-2024 Formula) vaccines. Presented at: FDA VRBPAC Meeting; June 5, 2024. Accessed June 12, 2024. https://www.fda.gov/media/179140/download

2. Wu N, Joyal-Desmarais K, Vieira AM, et al. COVID-19 boosters versus primary series: update to a living review. The Lancet Respiratory Medicine. 2023;11(10):e87-e88. doi:10.1016/S2213-2600(23)00265-5

3. Menegale F, Manica M, Zardini A, et al. Evaluation of Waning of SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine–Induced Immunity: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. JAMA Netw Open. 2023;6(5):e2310650. doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2023.10650

4. Nguyen DC, Hentenaar IT, Morrison-Porter A, et al. SARS-CoV-2-specific plasma cells are not durably established in the bone marrow long-lived compartment after mRNA vaccination. Nat Med. Published online September 27, 2024:1-10. doi:10.1038/s41591-024-03278-y

title: Comment from Walden, Robin
comment:
I urge the CDC to vote on making 2 shots of the COVID-19 vaccines, plus a possible third shot, available for anyone who wishes to get two, or possibly three shots. There are many reasons this approach makes a lot of sense.
* It is smart to support and encourage the general public health policy of getting vaccinated.
* Covid-19 has proven to NOT be a seasonal virus infection. The public needs more than one shot a year to build adequate immune response since coverage wanes relatively quickly, especially with only one shot.
* Research continues to support vaccination has a positive impact on lowering the odds of a COVID infection causing acute illness and even of developing Long COVID in many individuals.
* Commercial insurance companies will pay for additional shots if the CDC approves and recommends the multi-shot series. This is especially important for immune priming or for those who are medically vulnerable or the aged. I am old, have chronic medical conditions that increase the likelihood of a poor outcome if I get infected, and I have had a post-vital syndrome before. I need a second Novavax shot this. Year to complete a primary series and would like to get a third in the first half of next year.
* These vaccines were developed with our taxpaying dollars. They have expiration dates. They should be made more easily available to those who want them. And restart the Bridge program as well!
* With other infectious and contagious diseases on the rise that could tax our limited resources, we as a nation, and the CDC, should be eager to minimize the continuing impact of Covid-19 on individuals, the medical system, and the economy.
* Adequate vaccine access is one of the few tools available until sterilizing vaccines, intranasal/mucosal vaccines, or pan-corona virus vaccines become available to the public.

Please do not miss this opportunity to optimize the resources we currently have available in our ongoing fight to protect our nation from the known and yet to be discovered ravages of SarsCoV2 infections. Make multiple shots of all COVID-19 vaccines available to all who want them.

Thank you,
Robin Walden

title: Comment from Lazaris, Tim
comment:
COVID-19 continues to be a deadly and persistent threat, and the risks and impacts of contracting the virus remain largely unknown. What we do know about its effects is terrifying. Long-term impacts will not be fully understood for years, perhaps even decades. In the meantime, denying people access to updated vaccines is both irresponsible and dangerous. The global impact of restricted vaccine access will be far more serious than any argument against regular, updated vaccinations.

We've lost the possibility of herd immunity, with drastically declining vaccine rates. On top of that, people are no longer masking properly, and some places are even going so far as to ban masks. This is not only irresponsible—it’s idiotic. With vaccine and mask rates dropping, one of the few reliable ways left to protect ourselves from a growing risk in the population is to stay vaccinated. Receiving the vaccine regularly, at least twice a year, ensures we stay optimally protected against infection and remain up to date with the latest variants and strains.

To safeguard public health, it’s critical that the CDC recommends COVID vaccines for all ages, at least every six months, and supports more frequent updates to match new variants. This approach will not only protect individuals but also reduce the overall burden on the healthcare system, especially as we face the uncertain long-term impacts of this virus.

title: Comment from Slaughter, Samantha
comment:
COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.
The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6]. 
The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.
The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Trawitzki, Kaila
comment:
The risks of COVID-19, especially with repeated infections, are well-documented and serious.

Vaccines significantly reduce the likelihood of severe outcomes, including disability and death.

COVID weakens immune systems, meaning that eventually, everyone falls into the vulnerable population.

Current vaccine protection peaks after one month and wanes considerably by four months, leaving minimal protection.

Even during this short window, vaccines only offer about 50% protection against infection.

Therefore, I urge you to provide access to COVID vaccines for all age groups at least twice a year.

Additionally, it is essential that medical facilities nationwide require respirator masks at all times to protect staff and patients.

It’s unacceptable that the CDC has yet to implement these critical measures.

You have the opportunity to rectify past mistakes with decisive action, not the inaction we’ve grown accustomed to from the agency tasked with disease CONTROL.

Please take back control and make the right choice for public health.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
This is to request an update to covid vaccine recommendations and authorization to allow anyone to get a covid shot every 6 months or twice a year, regardless of age and health status. Larger covid waves have been happening twice a year, and vaccine effectiveness erodes after 4-6 months. While the vaccines are intended to prevent severe illness and death, they do also provide some increased protection for short periods of time against symptomatic infection. This helps prevent long covid, which impacts the entire population, not only the elderly and immunocompromised. It is important to offer the option for people to be able to better protect themselves against disability as well as severe disease and death, both from a humane standpoint and from an economic standpoint. It is also very important that those who are living with or caregivers or otherwise in close contact with higher risk people have an option to get a second seasonal shot at 6 months if they want one, so that they can further protect their loved ones. This extends to families expecting births, and there should be a recommendation for women in their third trimester to get a covid vaccine to protect themselves and their fetuses.

In addition, increased access should include both authorizing the shot for everyone over 6 months and providing more funding for the shots to be covered for the uninsured. With low uptake rates, we should be doing everything we can to eliminate barriers for people who want covid vaccines. Populations who can't afford the vaccines are also most likely to not be able to take time off when sick (hurting both that person and further spreading covid) or afford the medical bills associated with a severe case of covid, and so prioritizing equity is important for all.

Finally, if possible, more frequent updates to covid vaccine formulations to target the newest strains should also be considered.

title: Comment from Kinsler, Lauren

title: Comment from Cedar, Denise
comment:
RE: Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

TO: CDC

I am a retired allied health professional, now disabled and "extremely" (my physician’s term) immunocompromised due to immune-suppression infusions monthly. Thankfully, I do meet the criteria to receive a COVID vaccine twice yearly, yet the optimal time frame for my health status is very 3 months per my immunologist. I appreciate the CDC guidance allowing this and will be doing it.

In this context, I am writing to encourage CDC to recommend twice yearly COVID vaccination to other people of all ages and every immune status.

Currently, I am not safe socializing with my healthier friends once their vaccine has become less effective/ineffective due to the passage of time.

I am also not safe at in-person healthcare appointments now that the CDC does not recommend masking in healthcare settings. My providers, who resist my request to mask by stating “the CDC doesn’t require it now”, are also not well-vaccinated enough to, at a minimum, spill less virus in my airspace. Your recommendation for COVID vaccination every 6 months will make healthcare safer for everyone – patients, families and the healthcare providers.

Research has established that all types of COVID vaccines have waning efficacy in the months following vaccination. [1-3] Recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months addresses the constant emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains. Recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs for everyone, of all ages regardless of immune status.

When/if the CDC supports COVID vaccination every 6 months it will improve public awareness in people of all ages and all societal roles re the importance of recent vaccination for the best protection.

Recent SARS-CoV2 vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6]. Long COVID and conditions such as MIS-C seriously affect the future health of the American population. An unhealthy population weakens the stability and strength of a nation.

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. This is an essential aspect of controlling disease and keeping our population healthy.

Wide access to updated vaccines and prevention of limited access due to financial constraints or demographics must also be addressed. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and under-insured adults without COVID vaccine access. I, personally, had several people tell me they weren’t able to afford the vaccine now, although they wanted it.

Therefore, CDC must advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all to reduce barriers and lessen hesitation re vaccination. Health is a human right and essential care such as COVID vaccination should not depend upon an individual’s wealth, demographics, or location.

Please ensure that COVID vaccine access is also equitable for Indigenous communities through the Indian Health Service as well as through Medicaid clinics for under-served populations in all 50 states and the US territories.

Thank you for considering these requests and acting upon them to protect the health of all and thus the strength of our nation.

Regards,
Denise Cedar, RDN-retired

title: Comment from Bernish-Fisher, Kathryn
comment:
We are approaching the five-year mark since the first official COVID-19 case in the US. The data is clear. COVID-19 is not “seasonal.” COVID-19 is not the flu. And Long COVID is a profoundly disabling condition with devastating impacts on children and adolescents, adults in their 20s, 30s and 40s…. And affecting women more than men. An “annual” vaccination schedule that focuses on “flu season” timing is ineffective and ignores the accumulating evidence that shows we need a COVID-19 specific public health strategy.
The universal recommendation should be evolved to include clear guidance:
-Recommending / allowing immunocompromised people to get a 2nd COVID vaccine 2 months after the first
-Broaden language to allow ANYONE to get a 2nd dose after 2-4 months depending on their health status - with emphasis on people with underlying medical conditions and other risk factors (not just the arbitrary age marker of over 65)
-Recommending COVID vaccines every 6 months instead of yearly

All viruses transmitted via respiration pose their distinct risks. But clearly COVID-19 is a virus more like an airborne HIV with its multiple impacts on the immune system and causing damage to vascular cells, inflammation of the brain stem - and acting like an accelerant of other chronic conditions (like CVD and diabetes and dementia / cognitive decline).

We need clarity and people need to understand the urgency to integrate COVID-19 vaccination as a regular and frequent healthcare strategy.

title: Comment from Yau-Weeks, Sophia
comment:
The CDC must support equitable and affordable access to at least twice a year COVID vaccination for people of all ages, regardless of health insurance status. The CDC should also authorize the use of vaccines for H5N1 for people exposed to increased risk of H5N1 due to occupational risk to reduce the likelihood of severe disease and death from the virus. For H5N1, these groups would include people working in dairy, slaughterhouses, or poultry farms, local communities affected by H5N1 outbreaks, and others working closely with animals or the virus. Additionally, with the COVID-19 Bridge Program ending earlier this year, those who are uninsured no longer have access to COVID Vaccines at no cost, exacerbating vaccine inequities. Vaccines should be affordable and accessible to all and we call on you to take action to ensure that those who are uninsured or underinsured have access to updated COVID Vaccines. Everyone deserves access to updated vaccines.

title: Comment from Cook, Kathy
comment: Please allow vaccines for Covid twice a year. This virus doesn’t have seasons and the current mRNA vaccines are only truly effective 3-4 months. Thank you.

title: Comment from Filseth, Paula
comment:
One updated COVID vaccination annually is not enough. COVID is not the same thing as a seasonal flu or cold. It is a virus for which infection can have impactful long-term consequences, even for people with mild symptoms -- and for those of us who are immunocompromised, the risk is higher.

We need at least two updated COVID vaccines a year. And while the mRNA vaccines have, disappointingly, not proven to convey lasting protection, Novavax, a traditional protein vaccine, has been shown to do a better job of this. To protect themselves as much as possible, Americans who want longer-lasting protection need to be able to "start over" with Novavax, which means getting a series of two shots, with the second dose at least 3 weeks after the first -- and a third dose, if desired (or needed, such as by immunocompromised individuals), at least 8 weeks later.

Please give whatever approvals are necessary to make this option available to all Americans.

For the love or everyone trying to protect ourselves in a world that downplays a still pandemic, please give us the means to protect ourselves. Living is already so inaccessible. comment: The CDC needs to change their recommendation to include two shots a year, with a third available if needed FOR ALL PEOPLE, with additional shots available for the immunocompromised.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
I had covid in 2020 before vaccines were available and am living with the long term after effects. All that can be done to prevent or lessen the effects of covid should be done for the benefit of everyone, therefore:
— Allow immunocompromised people to get a second Covid vaccine two months after the first

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: The rise in COVID cases in 2024 in the summer in California illustrates why this spread of this virus is not limited to the winter months and assumptions of how people congregate. Also, it’s possible that some people may need more frequent vaccination without having a diagnosis of immunocompromised and may want to. I had long term symptoms of COVID including brain fog and asthma after breakthrough infection twice in 2022. I caught COVID for the 3rd time in early August 2024 while pregnant and was happy that I had received a booster in June 2024. My symptoms were much milder that the last two times, possibly due to the variant, but maybe taking the booster for a closer related variant helped. We just found evidence of a microbiome in the brain and suggestion of brain plasticity surrounding childbirth in pregnant women. This is not my field, but as a PhD holding cell and molecular biologist, my opinion is that we do not know enough about the compounding long term effects of COVID to limit an individual’s choice to protect themselves year-round.

title: Comment from Van Pelt, Paige
comment:
I am concerned about being immune compromised and needing additional doses of the Novavax Covid-19 Vaccine. The proposed rule would require an immune compromised person to jump through hoops such as extra doctor's appointments in order to qualify for additional doses. These folks need immunity at the level of prevention of transmission and would benefit from the CDC's support to reduce the requirements to obtain additional doses.

The reality is that so few people are even interested in getting vaccinated that we haven't heard the term "herd immunity" for years. Thus, the immune compromised have a greater need for protection than ever expected. Those of us who are elderly or who expect to be in contact with those at risk would also benefit from access to extra doses with minimal hassle.

Please note that I personally got Covid for the first time in June 2024. I had been trying to get Novavax in anticipation of a trip to NY for an oncology appointment. Due to the delays in the decision about the variant selection, I found that our local pharmacies' supplies of Novavax had expired. When I tried to get an MMR vaccine instead, my first two sources has no in-date vaccines and were also waiting for the variant selection before re-ordering. When I checked our CVS pharmacy, they had Phyzer available, but the pharmacist insisted that I make an online appointment for the next day. I eventually received my booster 2-1/2 hours before I rode to an event with a friend who didn't realize that his cold was actually Covid.

The result was that I tested before the second leg of my trip, got a definite positive and had to cancel flights, appointments and hotel reservations. Thank you for listening to my story, for making the Novavax choice available and for the hard work you do hammering out the best use of our nation's resources.
