title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

References:
1. Link-Gelles R. Effectiveness of COVID-19 (2023-2024 Formula) vaccines. Presented at: FDA VRBPAC Meeting; June 5, 2024. Accessed June 12, 2024. https://www.fda.gov/media/179140/download

2. Wu N, Joyal-Desmarais K, Vieira AM, et al. COVID-19 boosters versus primary series: update to a living review. The Lancet Respiratory Medicine. 2023;11(10):e87-e88. doi:10.1016/S2213-2600(23)00265-5

3. Menegale F, Manica M, Zardini A, et al. Evaluation of Waning of SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine–Induced Immunity: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. JAMA Netw Open. 2023;6(5):e2310650. doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2023.10650

4. Nguyen DC, Hentenaar IT, Morrison-Porter A, et al. SARS-CoV-2-specific plasma cells are not durably established in the bone marrow long-lived compartment after mRNA vaccination. Nat Med. Published online September 27, 2024:1-10. doi:10.1038/s41591-024-03278-y

5. Fang Z, Ahrnsbrak R, Rekito A. Evidence Mounts That About 7% of US Adults Have Had Long COVID. JAMA. Published online June 7, 2024. doi:10.1001/jama.2024.11370

6. Yousaf AR. Notes from the Field: Surveillance for Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in Children — United States, 2023. MMWR Morb Mortal Wkly Rep. 2024;73. doi:10.15585/mmwr.mm7310a2

7. https://www.cdc.gov/vaccines/programs/bridge/index.html

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Please vote to make additional Covid vaccine shots available to immunocompromised persons, and also to immunocompetent persons, at least every 6 months.

With a vaccine only strategy being used against an airborne virus, making the vaccines available to everyone will be the only way forward, especially for a vaccine that is known to wane in effectiveness after about 2-3 months against infection and forward transmission. It is particularly important because we know that non-symptomatic (asymptomatic, or also not having any symptoms but still infected) persons do forward transmit (are infectious, and breathing out virus particles) although data suggest that vaccinated persons tend to breathe out far fewer virons during their infection. That means less contagion, and fewer other people catching Covid, as well as lower viral loads when another person is infected during that forward transmission.

As part of the sandwich generation, I'm stuck in the middle of a bunch of people at work who rely only on vaccines -- and they get sick with Covid and other things quite frequently. When norovirus made the rounds at work just about everyone got sick (except for me since I wear a respirator and haven't slacked off on the hand washing). I'm wearing a respirator so I don't bring anything back to my mom who has stage 4 lung cancer. Yeah, it is perturbing that my co-workers who give me well-wishes for my mom won't wear a mask around me to make sure they don't give me anything that I might accidentally bring to her. They count on my n95 to work perfect all the time. So far, so good. There have been three close-calls where I worked closely with a co-worker who felt perfectly fine one day and then tested positive for Covid the next day (and stayed home for an entire week very ill). Asymptomatic spread is very real. People feel perfectly fine the day before they test positive.

And our son is at college for his freshman year, and also not masking--so difficult for a teenager to mask when he wants to make friends, when Public Health messaging is lacking--so when he comes home for Thanksgiving, I'm going to have to mask at home so that when I go to care for my mom, I reduce the risk of bringing something to her. Squeezed on all sides. Between unmasked co-workers and a college kid, I'm relying on my n95 -- but I'd really like to be able to keep up both the immunizations for my mom who has cancer and is at great risk of having bad outcomes if she were to catch Covid and for myself, because I'm exposed to people all the time who take no other precautions and I want my humoral immunity to be sufficiently high through-out the year so that I can avoid getting sick (and thus not being able to work, or take care of my mom, because in this country we rely on the invisible work of moms and daughters instead of an actual social safety net) and also avoid bringing anything to my mom.

So please, make the vaccines available every six months.

When you talk about how infections spread through populations, you forget that each infection is a story unto itself. My mom matters to me. Like the starfish on the beach in the story, she matters. And I matter to her. We both deserve as much protection as we can get to continue to live as long as we can, with as much health as we can. Please don't make our journey any harder than it has to be by denying vaccine availability.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Last year when I was vaccinated, I got Novavax. But it showed up as Moderna on my med chart. It took three e-mails and two phone calls to finally get that information corrected in my chart.

When I wanted Novavax originally (earlier in the pandemic), it took a lot more emails and phone calls. I nearly had to be on a first name basis with some of the employees at my state's health department. I was the one talking to my doctor about how to order up Novavax so I could get the vaccine at my doctor's office. My husband's doctor's office couldn't navigate it at the time, even with my carefully written out points of what needed to happen. In fact, it became clearer later that his doctor didn't even understand that he was asking for the protein vaccine. He thought my husband was just asking for a general covid vaccine.

This year, I knew hoe to navigate the system, and I got my family vaccinated with Novavax, but it still involved calling pharmacies to check that it would indeed be in stock. I had to help my parents sign up because when they tried to make appointments for Novavax, they couldn't. I had to make the appointments on the website while I had them on the phone with me.

So don't think that it is easy to get vaccinated. Or to keep records straight. It's a jungle out here.

I don't even want to think about the nightmare it must be to get children vaccinated. At least I was only dealing with trying to schedule adults.

The CDC needs to make vaccine access a priority. It needs to be clear and it needs to be easy. Doctors offices are not informed enough. Pharmacists are not informed enough. They often put up roadblocks because they lack the knowledge. There is a lot of gatekeeping at points where there shouldn't even be questions asked.

Not everyone is like me, willing to sit through waiting on the phone, sending emails, checking and double checking appointments. People need the permissions to be lenient and easy to navigate and explain, not convoluted language that can be misinterpreted by those administering the vaccines, who might have some bias about vaccines and the system and make it more difficult for everyone to get their shots.

There's obviously a lack of uptake on the vaccines, which impacts the population by not having enough shots in arms to do enough to mitigate the continuing waves of infection that roll on throughout the year. A huge part of that is because of the stumbling blocks inherent in the language and permissions that the CDC puts into place around the administration of the vaccine.

We need access. We need to be able to get new primary series, a booster every 6 months, and access for everyone-- not just immunocompromised people.

I'm never going to get my time back that I spent navigating the labyrinthine policies prior to this year, but I certainly don't have to waste my time going forward if the CDC could make Covid vaccine access available to everyone.

title: Comment from Hill, Luke
comment: The CDC needs to change their recommendation to include two shots a year, with a third available if needed FOR ALL PEOPLE, with additional shots available for the immunocompromised.

title: Comment from Lamanna, Giulianna
comment: Please allow and recommend people of all ages and health statuses to receive vaccines at least twice a year. At this point, we have enough data on Covid-19 to know that it’s not seasonal like the flu. Every year, there are at least two waves, and our vaccines wane in effectiveness after only four months. The vaccines also don’t do very much to protect us from long Covid, which can often be debilitating and for which there is no known cure. It’s not just the most vulnerable who need protection, it’s all of us, and we don’t just need it once a year — we need it at least twice a year. Please make your recommendations based on observed data about Covid-19, not wishful thinking that perhaps if we act as if it is the flu, it will become the flu. It’s not like the flu, it never has been, and it never will be, no matter how much we may wish that were the case.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
ACIP should recommend the authorization of the use for H5N1 vaccines for farmworkers and others facing occupational risk of H5N1 exposure and infection. While the CDC is recommending seasonal flu vaccines for farmworkers to reduce the risk of human flu viruses mixing with H5N1, the seasonal vaccine does not provide protection against H5N1. As we head into fall and winter flu season, if H5N1 swaps genes with a human flu virus it raises the risk of a more transmissible and virulent strain that could spread as easily among people as seasonal flu viruses do. The US should be proactively taking action to offer the vaccine for those facing the highest risk, and precedent already exists in the case of Finland, which has already moved to vaccinate farmworkers at risk to prevent the risk of severe illness and death with a virus that has historically had over a 50% fatality rate in humans.
In the past year or two, the virus has quickly adapted to spread among a growing range of mammals and is now widespread in dairy cattle in the United States and most recently has found a foothold in dairy farms in California, the largest dairy producer in the nation. With the growing number of dairy farms with H5N1 cases and little sign of this outbreak being controlled, farmworkers are at increased risk and this opens up the possibility of the likelihood of a strain that is better at infecting and spreading between humans. There has been little testing and a lack of transparency in data and information around H5N1 from both the USDA and CDC and we should not wait to take action until we see even more clusters of infection. Vaccines should be authorized and made available to dairy and poultry farmers, those working in slaughterhouses, and others facing occupational risk of exposure. In the words of Rick Bright, former Deputy Assistant Secretary for Preparedness and Response and the Director of the Biomedical Advanced Research and Development Authority (BARDA),“There’s no such thing as just a little conjunctivitis or just a little respiratory (issue) when you’re dealing with a deadly virus. The virus has already demonstrated the ability to mutate very easily, very quickly, and it can cause severe illness and death. So let’s stop it in its tracks, not wait and see and let it get worse.

title: Comment from Hau, Larry
comment:
COVID vaccination should be recommended at least twice a year for everyone, regardless of age or health status. Restricting eligibility creates unnecessary barriers for vulnerable people and discourages those at high risk from getting crucial booster shots. People of all ages, including those 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule needs to address both the decline in efficacy over time and the emergence of new variants by recommending updated vaccinations every six months. This waning efficacy occurs with all types of COVID vaccines, no matter a person’s age or health condition. Recent research supports that receiving the vaccine lowers the risk of Long COVID and reduces the chances of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C).

The CDC should clearly recommend COVID vaccination twice a year for all ages, which will influence healthcare provider guidance and health insurance coverage. This approach will also help raise public awareness of the importance of staying up to date with vaccinations to provide the best protection against illness.

It's crucial that the CDC ensures everyone has equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines, preventing barriers related to cost or demographics. With the end of the CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program in August 2024, many uninsured and underinsured adults are now without easy access to COVID vaccines. We must advocate for free access to COVID vaccines to reduce hesitancy and ensure everyone is protected.

title: Comment from Wright, Michael
comment:
Dear CDC/ ACIP Members

I would like to express my desire to receive more than one COVID shot per year. I don't believe that a one-shot protocol is protective for a 12-month period. Please update the guidelines to allow for two (at a minimum) yearly COVID shots.

Respectfully,
Michael Wright
Littleton, NH

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
A restrictive approach to eligibility for Updated COVID vaccination and boosters creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccines.
COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status.
People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access.
I beg you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from George, Bruno
comment:
I am writing to ask that the CDC make updated COVID vaccines available at least twice year, to people of all ages and all health statuses. COVID’s mutations and surges do not happen on an annual schedule, so once-a-year vaccinations are not sufficient.
Moreover, the restriction of spring boosters to only people of a certain age or a particular health status makes it harder for people who DO belong to those groups to get vaccinated: when boosters are viewed as an exception, insurance companies are less likely to pay for them, and pharmacists become gatekeepers, turning people away if they don’t like the answers they give to questions about eligibility. The difficulty of getting vaccinated now—including the dismantling of the Bridge program, which helped the underinsured and uninsured—means there is even less vaccine uptake, putting all of us at risk.
I am sixty-two years old, three years shy of eligibility for reasons of age, and I have Long Covid, a poorly understood health condition that many gatekeepers would not recognize as an immunocompromising condition. I am lucky in that my Long Covid has abated enough that I can work again, which is my only way to survive. Another COVID infection could permanently disable me and leave me homeless. So I would like to be able to vaccinate more often than annually, but I cannot.
Please advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Grimes, John
comment:
Docket No: CDC-2024-0072
Dear ACIP,

As a 19-year-old sophomore at Texas Tech University, I was a typical student and like most teenagers, felt like I was 10 feet tall and bulletproof. I was an active member of a fraternity and everything seemed to be following an ordered path. And then, on February 13th, I woke up in the hospital. Miraculously, eight days prior, my fraternity brother had found me unconscious on my bedroom floor. Rather than being the result of a memorable college party, my experience was thanks to a nasty bout with bacterial meningitis.

At the hospital, my parents held a tenuous weeklong vigil at my bedside, anxious for a sign that I would emerge from the coma. The stone-faced doctor on my case informed them I might not, while nonchalantly inquiring about my organ donor status and warned that if I did pull through, much of who I had been would not likely ever return, physically or mentally. I might still be here but I won’t be me. Yet, when I did come out of the coma, my brain remarkably was okay. Most of my body was also willing to respond if I was willing to do the work. But there were some significant disconnects.

As I swim back to an uneasy consciousness, the first thing I see is darkness. I’m disoriented by the darkness. I can hear—beeping machines, a constant stream of doctors, nurses, and therapists coming and going—but I cannot see. There’s a tube in my nose. I’ve lost control of my muscles. Speaking is out of the question. I can’t even smile—not that I have cause to smile; I’m exhausted, irritated, and confused. I have no idea what’s going on, why I’m here, how long I’ve been here, or how much longer I might continue to be.

Eventually the darkness dissipates somewhat. My so-called vision is almost more disorienting than the total blackness, I later learn I’m sightless in my right eye and the vision in my left eye is 20/300. For reference, 20/20 is perfect vision. 20/80 is poor vision. 20/300 is like looking through binoculars backwards. Essentially, everything looked far away … except my problems. They were very close.

Surviving meningitis turned out to be the easy part—so easy that I'd done it in my sleep. The seemingly impossible part was living after surviving meningitis. At 20 years old, I thought my sight would come back. It did not. I had to adapt.

Missteps aside, my return to school went pretty well. I was able to participate in typical rites of passage and social gatherings despite a new neurogenic bladder and an unusual plus-one. When you’re going to college parties, meeting new people, and trying to live a normal experience, your collegiate catheter is an awkward social companion.

While learning to adapt my approach and revise goals to fulfill aspirations on the work front, I found myself powerless with respect to my deepest relational aspirations. Back when I was coming to terms with my physical limitations, I’d accepted that my meningitis-induced neurological damage meant that being a father was a desire and a dream, but not likely a reality.

My disabilities, though mostly invisible to others, consumed me and impeded my ability to perform to my potential. Eventually it dawned on me that a world that can’t really see my disabilities is not going to wait for me to catch up. That responsibility belonged to me—I would never truly succeed until my desire to do so was greater than my disabilities. I quickly learned that survival isn’t the end of the journey, it’s just the beginning.

Had the current vaccine schedule been in place in 1998, my story would be nothing more than a 'what if.' Instead, I am living proof of what happens when gaps in protection leave lives vulnerable. Thankfully, the progress we’ve made since then has been remarkable. The existing schedule has consistently protected at-risk populations, prevented meningococcal disease, and ensured broad, reliable immunization coverage.

Disrupting this proven system risks reversing that progress. Any change in timing or recommendations could lead to dangerous gaps in protection, particularly for young people and other high-risk groups. It might also sow confusion among healthcare providers and the public, potentially reducing vaccine uptake and leaving more people exposed. Continuity in the current schedule reflects years of research, hard-won public health experience, and best practices that have saved countless lives.

I urge the committee to carefully weigh the potential consequences of any changes and prioritize the stability of this life-saving immunization strategy. Your decisions have the power to protect or to put lives at risk. Thank you for your dedication and commitment to safeguarding public health."

John B. Grimes

title: Comment from Cantrell, Paul
comment:
I understand that ACIP is considering opening the door to the public receiving COVID boosters more frequently than once a year.

Since vaccines became available, I have been doing exactly that: one booster in the early fall, one mid-winter as cases peak, approximately four months later. Apparently this is a violation of the CDC's recommendations, but it seems well justified by research: as far as I can gather, vaccine effectiveness wanes somewhere around 3 or 4 months. And this personal has apparently kept me and my family safe: none of have, to our knowledge, ever had COVID — despite both parents and child spending all day in classrooms during the school year, despite travel and socializing, despite testing at any sign of symptoms, none of us have ever tested positive.

In the presence of ambiguous research and intense political pressure, I realize it is hard to choose a single best recommendation. I would encourage you to at least give people like me and my family the officially blessed _choice_ of receiving a vaccination multiple times a year.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Dear Members of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices,

As an immunocompromised individual, I am writing to respectfully urge the committee to reconsider and update its policies regarding COVID-19 vaccinations to better protect vulnerable populations and enhance public health. Specifically, I suggest the following three points:

1.Allow immunocompromised individuals to self-attest and receive a second COVID vaccine dose two months after the first dose.
Immunocompromised individuals often experience weaker immune responses, making it crucial for us to access a second dose sooner to achieve optimal protection. Waiting for lengthy periods can leave us vulnerable to severe outcomes, especially in the face of evolving variants. Self-attestation would streamline access to this protection without administrative barriers, which can delay timely vaccination.
2.Broaden policy language to allow anyone to receive a second COVID dose after two months.
While immunocompromised individuals may need enhanced protection, others can also benefit from booster doses sooner than currently recommended. Waning immunity affects everyone, and providing more flexibility in vaccination timing would help strengthen protection at the population level, particularly during surges of infections or new variants.
3.Recommend COVID vaccines every six months, rather than yearly, due to the quick decline in vaccine efficacy over time.
Studies have demonstrated that immunity, especially against infection, wanes considerably within months of vaccination. A six-month interval for boosters would better align with the pace of immunity decline and offer more consistent protection throughout the year, particularly for those at higher risk. Recent studies, such as one published in The Lancet, have shown that protection from severe disease begins to wane around four to six months post-vaccination, suggesting that a yearly booster may not be sufficient to maintain strong immunity.

These policy updates would not only better protect individuals like myself but also contribute to the greater public good by reducing overall transmission, preventing severe outcomes, and alleviating the burden on healthcare systems during surges.

Thank you for your consideration of these important recommendations. I trust that your committee will continue to prioritize the safety and well-being of all Americans, particularly the most vulnerable among us.

title: Comment from Rhodes, S R
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

As a health care leader and extremely concerned citizen, it is imperative that COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. We see it with health care workers and we see it with patients and the community at large. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025 or even more frequently.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months and up to every two months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6]. Patients are coming to clinic and hospital 4 weeks after vaccination with full blown symptoms and testing positive. Hospitals are not requiring masks for ANYONE and as a result we continue to have ongoing spread that takes place at hospitals and clinics as well as in the community at large.

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year (and ideally as frequently as every two months) for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Marks, Paula
comment:
This is the most important comment you're going to read, because I'm going to lay out exactly what ACIP/CDC needs to do.

You're currently voting on allowing a 2nd dose of the COVID vaccine 2 months after the first for immunocompromised people. Committee should vote yes on this as they have in past years. HOWEVER, another dose shouldn't be limited to just immunocompromised people. Everyone who has had COVID has a damaged immune system. The once-per-year COVID vaccine strategy is failing. mRNA vaccines wane after 4 months, and COVID has shown to spike in every season, not just fall and winter.

So in addition to broadening access to everyone to get boosted every 2 months, all three COVID vaccine manufacturers should either update or study their vaccine every 6 month to provide more protection. And ACIP needs to make COVID vaccines recommendations for everyone every 6 months - spring and fall. mRNA was tailor made to update quickly and adjust for mutating variants, so it's a waste of the technology not to do this twice per year, especially since mRNA wanes so quickly. Novavax takes longer to update, but their vaccine offers a more broad protection, so their vaccines should be studied every 6 months to see if they need an update or if they can just be given as a booster from the fall.

By CDC's admission, COVID surges in summer and winter. But even low dips in COVID infection trends aren't that low any more. So why are you only recommending COVID vaccines once per year? This should be spring and fall to account for surges in summer and winter.

Going forward, I'd like to see more access to Novavax's COVID vaccine.

You also need more urgent studies into Castlevax and Novavax non-live attenuated nasal vaccines as boosters with intramuscular vaccines. Important that future COVID nasal vaccines come out asap and be non-live.

Finally, this isn't on the agenda, but future H5N1 vaccine must block transmission and not be on a fall-only schedule. Efficacy must go above and beyond what we consider acceptable to the flu and COVID vaccines. And it should not be yearly on the same timeline as the flu vaccine. Nor should it be a live vaccine. You are not here to manage H5N1. You need to PREVENT it from becoming widespread. Doing what you did with COVID by managing it instead of eradicating it is unacceptable. Do better for H5N1

title: Comment from Sundling, Kaitlin
comment:
This is an update to previous comments I have submitted to both the CDC’s ACIP and the FDA’s VRBPAC, dating back to February 2023, and the essential message continues to be unchanged. As a physician-scientist (MD, PhD), I support eligibility for COVID vaccines at least twice a year (including spring 2025 vaccination) for all ages and regardless of health status, not just for people aged 65 and older or immunocompromised people.

Across all age and risk groups, vaccination rates are far too low. The current annual approach has failed to maintain or improve vaccination rates, and leaves far too many people at risk even if they have gotten every vaccine they are eligible for.

Many friends, family, and community members have faced undue barriers to vaccination, including people who are high risk, aged 65 or older, and immunocompromised. Last year's (spring 2024) restrictive approach to spring vaccination led to decreased access for vulnerable populations and confusion over who would benefit from an additional vaccine dose.

All of us (myself included as an adult under age 65 with no immunocompromising conditions) would benefit from vaccination at least every 6 months. Restricting access to only certain groups not only puts people at risk of severe consequences of infection, including both acute sequelae and Long COVID, but also creates unnecessary confusion for both the public and vaccine providers.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. More frequent vaccination will help more people avoid Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as lowering the risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

More frequent updates COVID vaccine formulations should be made as needed to better match emergence of variants, with a frequency of at least every 6 months. The annual update approach is not appropriate given that COVID spreads year-round, leading to emergence of diverging variants frequently and outside of a fall-winter season [7]. I urge this committee to recommend updated vaccine formulations as soon as they are made available by FDA authorization, which may require additional meetings or review off-cycle.

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

Please also express your support for affordable or free access to updated vaccines, including a permanent replacement to the CDC's Bridge vaccine access program that ended in August 2024 [8], leaving many uninsured and underinsured people without access to the latest vaccines. Even insured individuals face undue barriers, as insurance does not cover all vaccine providers that may be conveniently available, particularly impacting people who lack time off work for vaccination, use public transit, or lack transportation. Free COVID vaccines for all of us would reduce barriers to vaccination and improve vaccination rates.

Allowing COVID vaccination at least every 6 months would allow my immediate family to receive updated vaccines more often, better protecting me from severe COVID infections.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from AKERS, MICHELLE
comment: For crying out loud, please STOP restricting people's access to COVID vaccinations!  It's impossible to overstate how completely counterproductive this is.

title: Comment from Arrandale , Katherine
comment:
Hi - my comment is in favor of changing the COVID vaccine schedule to every 6 months for everyone, and allowing a second dose of the current vaccine at 2 months for immunocompromised people.

The CDC recognizes that COVID isn't a winter illness like the flu — there are multiple COVID peaks throughout the year, most notably in the summer and winter. Protection from the vaccines wanes, making people much more vulnerable during the summer surge, which is often 8 months after their last vaccination. The vaccine schedule should reflect this reality.

It's true that vaccination timing was a bit confusing at first, because everyone was on different schedules due to staggered availability. But twice a year would be no more confusing than once a year, and would decrease the burden of summer peaks. Accurate, simple public health communication could also help avoid confusion.

On a personal note, I am high risk for COVID, and summer surges are a particular risk to me because of my lack of protection (and the lack of protection of people around me). If more people were able to get vaccinated more frequently, I wouldn't have to delay medical treatment as often.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
As a citizen with both elderly relatives and family members with compromised immune systems, I am begging approval be given to recommending COVID vaccines for all ages and health statuses at least twice a year. Not only do my elders and/or family members with health issues benefit with being boosted more often, we as a family and as individuals are safer from spreading the virus to each other and anyone in our day-to-day lives.

I am also commenting anonymously out of an abundance of caution that I or my family may be targeted by providing identifiable information. We should not have to live in fear of harassment or worse just because we support vaccination.

title: Comment from Etheridge , Gena
comment: We need Covid boosters as needed. Not once a year.

title: Comment from Alliance for Women's Health and Prevention
organization: Alliance for Women's Health and Prevention
comment:
As an organization dedicated to supporting women’s health and wellbeing across the lifespan, the Alliance for Women’s Health and Prevention (AWHP) advances efforts that promote equitable access to and uptake of vaccines. As such, AWHP strongly supports clear and comprehensive vaccine recommendations.

As part of the HPV Coalition, AWHP supports lowering the age recommendation for HPV vaccination to 9. Given the effectiveness of HPV vaccines in preventing cancers caused by HPV, including cervical cancer, robust uptake of the vaccine is critical for public health. However, uptake remains suboptimal. Initiating the HPV vaccine series at age 9 has proven to be an effective approach to addressing these rates in the U.S., with evidence demonstrating the impact on vaccination uptake. This change would also align ACIP’s age recommendation with other leading groups, including the American Academy of Pediatrics and the American Cancer Society. Having a clear, consistent recommendation would reduce patient and provider confusion and ultimately reduce preventable deaths from HPV-related cancers.

Additionally, as the ACIP has previously discussed a timeline for revisiting the adolescent meningococcal schedule, AWHP strongly encourages the Committee to expedite its reassessment of the schedule and prioritize a timely vote.

AWHP supports a clear and comprehensive meningococcal vaccine schedule for adolescents and young adults and encourages the ACIP to make recommendations that further this goal by updating guidance related to pentavalent meningitis vaccines that promise to streamline implementation. We welcome a simplified vaccine process for this serious, yet preventable disease and encourage broad, equitable meningococcal vaccination. Given the delay of the Meningococcal Working Group’s assessment, the ACIP can make a tangible difference in meningitis prevention by advancing a clear and easy to understand recommendation that protects young adults against all five strains of meningococcal bacteria most likely to cause meningococcal meningitis. This is a unique and rare opportunity to revise the adolescent meningococcal vaccine schedule to improve broader, equitable access to new prevention options, and stakeholders that are focused on preventing this terrible disease are waiting for this critical vote to take place.

title: Comment from Kolodziejski, Camille
comment:
Can we please allow immunocompromised people to access a booster or full dose of the Covid vaccine (any type they choose) two months from vaccination! Anyone should be able to get another vaccination at two months and the recommendation from the government shouldn’t be yearly but every 6 months to fully cover folks all year round.

Please make vaccines more accessible and normalize getting them more often if people choose to or need to with autoimmune conditions.

title: Comment from McCall, Benjamin
comment: COVID vaccination should be recommended for all people at least twice per year.  Given the well-established waning efficacy of vaccines, permitting only a single vaccine per year is a grave disservice to the American people.  The current draconian approach forces people like me to forgo vaccines during certain seasons in order to "time" my vaccine for the period where I expect to have the greatest exposure.  It would be such a simple change for CDC to make to recommend twice-yearly vaccines, and doing so would greatly reduce the American people's exposure to this life-threatening and life-altering disease.

title: Comment from Lovett, Elliott
comment: Please make COVID vaccines available at least twice annually. Protection wanes far sooner than a year. If we want to avoid the spread of illness, disabling more people, and killing the most vulnerable, this is a necessary step.

title: Comment from MCKINNEY, KAMERON
comment: The CDC needs to change their recommendation to include two shots a year, with a third available if needed FOR ALL PEOPLE, with additional shots available for the immunocompromised.

title: Comment from Anderson , Chuck
comment:
Please approve allowing people to get up to 3 Covid vaccines per year for the following reasons.
When getting mRNA, they might want to update their memory response. There are examples of this type of update even being necessary with influenza as well.

We’ve learned from flu vaccines, that getting vaccinated every year without exposure can make hospitalization risk higher. We can likely mitigate that with an additional shot to update the memory response.

If folks switch to Novavax, they may want to prime with the protein-based options and that can include 3 shots; 2 shots 8 weeks apart and a 3rd no sooner than 6 months later.

They might have a high risk event to boost their immunity for and mRNA protection is know to only last for 112 days. That’s three to cover 365 days.

As the pandemic drags on, someone might go a long time between shots, making them want to start over… They should have that freedom.

Thank you for your consideration.

title: Comment from Dobson, Teri
comment:
COVID vaccination must be allowed at least every six months must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status.

A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

It is absolutely critical that the vaccine schedule address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages AT LEAST EVERY SIX MONTHS.

Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C).

The CDC must issue a clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages.

In addition, the CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. It is of upmost importance that you advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

High risk individuals don't generally live alone, all family members must have vaccination coverage by insurance to protect their vulnerable family members, and indeed for themselves since many studies have shown persistent physical harms (such as increased risk of stroke or heart attack) from COVID to even "low risk" individuals.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
The fight against Covid would benefit from twice annual vaccination and new vaccines released twice a year, for all age groups, regardless of health and age status.

While Covid often spikes seasonally it is not just a winter disease. While mass traveling and intermingling over the holidays caused a spike, so does the back-to-school season, large gatherings and vacation travel. The south goes back to school in August, not September, fueling the spike.

Throughout the summer, anyone looking for a new Covid vaccine is simply told to wait until September when the new ones come out (while being at risk of contracting COVID) Meanwhile, that vaccine was most likely manufactured just a few months ago, but with mRNA from a strain from 15 months ago. Both the recentness of the shot and the match of the strain affect immunity. The benefit of mRNA is its rapid manufacture. Take advantage.

In fall 2023, when I was eight months pregnant, my family fell victim to Covid about two weeks before the new vaccine came out. It put both a lot of stress on my pregnancy and ruined activities that were actually months in the making and I lost three weeks of work time.

Repeat Covid exposures have been shown to increase the likelihood of long Covid, heart issues and cognitive impairment. I work in a national security job that requires me to use my brain to code and analyze, cognitive impairment makes it more difficult and puts our security at risk. One of my coworkers whose husband got long covid talks about how he is not all there any more. Good thing she’s a breadwinner!

Meanwhile getting a covid shot costs less than the sick days COVID causes and with low risk (that guy who fraudulently got 100s of shots is fine). My almost 1 year old who has had his covid shots at 6 months and this fall is doing just fine (thank you for making the new booster available to all ages)

If not everyone wants to get vaccinated every six months, that’s their decision, but that’s a decision that I would like to make for myself and my family.

comment: Good day.  Thank you for all you do in support of good health for Americans.  I write today to ask for your urgent consideration of enabling Americans to obtain multiple doses of the COVID-19 vaccines during a year. title: Comment from Gloshen, Braedi
comment: I am writing this comment in hopes that everyone will be eligible to receive more than one updated COVID-19 vaccine per year. I live with chronic illness but I'm not considered immunocompromised. I am still very high risk, as is my husband. We both care for my elderly parents. We need access to multiple vaccines per year, with updated formulas for current variants. I also live in a state with a very very high elderly population. We need to protect our older residents, very young children who may not be eligible for a vaccine at all, those that are immunocompromised, chronically ill, high risk, or for whatever reason cannot (or will not) get vaccinated. The more of us that are vaccinated, the more of us that have a degree of protection from infection and severe outcomes. Making everyone eligible to receive more than one vaccine a year is extraordinarily important to creating a healthier population. This has a great deal of impact on the economy in general, and to everyone's individual health status. Thank you.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: The CDC needs to change their Covid vaccine recommendation to include two shots a year, with a third available if needed FOR ALL PEOPLE, with additional shots available for the immunocompromised.

title: Comment from S, Laura
comment:
Hello, I am writing today to request that the CDC recommends Covid vaccines at least twice a year, universally for all ages, 6 months and up. I also request more frequent updates to the vaccines to keep pace with ever-changing variants.

I am the mother of young elementary school aged child and also help take care of my aging parents.

My parents are both over the age of 65 and high risk - my mother is immunocompromised and my father is in treatment for cancer.

Please continue to allow additional, life-saving vaccine doses for vulnerable people like them.

I am an otherwise “healthy” individual, however, as a primary caretaker, it is very important to me to protect my parents health (as well as my own). During the most recent Summer wave of Covid, I was very fearful of accidentally transmitting Covid to my family, especially since the immunity benefits of my last booster vaccine (Fall 2023) had waned.

I worried not only about infecting others, but also being unable to work or take care of my parents or young child while I was sick or recovering, which with Covid, is often a week or two of illness.

This was especially nerve-racking once my child returned to school in August and the updated Fall 2024 boosters were still not available.

Please make updated vaccines available for people like me (everyone) at least twice a year, and preferably even more frequently (every 3-4 months) to account for waning immunity.

This would also allow the Covid vaccines to more closely match the most widely-circulating variants at the time, and thus provide better protection. The ability of mRNA vaccines to be updated more quickly is amazing, and we should be taking advantage of it to more effectively fight this virus.

I am also concerned about the risks of Long Covid, which affects Americans of all ages and health statuses. Not getting Covid is the best way of avoiding Long Covid, and frequent vaccination can help reduce spread.

With very few people still masking in this ongoing pandemic (even in healthcare settings), updated vaccines are one of the few protections we have left. Please make them available for all.

Thank you.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
As mentioned by the CDC, COVID can surge year-round, unlike flu and RSV, which have distinct seasons [1]. For example, this past summer had a significant COVID surge [2]. Since COVID can surge year-round, unlike flu and RSV, everyone should be eligible for at least 2 COVID vaccine doses per year, particularly people at high-risk. According to the CDC, people with risk factors, such as cancer, cardiovascular disease, chronic kidney disease, chronic liver disease, chronic lung disease, smoking, diabetes, immunocompromised condition, are at an increased risk of severe outcome from COVID. It does not make sense that people with risk factors for severe COVID are currently eligible for only 1 COVID vaccine dose per year when COVID can surge year-round.

comment: I have a few of the conditions that make Covid more difficult for me, such as asthma. I am over 60 but not yet 65. I would very much like access to a second dose of Novavax 8 weeks after my first dose so I will be adequately primed. Many pharmacists are still gatekeeping so I can’t get the second dose. The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Alliance for Patient Access
comment:
Dear Dr. Talbot and Dr. Wharton,

On behalf of the undersigned organizations and clinicians, we commend the Advisory Council on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for its leadership in providing guidance on the most effective strategies to reduce vaccine-preventable diseases (VPDs). ACIP’s efforts play a critical role in advancing population health in the United States.

We are writing to encourage ACIP to adopt age-based recommendations for adults starting at age 50 for pneumococcal vaccines at the October 23-24, 2024 meeting. Data supports and research has shown that implementing such a recommendation would advance population health, disease prevention, and health equity.

During the June 25-26, 2024, meeting, ACIP members acknowledged the value of starting appropriate age-based recommendations at age 50 rather than 65. Members highlighted how lowering age-based recommendations would enhance the population health of our communities and increase equity – particularly among Black Americans, as well as the American Indian and Alaska Native population. The benefits of a universal recommendation and the need to make a timely decision were also emphasized. We greatly appreciate these acknowledgements and hope a decision will be made at the upcoming meeting.

We are concerned that vaccine coverage rates of adults aged 50-64 years have been observed to be roughly 8%, the lowest of all age groups.i Given the increasing prevalence of chronic conditions as individuals age, early vaccination may offer a vital opportunity to protect vulnerable populations from severe disease.ii As former ACIP Chair Dr. Grace Lee remarked in October 2023, the most straightforward way to increase vaccination rates in this age group - and address health disparities - is through broad age-based recommendations.

Recommending vaccine access beginning at age 50 would be an effective allocation of resources, enhancing the population health of our communities. Furthermore, lowering the age-based recommendations can help address some health disparities. Studies have shown that Black and Hispanic Americans are significantly more affected by chronic conditions, including higher rates of diabetes and death from heart disease.iii Black Americans are also diagnosed with chronic conditions like heart disease and diabetes 5-10 years earlier than the general population.iv The increased impact of these chronic conditions can also increase risk of VPDs and severe illness. Lowering the recommended vaccination age to 50, where supported by data, could be an effective strategy mitigating these disparities while lessening the burden on an often-underserved population.

We urge ACIP to urgently consider the impact of VPDs on those in the 50-64 age range, and the value of lowering age-based recommendations for pneumococcal vaccines to age 50-64, during the October meeting. Doing so can help simplify the adult immunization schedule, improve health outcomes, and encourage increased vaccination. We are eager to support ACIP in its continued efforts to reduce VPDs and appreciate the opportunity to submit comment. Should you have any questions, please contact Stephanie Hu at shu@allianceforpatientaccess.org.

Thank you for your commitment to advancing population health and health equity.

Sincerely,

This morning I sat in a courtroom and watched an unmasked officer cough directly onto a person in his custody, then continue coughing on his fellow officers, the judge, and the public. There is almost no testing, there are no mask mandates, and the cost of treating & testing for COVID-19 continues to go up. Vaccines are the best way for me to protect myself and my community from this deadly, disabling illness. They are also hard to access (even with insurance), and new variants of the illness are appearing more quickly than new vaccines become available. I am trans and live with a mental illness. While neither of these facts affect my immune system, both affect my ability to get correct, effective emergency care. Improving access to vaccines for me and my family lowers my risk of needing emergency services that are not designed for a body like mine. It also protects the children, old folks, disabled people (especially disabled veterans exposed to respiratory toxins during their service), and people like me whose visible identities have as much of an impact on their healthcare experience as their reason for going to the clinic.

Please please please expand access to frequent vaccines against COVID-19!

title: Comment from Rosenberg, Jennifer
comment: I would welcome the opportunity to get a Covid vaccine every 6 months. The current vaccine effectiveness wanes greatly after 4 months leaving me without coverage during high respiratory disease months like April/May and September.

title: Comment from Vigh, Jonathan
comment:
Dear ACIP Committee Members,

It is very important for many people to be able to get vaccinated several times per year. Everyone has a unique situation and sometimes people must undertake high risk travel during periods that are "off-season" for the current Covid-19 "vaccination season".

My own experience is a case in point. In June, I took an Alaskan cruise with my extended family. This wasn't something I would normally choose to do in this era, but it was a multi-generational cruise to celebrate my parent's 50th Wedding Anniversary The ship had a massive outbreak of Covid-19 that week and by the end of the week, many people were sick. Despite taking all possible precautions, including wearing an N99 FloMask respirator (including during meals) and having two air purifiers in my room, I still got a light case of Covid-19, likely due the ships ventilation system. This was my first confirmed case of Covid-19, to my knowledge since the start of the pandemic. Then weeks after recovering, I developed what I believe to be Long Covid, including symptoms of moderate brain fog, stomach dyspepsia, and frequent bouts of Post Exertional Malaise (PEM). Nearly everyone in my extended family caught Covid-19 with the exception of my Dad, who had had a Novavax booster 3 months before the cruise, and my 6-year old son who has likely been micro-dose exposed in school, despite wearing a FloMask. I had hoped to get a Novavax booster the month before the cruise, but it was hard to find in my area, in part due to the strategy that the CDC has been following of one booster per year and because doses may have expired.

From my extended family's experience (n=9 with only 2 avoiding Covid infection), one booster is simply not enough to provide enough protection throughout a year.

I urge the committee to come up with a recommendation that considers both immune imprinting and the need for bi-annual boosters. Anytime the vaccine formula changes, such a recommendation should allow people to get an optional second primary series booster two months after their first booster in order to overcome immune imprinting. A second booster should be *recommended* for immune compromised individuals, which should include anyone who has had a previous Covid infection. Please also recommend that people be able to receive an additional booster 6 months after their primary series shots.

I believe if I had been a been able to get a Novavax booster a month before my cruise, I very likely would have remained infection free and avoided getting Long Covid. Thankfully, my Long Covid symptoms are starting to resolve. The most likely cause of the improvement is the updated Novavax booster that I got about a month ago.

Thank you for considering this request.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

I am writing to urge the CDC to:
1.) Recommend free COVID vaccines for all ages and health statuses at least twice a year (spring vaccine access for all),
and
2.) Support more frequent updates to the vaccines, adjusted for the latest variants.

A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. Many people under the 65 or older age group have vulnerabilities that they may not be able to get diagnosed due to cost of, lack of transportation to, and lack of time for doctor's appointments, among other reasons. Marginalized people are already some of the most vulnerable groups in our society, and restricting vaccine accessibility to only officially diagnosed or older people would cause harm to our whole society. We need young people as healthy as possible to help our economy recover from this ongoing pandemic. Long covid is a growing threat, and it does not discriminate between the young and old, the healthy and vulnerable. Concerning numbers of young people are losing their ability to work due to long covid. I have had covid 3 times now, and despite experiencing negative outcomes, I count myself lucky to have had every vaccine so far and retained my ability to provide for myself. Regular, updated vaccines not only reduce the chance of fatality upon infection but also the chance of long covid. [1]

The vaccine schedule should address the emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [2] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status.

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness. Far too few Americans have received the latest vaccines. As of October 11, 2024, only 11.2% of all adults and 26.7% of adults aged 65 and older had received an updated 2024-2025 COVID vaccine, and as of July 27, 2024, only 9% of adults aged 65 and older received the recommended two doses of last year’s 2023-2024 vaccine. [3]

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024, leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. I ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce hesitation and barriers to vaccination.

3. https://www.cdc.gov/respiratory-viruses/data/vaccination-trends.html

title: Comment from Cohen, Greysen
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

It is essential to recommend COVID vaccinations at least twice annually (every six months) for individuals of all ages and health conditions. Restrictive eligibility criteria create unnecessary obstacles for vulnerable populations and may discourage high-risk individuals from receiving booster shots. People of all age groups, including those 65 and older or those with compromised immune systems, should be able to access another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The recommended vaccination schedule should address the reduction in vaccine effectiveness over time [1-3] and the emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 variants. By advising updated vaccines for all ages at least every six months, we can mitigate the effects of waning immunity, which occurs across all vaccine types. Recent studies into the biological mechanisms of this decline [4] confirm that it happens regardless of age or immune status. Furthermore, staying up to date with vaccinations has been linked to a reduced risk of developing Long COVID following infection [5] and a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

A clear recommendation from the CDC for biannual COVID vaccinations for all age groups will influence medical provider guidelines and ensure health insurance coverage. This approach will also enhance public understanding of the importance of receiving vaccinations within the last six months for optimal protection as part of a broader strategy to prevent illness.

The CDC must prioritize ensuring equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and avoid financial or demographic barriers to care. Since the CDC’s Bridge Access Program concluded in August 2024 [7], many uninsured and underinsured adults now lack access to COVID vaccines. We urge you to advocate for free and accessible COVID vaccinations for everyone, to reduce barriers and encourage more widespread vaccination.

title: Comment from Ell, Gee
comment:
This comment is regarding the use of additional doses of COVID-19 vaccine in immunocompromised individuals and older adults following an initial dose of 2024–2025 vaccine.

The lack of access to twice-annual COVID-19 vaccination for all people is a necessary step to accomplishing the goal we all share: making COVID-19 a mild, endemic, occasional infection. Before 2020, my family and I were able to lead outgoing, social, public lives; as COVID-19 rates continue to spike throughout the year and as COVID-19 still poses a serious risk of acute infection and long-term disability, we have experienced a substantial reduction in our capacity to socialize. We see our friends and extended family infrequently. We do not eat at restaurants. We want to be able to resume the activities that brought us simple joy prior to 2020 -- even sitting in a coffee shop reading a book -- without weighing the risk of infection. In the absence of robust COVID-19 protections, which must necessarily include a recommendation for twice-yearly vaccination, we will be unable to do so. To protect my family's health, the CDC must recommend twice annual vaccination for COVID-19 for people of all ages regardless of disability.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
CDC-2024-0072-0001

I am writing to urge the CDC to change their recommendation for COVID vaccines to include two shots a year, with a third available if needed FOR ALL PEOPLE, and with additional shots available for the immunocompromised (including me) and elderly.

It is widely accepted that at least two shots of any vaccine are required for any lasting response. And it has recently come to light that mRNA does not create the desired persistent antibody response that we would normally expect from a priming series. Therefore, the official recommendation should move to twice a year (especially since a summer and winter wave predicted for the foreseeable future), with a third shot available to anyone, should they need it, not just the elderly and immunocompromised.

There are many reasons someone might need additional access:

- When getting mRNA, they might want to update their memory response. There are examples of this type of update even being necessary with influenza as well.

- We’ve learned from flu vaccines, that getting vaccinated every year without exposure can make hospitalization risk higher. We can likely mitigate that with an additional shot to update the memory response.

- If someone switches from the mRNA vaccine to Novavax, they may want to prime with the protein-based options and that can include 3 shots; 2 shots 8 weeks apart and a 3rd no sooner than 6 months later.

- They might have a high-risk event for which they want an additional shot to boost their immunity. mRNA protection is known to only last for 112 days.

- As the pandemic drags on, someone might go a long time between shots, making them want to start over. They should have that freedom.

- ACIP approval means insurance coverage, meaning more people would be able to afford getting as many COVID vaccines as they needed.

COVID is not a seasonal virus. All people need year-round protection, since everyone is at risk of severe illness. These vaccines have excellent safety profiles and with uptake low, they should be available to ANYONE wants them, as long as they are at least two months apart. The more people that receive year-round vaccine protection, the more protected I and other immunocompromised people will be as well.

title: Comment from Fawzi, Safaya
comment: Covid is airborne and impacts the vascular system. comment: We must mitigate the risks against COVID and Long COVID for both the healthiest and the most vulnerable people, and the best way to do that is to offer protections in the form of vaccines. title: Comment from Anonymous

I want to feel safe again to participate in public life, and having universal access to twice yearly vaccinations against the most current circulating COVID-19 strains would help me to move toward this goal. Thank you for your time and attention to this vital aspect of our healthcare system.

title: Comment from Rosales, Elizabeth
comment:
I am writing to provide public comment regarding the current CDC COVID-19 vaccination recommendations. In light of evolving research and real-world outcomes, I believe that it is essential for the CDC to revise its guidelines to recommend two COVID-19 vaccine doses annually for all people, with a third dose available if needed. Additionally, more frequent boosters should be available for individuals who are immunocompromised, as their needs for sustained protection may be greater.

There is ongoing discussion about whether two Novavax doses are necessary when transitioning from mRNA vaccines. This debate is both valid and timely, as it reflects the complexity of immune responses across different vaccine platforms. However, it is widely acknowledged that at least two doses of any vaccine are required for a lasting immune response. This is consistent with the established understanding outlined in the CDC’s General Best Practice Guidelines for Immunization, as described in your publication (https://www.cdc.gov/pinkbook/hcp/table-of-contents/chapter-2-general-best-practice-guidance.html).

Recent findings have also indicated that mRNA vaccines may not generate the persistent antibody response expected from a traditional priming series. This emerging data underscores the need for updated guidance to ensure the most effective and durable immune protection, particularly given the ongoing risk of COVID-19 variants.

Given these factors, I strongly urge the CDC to consider updating its vaccine recommendations to better reflect the need for more frequent dosing to maintain robust immunity. This will not only provide better protection for the general population but also offer critical support to vulnerable groups such as the immunocompromised.

Thank you for considering these comments. I trust the CDC will continue to take into account the latest scientific evidence and public health needs when revising its guidance.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Please recommend Covid-boosters (and greater Novovax availability) more than once a year for the general public, if not just for the immunocropromised. I had Covid 3X and yet, after the Novovax booster this spring, I was able to avoid it, unlike the rest of my family.

title: Comment from Salema, Christie
comment: Please ensure that individuals can access at least 3 Novavax shots. Priming series plus one every six months thereafter. Thank you.

title: Comment from Siegrist, Kathy
comment: Please approve additional priming shots of Novavax so it will be available throughout the season for older adults and the immunocompromised.

title: Comment from Wang, Constance
comment:
Covid is not a seasonal virus, so we need to have vaccines twice a year. In less than a year, I got covid three times: December 2023, June and August 2024. In the fall, I wasn’t aware of the booster shot and didn’t get it, so my December infection was without the booster.

In May 2024, I learned my lesson and tried to get a booster shot at Walgreens, since it was about time my immunity wore off from the previous infection and I was about to travel. I was able to book an appointment at Walgreens but the pharmacist told me the vaccine was "out of season". In June 2024 I tested positive for covid, and ironically went to the same pharmacist to pick up my Paxlovid prescription.

The summer wave of covid had arrived. The San Francisco Bay area was one of the regions hit hardest. Everyone I knew either got infected themselves or knew someone infected (other than myself) - three coworkers, my two roommates, my roommate’s sister, my roommate’s boss, and my mother who unluckily transferred flights through California. My good friend came from abroad to visit San Francisco at this time, having no idea the extent of the pandemic in the Bay area, just to catch covid at the airport, quarantine, and head home. My mother was making progress on health issues, especially weight loss, but was majorly set back by long covid. It took away her ability to exercise and worsened her sleep apnea. Her cough hasn’t recovered in these last four months. And just when I thought I was in the clear, having been unlucky enough to get the virus at the beginning of the peak, the summer wave continued to spiral out of control. I got covid again in August.

Despite the wastewater levels of covid in the Bay Area being the highest ever in summer of 2024, CDC messaging caused the media and public to think covid was a “seasonal virus” like the flu. Take one look at the wastewater graphs and we can see how absurd this is. We know the vaccine is only effective for three months, so why do we plan to let the virus rampage on when vaccine protection wears off in the summer?

I know making the vaccine available twice a year will cost more, but so will long covid. As time passes we are only seeing more and more studies identifying the long-term impact of covid: fatigue, respiratory/heart problems, difficulty breathing, chest pain, coughing, brain fog, digestive issues and more.

After these bouts of covid, I experienced new allergies and chest pain after exercise. I was prescribed asthma inhalers, which also cost money - more than an extra vaccine dose. And I wouldn’t even be classified as someone with long covid. Whatever we can do to avoid being reinfected is worth it.
