title: Comment from K, Christopher
comment:
Hello, my name is Christopher. I am a life-long Hoosier who cares about the health and the liberty of the people in my community.

I would like to emphasize the three most important aspects to keep regarding the vaccine program moving forward:
1 - Universal Availability: every person has the ability to contract and spread COVID-19. Therefore, the vaccines should continue to be available for everyone 6 months and older.

2 - Additional doses for the immunocompromised: individuals who have a compromised immune system should continue to be offered additional doses of the vaccine every two months. Taking these additional doses allows immunocompromised people to have more similar immune protection to non-immunocompromised individuals.

3 - Additional doses for people age 65+: similar to the previous point, the additional dose recommendations for people age 65 or older should remain.

There are also 3 things that we should change moving forward:

1 - The new covid vaccine frequency should be changed to every 3-4 months. The active immunity we get from a vaccine on average lasts only 3-4 months. We are certain and have undeniable proof that it is safe to receive vaccines at this higher frequency. Additional vaccine doses should be available at the same intervals at which our active immunity wanes: 3-4 months.

2 - MRNA technology advances should be taken advantage of and an updated covid vaccine should be made available on a schedule of every 6 months rather than the current annual schedule. The virus mutates rapidly, and the closer the vaccine being given to the public is to the current strain of the virus in circulation, the more effective that vaccine will be. There is no reason that the 12 month schedule for the current annual release cannot be condensed to a 6 month schedule. Maintaining the current 12 month schedule in the future will waste funding and resources manufacturing vaccines that are guaranteed to be outpaced by viral mutation.

3 - Lastly, those who are pregnant in their 3rd trimester should be offered an additional vaccine dose. Babies 6 months old and younger are unable to be vaccinated and therefore without intervention they are fully unprotected from covid19 for the first 6 months of their lives, a period of time in which all babies are highly vulnerable. If those who are pregnant are offered a vaccine dose in their 3rd trimester, the fetus is able to get the benefit of some immunity before birth. This is incredibly important as covid-19 has increased child mortality.

Keeping the universal availability of the vaccine, keeping 2 month boosters available for immunocompromised people, and keeping the additional vaccine dose recommendation for those 65+ are vital aspects to continue in this program. There is even more benefit (financially and for public health) if we increase the advised vaccine frequency to every 3-4 months, we release new MRNA vaccines on a 6-month basis, and we allow for additional vaccine doses to those pregnant in their third trimester.

Your time and consideration is appreciated.

comment:
I am submitting a comment related to Docket ID CDC-2024-0072 and the upcoming ACIP meeting on October 23-24, 2024. I believe that CDC should change its recommendations to two covid-19 vaccines a year with a third covid-19 vaccine available if needed – for all individuals and not limited to just those who are immunocompromised. For individuals who are immunocompromised, they should be able to get additional covid-19 vaccines as needed and recommended by their medical providers.

I believe that the current guidelines are confusing – I have had discussions with people and we all come to different conclusions as to who is eligible and who needs additional covid-19 vaccinations. There is also confusion as to when the different covid-19 vaccines’ effectiveness wanes. One person I know is waiting until closer to when they might visit family; however, they are getting the Novavax vaccine and it does not have the same waning issue in a short period of time as the mRNA covid-19 vaccine does. They are overdue to get a booster but are waiting.

Recommending two covid -19 vaccines a year with a third one (or more) if needed, would help eliminate this confusion. This could also help get insurance coverage for the costs of the covid-19 vaccines so individuals do not need to pay out of pocket – many cannot afford to do so.

I see the covid-19 prevalence go up and down throughout the year. If people are waiting until the flu season to update covid-19 their vaccination boosters, there is a large part of the year during higher covid-19 prevalence during which people are not as protected from serious illness.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Covid vaccinations twice a year must be provided to all ages. There is growing proof that the vaccinations wane over time. I have Crohn's disease, and while it can be considered an autoimmune disease, it doesn't put me in an immune-compromised category. I want to have good, consistent protection all year from the Covid vaccine and I think the best way to do this is through twice yearly vaccinations. This not only provides added protection for myself but also the people around me.

Vaccines need to be updated regularly to address new variants. This summer was one of the longest and highest waves we've seen due to waning vaccine efficacy and ever changing variants. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long Covid, and other inflammatory issues seen in children. As someone already living with a chronic illness, I need to do everything I can to avoid an additional inflammatory issues.

The CDC's recommendation of Covid vaccination twice a year for all ages helps influence healthcare providers and ensures coverage by health insurance. The CDC must ensure affordable access to updated vaccines regardless of financial constraints. Since the Bridge program ended many uninsured are left without Covid vaccine access and all the benefits it can provide. I urge you to reinstate free Covid vaccine access.

title: Comment from Drescher, Laura
comment:
I’m writing today not only as a member of the public, but as someone who deeply cares about the health and safety of our loved ones, our neighbors, and the most vulnerable in our communities. We’ve all seen the devastating toll that COVID-19 has taken on families and friends, and even now, as the virus continues to unfortunately evolve, the need for protection has never been greater.

The decision to get vaccinated is a choice that protects more than just ourselves — it safeguards everyone around us. This is why I urge the CDC to take a stronger stance: COVID-19 vaccinations must be recommended at bare minimum twice a year, for EVERYONE, regardless of age or health status. Too many people are still being left behind, confused by restrictive guidelines or limited access. We cannot afford to put up barriers that prevent those most at risk from getting the protection they so desperately need.

For families with elderly parents, for those caring for loved ones with compromised immune systems, for the child who could still develop serious complications like MIS-C — these are the people we must keep in our hearts as we make these decisions. Every family should have the peace of mind that comes with knowing their loved ones can receive a COVID-19 vaccine whenever they need it. Every 6 months must be the standard, because we know that vaccine efficacy wanes after just a few months [1-4], and we can’t afford to wait for another surge to act.

But this isn’t just about protection from death. It’s about ensuring that the small moments — family gatherings, birthday parties, even a simple hug — can happen with less fear of acquiring severe illness and long-term disability. Recent research has shown that staying up to date on COVID-19 vaccinations also reduces the risk of Long COVID [5], a condition that has already capsized the lives of millions of people, leaving some unable to work or care for their families.

For many, access to these vaccines remains a challenge. When the CDC’s Bridge Access Program ended in August 2024 [7], it left uninsured and underinsured adults without a clear path to receive these critical vaccines. Imagine the parents, the caregivers, the individuals who want to protect themselves and their loved ones, but can’t because they don’t have access to affordable vaccines. These are the people we need to think about when we make decisions that impact public health. No one should have to choose between protecting their health and paying their bills.

The CDC’s voice carries immense weight. Your clear recommendation for biannual COVID-19 vaccinations would not only guide healthcare providers but also ensure that health insurance covers these critical boosters, making it possible for more people to get vaccinated. We must also advocate for a return of programs like Bridge, so that cost never becomes a barrier to someone getting the protection they deserve.

Please, as you consider the future of COVID-19 vaccination guidance, think about the lives that could be saved, the families that could be spared from grief, and the children who could be shielded from suffering. We are all in this together, and protecting our communities means protecting each other.

Thank you for your dedication and for considering this urgent call to action. Let’s ensure that we are ready to meet the challenges ahead with compassion, equity, and a commitment to keeping all of us safe.

title: Comment from Klein, Amanda
comment:
My household of four struggles to avoid getting infected with COVID. I'm chronically ill and disabled and can't afford to get sick, one of my housemates has an autoimmune disorder, and all of us (regardless of disability status) are too aware that healthy people can become disabled by getting COVID, particularly if they have multiple infections. Many of our friends and family don't regularly mask in public any more and rely on vaccination for protection, but aren't necessarily staying up to date on vaccination. I also know lower-income people who are less likely to get vaccinated now because of the cost. Meanwhile, the data show that COVID vaccine only reduces transmission for a short time, and that even protection from serious complications starts to wane after a few months. One of my family members was infected with COVID for the first time 11 weeks after their updated vaccine last fall and doesn't fall into one of the clear higher risk groups. Six months seems like the minimum frequency that should be available for everyone.

I'm calling on the CDC to recommend COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages, which will increase public awareness that vaccines provide much stronger protection when they are updated. The CDC recommending this would also hopefully increase coverage of vaccines by health insurance and Medicaid: vaccines should be free for everyone, especially those with less access to healthcare. Please help keep our communities safe by communicating clearly about the tools we have and what makes them effective parts of a disease prevention strategy.

title: Comment from Benanav, Amanda
comment:

Additionally, there is no *knowing* that you are not "high risk" - just because you haven't been diagnosed yet doesn't mean you don't have some kind of underlying condition. Additionally, if you have high risk people in your life, it's not enough that only they be vaccinated - every person they come into contact with should also be vaccinated. Covid is still a public health crisis for all.

That's why I'm calling on the CDC make the spring vaccine available for everyone, not just "high risk" groups. I want to see at least 2x vaccines per year for all, updated to account for the latest variants.

Also - the cost for vaccines without insurance is prohibitively expensive. I have a good friend who is a US citizen but lives in Europe for work - when she returned to visit her newborn nephew she went to get the latest vaccine to keep him safe, and it cost her over $200 as she no longer has US health insurance. This is ridiculous.

title: Comment from Rilling, M
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. It also discourages people from getting vaccines before high-risk activities or when convenient for them. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

As a schoolteacher, I’ve witnessed the fall vaccine come far too late to protect children and staff in the first weeks of school.

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
ACIP should recommend the authorization of the use for H5N1 vaccines for farmworkers and others facing occupational risk of H5N1 exposure and infection. While the CDC is recommending seasonal flu vaccines for farmworkers to reduce the risk of human flu viruses mixing with H5N1, the seasonal vaccine does not provide protection against H5N1. As we head into fall and winter flu season, if H5N1 swaps genes with a human flu virus it raises the risk of a more transmissible and virulent strain that could spread as easily among people as seasonal flu viruses do. The US should be proactively taking action to offer the vaccine for those facing the highest risk, and precedent already exists in the case of Finland, which has already moved to vaccinate farmworkers at risk to prevent the risk of severe illness and death with a virus that has historically had over a 50% fatality rate in humans.

In the past year or two, the virus has quickly adapted to spread among a growing range of mammals and is now widespread in dairy cattle in the United States and most recently has found a foothold in dairy farms in California, the largest dairy producer in the nation. With the growing number of dairy farms with H5N1 cases and little sign of this outbreak being controlled, farmworkers are at increased risk and this opens up the possibility of the likelihood of a strain that is better at infecting and spreading between humans. There has been little testing and a lack of transparency in data and information around H5N1 from both the USDA and CDC and we should not wait to take action until we see even more clusters of infection. Vaccines should be authorized and made available to dairy and poultry farmers, those working in slaughterhouses, and others facing occupational risk of exposure. In the words of Rick Bright, former Deputy Assistant Secretary for Preparedness and Response and the Director of the Biomedical Advanced Research and Development Authority (BARDA),“There’s no such thing as just a little conjunctivitis or just a little respiratory (issue) when you’re dealing with a deadly virus. The virus has already demonstrated the ability to mutate very easily, very quickly, and it can cause severe illness and death. So let’s stop it in its tracks, not wait and see and let it get worse.”

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
The CDC must support equitable and affordable access to at least twice a year COVID vaccination for people of all ages, regardless of health insurance status. The CDC should also authorize the use of vaccines for H5N1 for people exposed to increased risk of H5N1 due to occupational risk to reduce the likelihood of severe disease and death from the virus. For H5N1, these groups would include people working in dairy, slaughterhouses, or poultry farms, local communities affected by H5N1 outbreaks, and others working closely with animals or the virus. The H5N1 vaccine should be made freely available for those at high-risk, especially as we enter the seasonal flu season, increasing the possibility that the H5N1 virus may reassort with a human seasonal flu into a more transmissible and dangerous virus.

Additionally, with the COVID-19 Bridge Program ending earlier this year, those who are uninsured no longer have access to COVID Vaccines at no cost, exacerbating vaccine inequities. As COVID-19 continues to evolve, it is essential that communities have access to the most up-to-date vaccines to prevent the likelihood of hospitalization, severe disease, and death. Vaccines should be affordable and accessible to all and we call on you to take action to ensure that those who are uninsured or underinsured have access to updated COVID Vaccines.

title: Comment from Hollingsworth, Sidney
comment:
Dear CDC Leadership,

As a disabled community member, I strongly recommend expanding COVID-19 vaccine access to all individuals, regardless of age or health status, with biannual vaccinations (including spring availability) and more frequent updates to vaccine formulas based on the latest variants. Evidence suggests that immunity from vaccination wanes significantly over time, necessitating regular booster doses to maintain protection. A systematic review highlights this decline, emphasizing the need for timely boosters to reduce risks of severe disease and transmission (Menegale et al., 2023). Furthermore, new research indicates that SARS-CoV-2-specific plasma cells may not establish durable long-term immunity in the bone marrow following mRNA vaccination, reinforcing the need for periodic updates (Nguyen et al., 2024).

Additionally, emerging data underscores the persistent threat of Long COVID, affecting approximately 7% of U.S. adults (Fang et al., 2024). With the continuous circulation of new variants, updated vaccine formulations tailored to current strains, such as those discussed in the recent FDA VRBPAC meeting (Link-Gelles, 2024), are critical to improving population health outcomes.

By adopting biannual vaccinations and regularly updated formulations, the CDC can enhance immunity across the population, mitigate long-term complications, and ensure a proactive response to evolving viral threats.

Sincerely,
Sidney Hollingsworth
Austin, Texas

References

title: Comment from Brown, Julie
comment: I don’t tend to comment on items such as these, but this is a very important issue to me. Going towards year 5 of the pandemic I think it’s obvious the prior strategy of restricting vaccination is not working. Those who are immune compromised, in particular, need to be able to access vaccines more frequently. For varients such as JN1 that have deviated so far from the prior strains, 2 shots should be allowed (8 weeks apart) in order to maximize the immune response. Further to this, anyone (not just those who are immune compromized by diagnosis) should be able to access a second shot in order to maximize their response. In addition to this we should be able to access shots every 6 months, not simply once a year. Covid is not and never will be similar to flu. It doesn’t have a season, it simply has new and continuous mutations. Freeing up access to vaccinations will allow the public to maximize their immunity thereby reducing health care costs and reducing impacts on the economy.

title: Comment from Flores, Jasmine
comment: People need covid vaccines to be accessible. the CDC needs to update its recommendations to at least 2 shots a year.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
I urge the CDC to recommend COVID vaccination at least twice per year (at six month intervals) for people of all ages as opposed to restricting access to those with specific health conditions. When pharmacists are instructed to discern who is eligible, inevitably some who are eligible are denied due to misunderstanding and misinterpreting confusing guidelines. Blanket guidance that everyone can receive a COVID vaccine twice per year is simple, easy to understand guidance that will further enable the most vulnerable to receive needed protection. People of all ages should have the opportunity to receive a second 2024-2025 updated COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

Studies have demonstrated that protection against infection from the COVID vaccines wanes in the months following vaccination, and does not last anywhere close to an entire year [1-3]. No matter how much we hope that COVID will one day behave like influenza with only a single Winter wave of infections per year, that is not yet the case as the CDC acknowledged on July 3, 2024 [4]. We are currently seeing two major surges each year as we head towards the sixth overall year of the COVID pandemic. With this pattern established, it does not make sense to base the vaccination schedule on what we hope may happen in the future that has not happened yet. Given the especially large Summer wave of infections this year, it seems obvious that access to vaccinations every six months has the potential to greatly reduce the twice-yearly surges of infections.

It has been absurd to live through the Summer wave of infections with no access to updated vaccines (or any vaccine, unless you are over 65 or immunocompromised), knowing that vaccine protection against infection from last Fall's shot has waned, only for the Fall messaging from the CDC to say that it is time for people to get their yearly COVID vaccine in preparation for the upcoming Winter wave of infections. This messaging began while many areas of the country were still surging from the Summer wave. Waning in efficacy against infection is present regardless of age or immunocompromised status [5]. This further points to the need for vaccine access every six months for the entire population, not just the elderly and immunocompromised. If the population as a whole are allowed to receive a vaccine every six months, those vulnerable groups will have even better population protection along with those who are unable to be vaccinated.

Studies also show that recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID [6] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [7]. Due to vaccines' ability to protect from severe acute infection and death, it has become apparent that our greatest threat from COVID is Long COVID from organ, vascular, and neurological damage. Allowing vaccination every six months will help to reduce the increasing healthcare and economic burden associated with a population increasingly compromised by Long COVID. Studies have shown that the risk of acquiring Long COVID increases with each new reinfection, potentially leading to a ~30% chance of acquiring Long COVID after three infections. As the current vaccination schedule does not prevent infection with COVID, on average people are experiencing ~1 infection per year. This has introduced an unacceptable level of new disability in people of all ages. Allowing vaccination every six months has the potential to halt the development of Long COVID from new infections.

comment: Based on the CDC notes regarding vaccine efficacy best practices for other vaccines (https://www.cdc.gov/pinkbook/hcp/table-of-contents/chapter-2-general-best-practice-guidance.html), I’m requesting that the CDC changes their minimum guidelines to two shots a year, with a third available if needed, and additional shots available for the immunocompromised, to match well-established practices for other diseases.

title: Comment from Tegene , Selamawit
comment: The CDC needs to change their recommendation to include two shots a year, with a third available if needed FOR ALL PEOPLE, with additional shots available for the immunocompromised.

title: Comment from Drake, Alan
comment:
Clinical and Public Health observations show that immunity to SARS-COV-2, either by infection or vaccination, fades within months.

This paper has an interesting hypothesis, SARS-COV-2 fails to convert naive B cells into Long Lived Plasma cells because the spikes are too far apart.
https://www.nature.com/articles/s41591-024-03278-y

The result is the observed short lived immunity.

Regardless of the modality, aspiration of bone marrow shows an absence or very low count of LLPC antibodies for SARS-COV-2 vs much higher levels of LLPC for tetanus and influenza.

The practical impact is that annual immunization is clearly and absolutely inadequate. Immunity from Year 1 immunization will simply not last till Year 2.
immunization. This is even more true for older patients.

In addition, waves of infection of SARS-COV-2 are not closely related to weather, but are more strongly related to virus mutation. Although a more infectious mutation coupled with winter weather has more impact than a comparable mutation in the summer. So weather does have some impact on infection rates.

We simply do not know the impacts of repeated Covid infections on long term health. The BioBank study - before and after infection brain MRIs vs matched controls for 401 subjects - clearly showed significant brain mass loss due to Covid infection. This is supported by the commonly reported "brain fog" sequelae. What are the impacts on, for example, early dementia, from repeated Covid infections ?

https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-022-04569-5#:~:text=The%20longitudinal%20nature%20of%20the,the%20direct%20or%20indirect%20contribution

We do not know, but the range of long term sequelae include some very frightening ones.

Admittedly, the uptake of multiple boosters each year will be relatively low. But the potential benefits of those that avail themselves of these boosters will be high. Some younger individuals with multiple cases of Long Covid have had their doctors recommend vaccination every 6 months.

Note: The commentator has never had symptomatic Covid, but he has had 12 doses of vaccine from all 4 FDA approved vaccines. One Jansen, three Novavax, five Moderna, three Pfizer/BioNTech. At age 71 now, he attempts to get a Covid vaccine dose every 4.5 months to keep his antibodies elevated with some adjustment to the currently circulating strains.

He also warned his brothers of the new SARS virus in China that seemed almost certain to spread worldwide on January 24, 2020. He suggested that they take preparations such as buying N95 masks and cancel trips then.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: I'm writing to request that you allow anyone 6 months or older to access a 2nd COVID-19 vaccine each year, if they choose. As a healthcare researcher whose work includes studies of vaccine immunogenicity and effectiveness, I know that antibodies wane and clinical protection dwindles long before a year. As the daughter of a patient in active cancer treatment and of an organ transplant recipient, I also know that there are many immune compromised people who may not be optimally protected by their own vaccines, may be unable to access prophylaxis, ineligible or unable to access treatment, and are vulnerable to infection from household members and other close contacts. I would like the option to protect my family by boosting myself and my children twice a year - an option we won't be afforded by vaccine provider or insurance gatekeepers without your endorsement that vaccination every 6 months is safe, effective, and warranted for those who choose it to protect those they love. Please center the most vulnerable in your decision-making and arm us with the best protection we can provide those who are most vulnerable, like my parents, and who have largely been left to fend for themselves, unprotected.

title: Comment from Geraghty, Aileen
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: The CDC must recommend that ALL people, regardless of age or health status, get COVID vaccines twice a year.  You must also support more frequent updates to the vaccines to be adjusted for any recent variants.  As you know, vaccines work best when most of the population is vaccinated on a regular, frequent schedule.  Recommending twice a year vaccines for only a small portion of the population will leave everyone at greater risk.  It's been shown that efficacy of the COVID vaccines wane after about 6 months so setting a 6 month schedule is the only evidence based decision that can be made.  Please don't let the people down!  The people paid for the creation of these vaccines, we deserve equitable and just access to them.  Thank you.

title: Comment from Blalack, Russell
comment: All ages should be eligible for the latest COVID vaccine because they are often in contact with vulnerable family members - parents, grandparents, etc - whom they otherwise might expose to the virus unwittingly.

title: Comment from Squires, Scott
comment:
Covid is still here, still killing, still disabling so we need:
1. Updated vaccines every 6 months. Studies show that the current vaccines wane most of their protection in only 3 or 4 months. With new variants coming quickly it's important to have updated vaccines which are a closer match than several months out of date.
2. Allow immunocompromised people to get a 2nd COVID vaccine 2 months after the first.
The immunocompromised need more protection.
3. Broaden language to allow ANYONE to get a 2nd dose after 2 months.
Provide people the choice as they and their health require. It's also important to know some vaccines
Now and possibly in the future, 2 doses are required to provide the most protection.

title: Comment from Helzer, Anna
comment: As a public school teacher, I see students every day who don’t have financial resources to access healthcare. The out-of-pocket costs limit their families’ abilities to stay healthy, and they often choose to do the minimum recommendations for vaccines and appointments. Increasing the frequency of recommended and available vaccines to them would help them stay safe and protect their growing brains to focus on their education rather than the challenges of constant exposure to sickness. COVID vaccines wear off within a few months and I see repeatedly the effects of sickness on the rise among students and staff in February and March. Updated COVID vaccines must be available at least twice a year for everyone in all age groups, not just those officially marked as high risk. All humans are at risk of catching and suffering from COVID and Long Covid. By increasing the recommendation, you will both raise awareness of the need for protection and the value of routine vaccinations, and also protect the public by increasing the protection for everyone in society. Additionally, I urge you to push for free COVID vaccine access so that my students and their families can all have equitable access to safety and healthcare no matter their insurance status. Our country’s future depends on students like mine, and I work every day to make sure that they have the resources they need to learn and grow. Please support me in my work by protecting me, my coworkers, and students everywhere.

title: Comment from Reid, Carol
comment: The CDC needs to change their recommendation to include two shots a year, with a third available if needed FOR ALL PEOPLE, with additional shots available for the immunocompromised.

comment: People need access to vaccines more frequently. We can only keep them safe if we all have access to COVID-19 vaccines twice per year and if the vaccines are updated more frequently than once per year.

title: Comment from Benanav, Ethan
comment:
Please release updated COVID vaccines twice a year, available to everyone!
Keeping it to only "high risk" individuals means for many in need it wouldn't be an option. Two years ago my wife had a tumor removed from her spinal cord. She's officially a paraplegic. Thanks.

title: Comment from Carr, Bryan
comment: I am an educator in a K-8 school; students all around me are routinely sick, and colleagues frequently fall ill. This week I have worked alongside two different colleagues who were sick, one with a loud, rough and "productive" cough, one in her second week of extreme fatigue following upon several days of being home.  I have two family members who are immunocompromised and have chronic health conditions which would be severely aggravated by catching COVID-19. The windows of time before vaccines' effectiveness has significantly waned are moments of respite in my household, when we can relax a little and not worry quite as much that one of us -- probably me -- will give another an infection. I myself also have an ongoing health concern (McArdle's Syndrome) which slowly takes its toll on my quality of life; I am willing to take vaccines as often as I can in order to lower the risk of complicating my prognosis.

title: Comment from Cassidy, Darcy
comment: As a birth worker and nurse, I know how essential preventative health measures like vaccines are. Providing COVID vaccines for everyone will contribute to a more equitable and healthy society.

title: Comment from Croston, J.
comment:
I believe COVID vaccination twice a year should be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. I live with my husband, who is over 65, my mother-in-law, who is over 90, and our son, who attends a public school. Since my husband has suffered from Long COVID for almost three years, with no appreciable improvement, I fear that an additional SARS-CoV-2 infection would likely worsen his extreme fatigue and some additional medical problems, for which no treatments have been offered by his physicians. We continue to wear N95 or KN95 masks consistently when in public, even our teenager, who is one of very few people in the large school who does so. Each of us being able to have a COVID vaccine every six months rather than once a year would be a valuable additional layer of precaution. Since the efficacy of the vaccines wanes after 1–3 months, the extra protection of second vaccine every year for each one of our family members, regardless of age or risk status, would give all of us more protection, which in turn protects the most vulnerable of us—my husband, due to Long COVID and age, and his mother, due to age and her existing chronic illnesses.

I would hope that, in addition, vaccinations be made completely free, in order to ensure that more people actually have the vaccine, thus reducing spread.

title: Comment from McCollum, Jessica
comment: I am writing to request that theCDC change their Covid-19 vaccination recommendation to include two shots a year, with a third available if needed FOR ALL PEOPLE, with additional shots available for the immunocompromised. People should have the choice, information, and right to protect themselves

title: Comment from Garber, Audrey
comment: We need covid vaccines available twice a year! I have Long Covid and once a year isn’t close to good enough to protect me from further complications.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Covid-19 vaccines are an important layer that work best when they are targeted to current variants and given to anyone every six months, based on what we have learned about how protection wanes. Unfortunately, without other layers to mitigate spread, Covid-19 continues to mutate faster than the current vaccine schedule and defy seasonal patterns. Many immunocompromised people don’t know they are immunocompromised. Either way, everyone would benefit from all year protection from more frequent vaccines targeted to current variants. My friends are struggling to pay for vaccines due to the privatization of this vaccine and barriers to access do nothing to improve uptake if the CDC is both set on overrelying on vaccines and underfunding Covid-19 vaccine education campaigns. The fact that I’m more at risk without vaccine protection for part of the year is dangerous. We deserve better.

title: Comment from Watanabe, T.
comment: Seeing as the public health system and the government have abandoned mitigation (both in practice and messaging) for Covid, the situation is a free-for-all.  People are not educated on mitigation methods, how to test, when to test, and they just don't test.  So Covid spreads throughout the community, with no regard to the safety of infants, elders, immunocompromised and disabled people.  It rises to ridiculous levels (I'm looking at you, August 2024) and falls.  No real official messaging on when and how to protect ourselves is offered.  Not mitigating Covid is playing Russian Roulette:  allowing the virus free reign encourages virus mutations and recombination.  You never know if your next case of Covid will be mild or severe or leave you with lingering disablement.  The research is out there---it is not good to get multiple infections of Covid.  Time will tell what long term damage is being done to young people, people of working age, and seniors.  Until the time that Covid goes away (doubtful with the current "management" of Covid) or we get an effective nasal vaccine that prevents the virus from causing an infection, we need access for all to vaccines.  Since vaccine efficacy wanes in mere months, twice a year vaccines should be available to those who want to/need to protect themselves.  We have been having two spikes of Covid a year.  We need to be able to protect ourselves from both spikes.  Preventing one person from getting Covid protects others from getting Covid.  Furthermore, it is cost efficient to have insurance cover the vaccine for everyone, and free vaccine should be available for the uninsured.  I strongly urge you to approve that everyone should be able to get a Covid vaccine twice a year if they feel they need it. Ideally, everyone should be vaccinated, and then we would have much less Covid around and could manage it better.

title: Comment from Urena, Anna
comment:
Hello! I’m writing to ask that the CDC change their recommendation to include two shots a year, with a third available if needed for everyone, with additional shots available for the immunocompromised.

I am trying my hardest to stay healthy and keep my family and community safe. Additional shots would help us stay healthy and be a great addition to our other precautions.

title: Comment from PPEforCA
comment:
ACIP should recommend the authorization of the use for H5N1 vaccines for farmworkers and others facing occupational risk of H5N1 exposure and infection. While the CDC is recommending seasonal flu vaccines for farmworkers to reduce the risk of human flu viruses mixing with H5N1, the seasonal vaccine does not provide protection against H5N1. As we head into fall and winter flu season, if H5N1 swaps genes with a human flu virus it raises the risk of a more transmissible and virulent strain that could spread as easily among people as seasonal flu viruses do. The US should be proactively taking action to offer the vaccine for those facing the highest risk, and precedent already exists in the case of Finland, which has already moved to vaccinate farmworkers at risk to prevent the risk of severe illness and death with a virus that has historically had over a 50% fatality rate in humans.

In the past year or two, the virus has quickly adapted to spread among a growing range of mammals and is now widespread in dairy cattle in the United States and most recently has found a foothold in dairy farms in California, the largest dairy producer in the nation. With the growing number of dairy farms with H5N1 cases and little sign of this outbreak being controlled, farmworkers are at increased risk and this opens up the possibility of the likelihood of a strain that is better at infecting and spreading between humans. There has been little testing and a lack of transparency in data and information around H5N1 from both the USDA and CDC and we should not wait to take action until we see even more clusters of infection. Vaccines should be authorized and made available to dairy and poultry farmers, those working in slaughterhouses, and others facing occupational risk of exposure. In the words of Rick Bright, former Deputy Assistant Secretary for Preparedness and Response and the Director of the Biomedical Advanced Research and Development Authority (BARDA),“There’s no such thing as just a little conjunctivitis or just a little respiratory (issue) when you’re dealing with a deadly virus. The virus has already demonstrated the ability to mutate very easily, very quickly, and it can cause severe illness and death. So let’s stop it in its tracks, not wait and see and let it get worse.”

title: Comment from PPEforCA
comment:
The CDC must support equitable and affordable access to at least twice a year COVID vaccination for people of all ages, regardless of health insurance status. The CDC should also authorize the use of vaccines for H5N1 for people exposed to increased risk of H5N1 due to occupational risk to reduce the likelihood of severe disease and death from the virus. For H5N1, these groups would include people working in dairy, slaughterhouses, or poultry farms, local communities affected by H5N1 outbreaks, and others working closely with animals or the virus. The H5N1 vaccine should be made freely available for those at high-risk, especially as we enter the seasonal flu season, increasing the possibility that the H5N1 virus may reassort with a human seasonal flu into a more transmissible and dangerous virus.

Additionally, with the COVID-19 Bridge Program ending earlier this year, those who are uninsured no longer have access to COVID Vaccines at no cost, exacerbating vaccine inequities. As COVID-19 continues to evolve, it is essential that communities have access to the most up-to-date vaccines to prevent the likelihood of hospitalization, severe disease, and death. Vaccines should be affordable and accessible to all and we call on you to take action to ensure that those who are uninsured or underinsured have access to updated COVID Vaccines.

title: Comment from KIZER, BEN
comment:
COVID vaccination at least twice a year (at least every six months) must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025. According to the CDC: “COVID-19 activity tends to fluctuate with the seasons, meaning it has some seasonal patterns. Data from four years of COVID-19 cases, hospitalizations, and deaths show that COVID-19 has winter peaks (most recently in late December 2023 and early January 2024), but also summer peaks (most recently in July and August of 2023). There is no distinct COVID-19 season like there is for influenza (flu) and respiratory syncytial virus (RSV)” [8].

As someone who did not meet the age or health requirements for an additional dose of the vaccine in Spring 2024, I was denied that vaccine dose by the administrative team at my PCP’s clinic solely because I was not eligible according to CDC recommendations. I had to wait until Fall 2024 for another vaccine — a full year after my previous vaccine. During the waiting period until that next vaccine from April-September 2024, Covid cases increased significantly in one of the worse surges since the start of the pandemic, but I was unable to protect myself with another vaccine dose even with the waning efficacy of my Fall 2023 dose. This was solely due to the CDC’s inadequate recommendations.

The vaccine schedule should address waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3] as well as emergence of new SARS-CoV-2 strains by recommending updated vaccination for all ages, at least every six months. Waning efficacy is seen with all COVID vaccine types, and recent research into the biological mechanisms of waning [4] supports that this effect occurs regardless of age or immunocompromised status. Recent vaccination is associated with a lower risk of developing Long COVID following a COVID infection [5] as well as a lower risk of Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in children (MIS-C) [6].

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination (within the last six months) to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from MaskBloc916
comment:
The CDC must support equitable and affordable access to at least twice a year COVID vaccination for people of all ages, regardless of health insurance status. The CDC should also authorize the use of vaccines for H5N1 for people exposed to increased risk of H5N1 due to occupational risk to reduce the likelihood of severe disease and death from the virus. For H5N1, these groups would include people working in dairy, slaughterhouses, or poultry farms, local communities affected by H5N1 outbreaks, and others working closely with animals or the virus. title: Comment from MaskBloc916
comment:
ACIP should recommend the authorization of the use for H5N1 vaccines for farmworkers and others facing occupational risk of H5N1 exposure and infection. While the CDC is recommending seasonal flu vaccines for farmworkers to reduce the risk of human flu viruses mixing with H5N1, the seasonal vaccine does not provide protection against H5N1. As we head into fall and winter flu season, if H5N1 swaps genes with a human flu virus it raises the risk of a more transmissible and virulent strain that could spread as easily among people as seasonal flu viruses do. The US should be proactively taking action to offer the vaccine for those facing the highest risk, and precedent already exists in the case of Finland, which has already moved to vaccinate farmworkers at risk to prevent the risk of severe illness and death with a virus that has historically had over a 50% fatality rate in humans.

In the past year or two, the virus has quickly adapted to spread among a growing range of mammals and is now widespread in dairy cattle in the United States and most recently has found a foothold in dairy farms in California, the largest dairy producer in the nation. With the growing number of dairy farms with H5N1 cases and little sign of this outbreak being controlled, farmworkers are at increased risk and this opens up the possibility of the likelihood of a strain that is better at infecting and spreading between humans. There has been little testing and a lack of transparency in data and information around H5N1 from both the USDA and CDC and we should not wait to take action until we see even more clusters of infection. Vaccines should be authorized and made available to dairy and poultry farmers, those working in slaughterhouses, and others facing occupational risk of exposure. In the words of Rick Bright, former Deputy Assistant Secretary for Preparedness and Response and the Director of the Biomedical Advanced Research and Development Authority (BARDA),“There’s no such thing as just a little conjunctivitis or just a little respiratory (issue) when you’re dealing with a deadly virus. The virus has already demonstrated the ability to mutate very easily, very quickly, and it can cause severe illness and death. So let’s stop it in its tracks, not wait and see and let it get worse.”

title: Comment from B, Sarah
comment: Please allow for all people ages 6months plus to be vaccinated twice a year. Since it is optional those of us who want/need additional protection can have it and others can abstain. We know that vaccine efficacy wanes sometimes after a month or 2 for mRNA vaccines and a few months for novavax. We are seeing a summer/back to school wave becoming a regular thing right as our vaccines almost a year old. It leaves so many of us open to high risk of long COVID and/or hospitalization/more severe outcomes. title: Comment from H, Abbey
comment:
Dear CDC,

I am a Clinical Lab Scientist who spent the last years of my career building a sequencing-based COVID test to analyze tens of thousands of samples in one run. I also spent a year at a student health clinic in 2021, where I also ran a triplicate test for Flu, RSV, and COVID. All the students had been vaccinated for COVID over March and April, but sometime in August, I started to see a wave of breakthrough infections. It was clear the shot was no longer preventing initial infection, so you authorized a third shot in October. Nevertheless we had another wave of cases, peaking in January of 2022. We have, ever since SARS-CoV-2 was disovered, had bimodal peaks of infection every year: one in summer, one in winter. And between peaks, the cases remain terrifyingly high.

COVID vaccination has, for inexplicable reasons, become politicized, and many people are still refusing to get the vaccines. We know from the resistance to the MMR vaccine how few unvaccinated people need to exist in order to put a whole community at risk. And the rates of COVID unvaccination are much higher as a result of misinformation and fearmongering tactics by the right.

Unfortunately, even for those who do get their shots, COVID immunity appears to be "forgotten" over time. I had received my last shot in September of 2022, and knowing how vulnerable I was in the middle of the summer 2023 wave, I was anxiously isolating myself and waiting for the next shot to be authorized. Unfortunately, as careful as I was with my own masking and distancing, my partner went out partying, lied to me about it, and brought COVID home to me. I developed Long COVID, ME/CFS, MCAS, new allergies, and exercise intolerance. I was a healthy endurance cyclist before my infection, and now I can barely walk a city block. I can't work, and I've been out on disability for over a year. I've had to give up my hobbies, my friends, my entire life to this new set of disabilities. I can't help but wonder how things would be different if a 2x annual vaccination would have been available to me.

I am kindly requesting that you make COVID shots available to us 2x a year or even more, so that those of us who want to protect ourselves, can.

Thank you for your time.

title: Comment from Matison, Leora
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2024-0072-0001

Considering scientific evidence shows that the efficiency of COVID-19 vaccines wanes after six months [1-3], all members of the general public should be allowed to access COVID-19 vaccines every six months instead of once per year. Considering most of the country has stopped taking covid precautions, and one-way masking has limitations, those who strongly wish to avoid COVID-19 should be allowed to increase their protection through quicker vaccination after the effectiveness of their vaccines wane. More frequent vaccination is especially important for those that have some of the many medical conditions that increase the risk of COVID-19 or those who live with them, but are not over 65 nor immunocompromised and hence are restricted to only one vaccine per year.

I personally have a brain injury, which means the neurological effects and other disabling affects of Long Covid would potentially be more debilitating for me than for someone without a pre-existing neurological injury. Recent vaccination had been shown to be effective for reducing the risk of Long Covid [4]. However, since I am under 65 and my condition is not on the list of eligible immunocompromising conditions I am only eligible for one vaccine per year, making me have increased vulnerability to COVID-19 infections for six months out of the year after my yearly COVID-19 vaccine wanes.

Additionally, allowing vaccines every six months gives the opportunity to update vaccines to the currently dominant COVID-19 strains every six months instead of once a year, increasing their effectiveness. More frequent vaccine updates are especially useful considering the COVID-19 virus mutates rapidly.

Finally, twice yearly COVID-19 vaccines must remain available and fully covered by public funds and/or insurance for people of all ages. It is important to ensure vaccines aren't made inaccessible or harder to access for certain demographics due to financial constraints, or that financial concerns are allowed to increase vaccine hesitancy. Unfortunately, a program that provided more equitable access to vaccines, the CDC's Bridge Access Program, ended in August 2024. To ensure equitable and affordable access to vaccines, the FDA should advocate for programs such as the CDC’s Bridge Access Program that provide access to vaccines at no cost.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
As research about long-covid continues to develop, showing that each reinfection heightens the risk of long-covid, the CDC must continue to recommend updated covid-vaccines twice a year for everyone, regardless or age or health status. With a disease that has been linked to increase risk of heart attack and stroke, the development of autoimmune diseases, and neurological effects, it would be extremely dangerous to limit access or add new barriers to the vaccination process.

We know that multiple infections does not create immunity, we know that vaccine efficiency wanes over time, and we know that COVID is still spreading and evolving. Covid is still killing and disabling so many people, and having as many ways as possible to protect ourselves and each other is the only way forward. Updated vaccines that target new strains, address waning efficiancy, and are readably accessible to everyone -- regardless of age, health status, or financial status -- is necessary and life-saving. Please advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from Sparrow Bools LLC
comment:
As a small business owner in a rural town where a number of our employees and customers are immunocompromised, I feel very strongly that COVID vaccination at least twice a year must be recommended for people of all ages, regardless of health status. A restrictive approach to eligibility creates undue barriers for vulnerable people and discourages high risk people from getting needed vaccine boosters. People of all ages, including those who are aged 65 and older or immunocompromised, should have the opportunity to receive another COVID vaccine in the spring of 2025.

I do not take lightly that my business creates an environment where people can gather indoors for long periods of time, and can therefore be a place where transmission occurs. Many people who visit my shop adhere to CDC recommendations. While most businesses (including my own) no longer require masks or vaccine status for entry, I take comfort in knowing that everyone who enters my store with an up-to-date vaccination is better protected and less likely to face fatal consequences or develop long covid if they were to contract covid.

For years, the CDC has been reiterating that one of the best ways we can protect ourselves and others from COVID and long-covid is by getting vaccinated. Research shows waning efficacy in the months following vaccination [1-3], and that long risk of long-covid increases with multiple reinfections. With that in mind, it is critical to continue recommending updated vaccines to everyone.

Long-covid is considered a mass disabling event that can leave people immunocompromised. It does not make sense to limit vaccine access to only those with certain high-risk conditions. The more people who get COVID, the more people will become high-risk. We must be proactive in preventing contraction and transmission, and that means ensuring vaccines are updated at least twice a year amid the emergence of new strains, and that everyone can access these vaccines, regardless of age.

The CDC’s clear and unequivocal recommendation of COVID vaccination at least twice a year for all ages will influence recommendations by healthcare providers, and coverage by health insurance. Moreover, it will improve public awareness in people of all ages about the importance of recent vaccination to provide the best protection as part of a multilayered approach to preventing illness.

The CDC must ensure equitable and affordable access to updated vaccines and prevent limited access because of financial constraints or demographics. The CDC’s Bridge vaccine access program ended in August 2024 [7], leaving many uninsured and underinsured adults without COVID vaccine access.

Even as someone who is lucky enough to have insurance, I have already experienced roadblocks this fall, when I visited the only pharmacy within walking distance from my hope to get the vaccine, only to be told that they don’t take my insurance and I would have to drive to the nearest pharmacy that does. How many people with busy schedules or without cars would this barrier keep from getting the vaccine at all? Let alone those who are not insured. We ask you to advocate for free COVID vaccine access for all of us to reduce barriers and hesitation to vaccination.

title: Comment from oliver, g.
comment:
We give lip service to the notion that each of us is a unique individual. Biochemical Individuality (Roger J Williams, 1998) provides convincing evidence on the wide range of our differences. I, 76 y.o.), was recently refused a covid vax when I tried to get one *4* days before the arbitrary 6-month wait from the previous one had expired. There is no reason, other than administrative convenience, for not allowing immunocompromised people to get a second covid vaccine around two months after the previous one since immunity often fades rapidly in such people. Sincerely,

--g. oliver

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Hello,

We ask the CDC to ensure the needs of the immunocompromised population are assessed in a holistic manner rather than a fragmented one. Firstly, there are immunocompromised people across ages and they do not live in a vacuum: they have parents, siblings, spouses, children, roommates, etc. In addition to this, they attend daycare, school, college. They work. They live in nursing homes. They access health care in a regular manner. All of these environments come with significant risks given there are no indoor air quality regulations and the population - including many health care workers - overwhelmingly doesn't consider COVID-19 as an important health concern anymore as a result of poor knowledge. People therefore don't tend to test, isolate, nor mask - even when symptomatic - which directly contributes to making all shared spaces risky in a lasting manner. The high baseline transmission levels on a yearly basis for over four years also contribute to pervasive hardships - with no progress in sight given the lack of political will, the poor public health messaging and recommendations, the disengagement of health care workers and health care settings, and the poor work done by medias. The combination of all these aspects lead to the immunocompromised population - and the vulnerable in general - being lastingly marginalized and maintained in harm's way. Poor policies, limited social support, the stigmatization - and now criminalization - of mask wearing, the inaccessibility to Pemgarda as a result of insurance denial/distance to treatment centers, etc., and the stalled progress disproportionately harm the immunocompromised and their family members - this must stop. The CDC must prioritize strong communications with the general public, health care workers/settings, and politicians, and make recommendations reflecting the state of the knowledge regarding the importance for individuals 6 months and older to receive the updated COVID vaccines twice a year given the epidemiology of disease. It is essential to put emphasis on the fact that the short lived immunity acquired through vaccination is preferable to the one acquired through infection which comes with important risks across ages and health status. With regards to the immunocompromised specifically, receiving the vaccine more than twice a year based on the assessment made by their care team is essential. In sum, the immunocompromised population suffers from neglect and this must change. The CDC has a critical role to play on many levels - from how it communicates about COVID, masking, etc., to the vaccine recommendations. Proper communications and recommendations will lay the foundation needed for necessary progress to happen and send a clear signal to politicians who must act with a sense of urgency and sustained commitment. Thank you for your attention. Regards,

title: Comment from Sweeney, Sydney
comment:
The CDC must support equitable and affordable access to at least twice a year COVID vaccination for people of all ages, regardless of health insurance status. The CDC should also authorize the use of vaccines for H5N1 for people exposed to increased risk of H5N1 due to occupational risk to reduce the likelihood of severe disease and death from the virus. For H5N1, these groups would include people working in dairy, slaughterhouses, or poultry farms, local communities affected by H5N1 outbreaks, and others working closely with animals or the virus. Vaccines should be affordable and accessible to all and we call on you to take action to ensure that those who are uninsured or underinsured have access to updated COVID Vaccines.
